name: Home
title: Home
description: Homepage that loads dynamic categories and featured content, and routes users into shopping, design services, style quiz, and journal sections.

core_features: Load all top-level product categories via getCategories and expose them to navigation and category shortcut components.
Load featured categories, products, design services, design packages, and articles via getHomeFeaturedContent for the homepage content modules.
Provide category-based entry points into the products listing page, passing the selected category identifier in the URL.
Provide quick entry points into design services and design packages sections from featured service/package content.
Provide access to the style quiz via a dedicated functional link panel.
Provide access to journal content through a featured articles strip that links to the main journal/blog page.
Provide constant access to account/profile and cart via the header navigation.
user_workflows:
Browse products by category:
1) User lands on Home; the data loader component calls getCategories and getHomeFeaturedContent.
2) When getCategories resolves, the category shortcut grid and header navigation render a list of categories using the API data.
3) User clicks a category item in the grid or header.
4) The click handler reads the bound category.id and constructs a URL products.html?categoryId={category.id}.
5) Browser navigates to that URL, allowing the products page to show items filtered by the selected category.

Explore featured products and continue to category listing:
1) User lands on Home; the data loader populates featuredProducts and categories.
2) Featured product cards render using the featuredProducts array.
3) For each product card, the component attempts to match the product's categoryName to one of the loaded categories to derive the corresponding categoryId.
4) User clicks a featured product card.
5) If a matching category was found, the click handler navigates to products.html?categoryId={derivedCategoryId}. If no match exists, the handler navigates to products.html without parameters or disables the link, depending on implementation rules.

Access design services and packages:
1) User lands on Home; getHomeFeaturedContent returns lists for featuredDesignServices and featuredDesignPackages.
2) The services/packages panel renders entries based on these lists.
3) User clicks a design services call-to-action element; the handler navigates to design_services.html.
4) User clicks a design packages call-to-action element; the handler navigates to design_packages.html.

Access custom furniture configuration tools:
1) User sees a dedicated link panel for custom furniture access on Home.
2) User clicks the custom furniture entry point.
3) The click handler performs a navigation to custom_furniture.html (no parameters).

Start the style quiz from Home:
1) User sees a style quiz link panel rendered on Home (it does not require API data).
2) User clicks the style quiz button or link.
3) The click handler navigates to style_quiz.html where the quiz flow is handled.

Access journal content from featured articles:
1) User lands on Home; getHomeFeaturedContent returns featuredArticles.
2) The journal preview strip renders cards using the featuredArticles data.
3) User clicks any featured article card or a general 'view all' entry point.
4) The click handler navigates to blog.html (no parameters), letting the blog page handle article selection and filtering.

Access account and cart from header:
1) Header navigation is visible on Home at all times.
2) User clicks the account link in the header.
3) The click handler navigates to account.html?section=profile.
4) User clicks the cart icon.
5) The click handler navigates to cart.html.
interactions: On initial page load, the home data loader component calls getCategories and getHomeFeaturedContent and updates child components when responses arrive.
Clicking a category item in the category shortcut grid navigates to products.html with the selected categoryId URL parameter.
Clicking a category entry in the header navigation also navigates to products.html with the selected categoryId URL parameter.
Clicking a featured product card attempts to navigate to products.html with a derived categoryId parameter based on the product's categoryName and the loaded categories.
Clicking a design services call-to-action element navigates to design_services.html.
Clicking a design packages call-to-action element navigates to design_packages.html.
Clicking the custom furniture entry element navigates to custom_furniture.html.
Clicking the style quiz entry element navigates to style_quiz.html.
Clicking any featured article card or an aggregated 'view all' entry element navigates to blog.html.
Clicking the account link in the header navigates to account.html?section=profile.
Clicking the cart icon in the header navigates to cart.html.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Home page does not consume any incoming URL parameters; it always initializes in the same logical state. On page initialization, a data loader component triggers getCategories and getHomeFeaturedContent with no parameters and stores the results in in-memory state variables (e.g., categories, homeFeaturedContent). These state variables are passed as props to downstream components.

Outbound navigation is handled entirely through URL parameters constructed at click-time:
- Category navigation: when a user clicks a category tile or category link, the component reads the bound category.id from the categories array returned by getCategories and constructs products.html?categoryId={category.id}.
- Featured product navigation: when a user clicks a featured product card, the component looks up the category whose name matches the product's categoryName from featuredProducts. If a match is found, it uses that category.id as derivedCategoryId and constructs products.html?categoryId={derivedCategoryId}. If no match is found, the component either navigates to products.html without parameters or disables the navigation according to implementation rules; no IDs are hardcoded.
- Design services: clicking the relevant elements always navigates to design_services.html without additional parameters.
- Design packages: clicking the relevant elements always navigates to design_packages.html without additional parameters.
- Custom furniture: clicking the relevant element navigates to custom_furniture.html without parameters.
- Style quiz: clicking the quiz entry navigates to style_quiz.html without parameters.
- Journal: clicking featured article elements navigates to blog.html without parameters; specific article selection is handled on the blog or article page, not on Home.
- Account: clicking the account link navigates to account.html?section=profile, where the section parameter is a static value describing which account subsection to open.
- Cart: clicking the cart icon navigates to cart.html without parameters.
No localStorage or other persistent client-side storage is used to manage Home page behavior; all view state is derived from the API responses and in-memory component state, and navigation state is encoded only in the outgoing URLs.

components.functionality: Initializes the Home page by calling getCategories and getHomeFeaturedContent with no parameters, aggregating their responses, and exposing loading, success, and error states to child components.

Displays top-level navigation including a Home link, dynamic category-based shopping links, and static links to design services, design packages, custom furniture, journal, style quiz, account, and cart.
Renders a grid or list of top-level product categories sourced from getCategories as interactive tiles that route users into the products listing filtered by the selected category.

Displays a subset of featured products from getHomeFeaturedContent.featuredProducts. For each product, it shows key information and, on click, navigates the user to the products listing filtered by the product's category, derived by matching the product's categoryName with the loaded categories.

Displays featured design services and design packages from getHomeFeaturedContent as grouped entries. Clicking any design service entry routes to design_services.html, and clicking any design package entry routes to design_packages.html.
Provides a dedicated entry point from Home into the custom furniture section, routing users to the custom_furniture.html page.
Provides a clear, functional link or button that routes users from Home to the style quiz start page at style_quiz.html.

Displays a selection of featured articles from getHomeFeaturedContent.featuredArticles as preview cards and optionally a general entry that routes to the full journal. All interactions navigate to blog.html.
Shows user-readable error feedback if getCategories or getHomeFeaturedContent fails, and exposes a retry action that re-triggers the failed API calls via the data loader.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a centered fixed-width layout on a 12-column grid (approx. 1100–1200px content width) with generous outer margins. Content within each major section appears to follow a simple 2-column grid (image vs text) for article listings.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a strictly linear, top-to-bottom flow with a full-width header at the top and all primary content stacked within a centered, fixed-width main column (approx. 1100–1200px). Inside that main column, important feature strips (featured product, journal previews) follow the mockup’s article pattern: image anchored on the left, corresponding text on the right, and a meta/CTA row beneath. Utility components (loader, error notice) sit in the same column but are visually lighter and do not break the editorial rhythm.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a strictly linear, top-to-bottom flow with a full-width header at the top and all primary content stacked within a centered, fixed-width main column (approx. 1100–1200px). Inside that main column, important feature strips (featured product, journal previews) follow the mockup’s article pattern: image anchored on the left, corresponding text on the right, and a meta/CTA row beneath. Utility components (loader, error notice) sit in the same column but are visually lighter and do not break the editorial rhythm.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header spans the full viewport width with a slim vertical footprint. All other components sit inside the centered main content column that occupies roughly 60–70% of the viewport width. Major sections (category grid, featured product, services, link panels, journal preview) are separated by 32–48px of vertical spacing, often reinforced by a thin horizontal rule at the top or bottom of the section. Within each component, content padding is 16–24px and vertical spacing between text elements is 8–16px. Image/text feature strips follow a 35–40% (image) to 60–65% (text) horizontal split.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep overall density low and decidedly editorial: only one or two key components are visible at a time, with ample white space between and within sections. Category shortcuts, service offerings, and journal previews are presented in small, curated sets rather than dense grids. Each component is allowed breathing room, with text kept concise and typography set with generous line height and moderate line lengths.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Follow a gentle Z-pattern: the eye starts at the logo on the top left, then scans right across navigation, drops into the main column to the first content block (category shortcuts), then is pulled left to the larger image anchor of the featured product strip and across to its text. From there, the eye flows down through services and link panels, with subtle horizontal rules providing visual checkpoints, and ends at the journal preview strip that mirrors the featured image/text pattern. Meta rows (date, category, CTAs like 'Read More >' or 'Shop now >') are aligned along the lower edge of key components to naturally catch the eye as it exits each section.

layout.overall_description: The page is structured as a calm, centered editorial layout: a slim, full-width navigation bar sits at the very top, with the brand mark on the left and a minimal text-based menu on the right, separated from the content by a thin horizontal rule. Below this, the main content forms a single, vertically stacked column centered in the viewport and constrained to about 1100–1200px, leaving generous left and right white margins. The first visible content inside this column is a refined category shortcut section, followed by a hero-like featured product strip that echoes a magazine feature: a wide landscape product or interior image locked on the left and descriptive copy on the right, finished with a quiet meta row. Further down, services and packages are presented as an editorial block, then two slim link panels for custom furniture and the style quiz, each styled like minimalist list entries with strong typographic hierarchy and right-aligned arrows. The page concludes with a journal preview strip that reuses the image-left, text-right pattern, grounding the page with a sense of depth and storytelling. Throughout, generous vertical spacing, thin dividing rules, and a restrained monochrome palette create an air of contemporary design studio sophistication.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader behaves as a subtle overlay within the centered main content column rather than a full-screen takeover. When active, a semi-transparent white veil covers the main column area starting just below the header navigation and extending down to cover all primary content components (categories, featured strip, services, links, journal preview), while leaving the header itself visible. At the vertical center of this main column, a small, minimalist loader indicator (e.g., a thin circular stroke or simple wordmark-style 'Loading…') appears, aligned to the center horizontally. Margins to the viewport edges remain untouched, preserving the feeling of a clean, editorial page. When inactive, the loader component effectively collapses and does not affect layout spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Overlay within the main content area; positioned fixed relative to the viewport horizontally (centered) but bounded vertically to the main content region below the header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header navigation is a full-width bar anchored at the very top of the page, stretching edge-to-edge across the viewport. Within it, the content is constrained to the same centered 1100–1200px width as the main column. The studio logo or wordmark sits on the far left, occupying roughly 15–20% of that inner width, set in a slightly larger, bold type. The primary navigation links are arranged in a single horizontal row on the right, aligned to the same baseline as the logo, using slim, uppercase or small-caps typography with generous letterspacing. Vertical padding is minimal (around 16–24px), creating a compact, refined bar. A very thin horizontal rule runs along the bottom edge of the header, visually separating it from the content below without adding bulk.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Optionally sticky at the top of the viewport so that it remains visible as users scroll, while the content beneath scrolls under it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category shortcut grid sits directly below the header (and below any error notice if present), inside the centered main column. A vertical gap of about 32–40px separates it from the header’s bottom rule, enhancing the clean editorial feel. The grid follows a 2-column layout aligned to the 12-column system: each shortcut card spans 6 columns (about 50% of the main content width) with a 24px gutter between them. If there are more than two categories, they wrap into subsequent rows with 24–32px vertical spacing between rows. Each category card is a light, borderless block with 16–24px of internal padding and a clear typographic hierarchy: the category name in a medium-weight heading at the top left of each card, a short one-line descriptor below, and optionally a minimal icon or right-aligned chevron that hints at navigation. There is generous white space within each card, and the card heights are consistent across a row to maintain a calm, grid-like rhythm. The entire grid is visually framed top and bottom by white space rather than heavy borders, aligning with the minimalist editorial style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow, expanding vertically as more category rows are added while preserving the centered column width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured product strip appears as the first major editorial feature after the category shortcuts, separated from them by about 40–48px of vertical spacing and optionally a light horizontal rule at the top of the strip. The section occupies the full width of the main column and is structured as a two-column layout reminiscent of the mockup’s primary article card. On the left, a large landscape product or interior image spans roughly 35–40% of the main column width and sits flush with the left edge of the column. On the right, aligned with the top of the image, a text block spans the remaining 60–65%: at the top, a small, understated label (e.g., 'Featured collection') in uppercase; beneath it, a bold but refined product or collection title; then a short, 2–3 line paragraph describing the piece or collection in a tone consistent with editorial copy. A primary action (e.g., 'Shop the collection >') appears below the description, aligned left within the text column, set in the same slim type as meta links. Below the main image/text area and spanning the full width of the main column, a slim horizontal rule separates the feature from its meta row: on the bottom left, secondary meta details such as product type or material tags; on the bottom right, a 'View all products >' link mirrors the mockup’s 'Read More >' placement. Generous breathing space (16–24px) surrounds each text element, and the overall strip feels like a hero story rather than a crowded product promotion.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section within the main column, maintaining its left-image/right-text layout on larger screens; on smaller screens it can stack image above text while preserving generous spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The services and packages panel sits below the featured product strip, with approximately 40px of vertical separation and a subtle horizontal rule at its top to mark a conceptual shift from 'shop' to 'services.' Within the main centered column, the panel uses a balanced two-column layout to echo the editorial feel. The left column (about 55–60% of the main width) contains the section heading (e.g., 'Design Services') aligned to the left, followed by a concise introductory paragraph explaining the studio’s approach and value. Text lines are relatively short with generous line height to maintain readability. The right column (about 40–45%) presents 2–3 key service packages in a vertical stack: each package name appears as a small subheading with 8–12px space below, followed by a one-line descriptor and a minimalist inline link (e.g., 'View package details >') aligned to the right edge of the column. Between packages, 16–24px of vertical space keeps the list from feeling dense. The panel’s bottom edge is defined by white space rather than a strong boundary, allowing a soft transition into the subsequent link panels.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the main flow, conforming to the central column and maintaining a consistent 2-column relationship on larger screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The custom furniture link panel follows the services panel as a slim, horizontal band that feels like a refined list entry rather than a full card. It is separated from the services section by about 32px of white space and possibly a very faint horizontal rule aligned with the main column. Inside the centered column, the content is arranged in a single row: on the left, a short, bold-ish heading such as 'Custom Furniture' aligned to the column start; to its right, a short, muted one-line descriptor set in lighter type; and on the far right, an inline link styled as 'Explore custom >' or similar, with a subtle right-facing chevron. The row sits on a baseline that corresponds to the rest of the typography in the main column. Vertical padding above and below this row is modest (16–20px), creating the feeling of a slim announcement strip. The overall effect is that of a calm transition link, anchoring the eye to the left for the title and ending at the right with the arrow link, mirroring the mockup’s bottom meta-row behavior.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline panel within the main column, behaving like a full-width list item with consistent left and right alignment to the main content grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The style quiz link panel is stacked directly beneath the custom furniture link panel with a small but clear vertical separation (24–32px), forming a visually related pair. It follows a similar slim, horizontal band treatment, but can be slightly more playful in balance to signal interactivity. Within the centered main column, the left side might carry a short, intriguing label like 'Style Quiz' while the right side holds an inline call-to-action such as 'Find your style >' in the familiar slim meta-link style. Alternatively, a brief supporting phrase (e.g., '3 minutes to your ideal interior') can sit just to the right of the label, with the CTA aligned flush right, maintaining the left-to-right reading flow. Vertical padding mirrors the custom furniture strip (16–20px), and there are no heavy borders—only subtle use of spacing and alignment. This mirroring of structure reinforces hierarchy and keeps the editorial rhythm consistent.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline panel that aligns perfectly with the custom furniture panel above, maintaining the same column width and left/right margins for a harmonious pair.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The journal preview strip is positioned near the bottom of the page as the final substantive content block, separated from the style quiz panel by 40–48px of vertical space and introduced with a simple section label like 'Journal' or 'From the Journal' aligned left within the main column. Below this heading, one or two journal previews are presented, each using the same image-left, text-right structure as the featured product strip but at a slightly smaller scale. Each preview is a horizontal card: on the left, a medium-sized thumbnail image (about 30–35% of the main column width) aligned to the left edge; on the right, a text column (65–70%) that includes a compact article title at the top, followed by a one or two-line teaser paragraph. Beneath this text block, and spanning the main column width, a slim horizontal rule and meta row echo the mockup: on the bottom left, the publication date and category; on the bottom right, a 'Read More >' link. Each preview card is separated from the next by 24–32px of vertical spacing, and the entire strip is cushioned by ample whitespace above and below, giving it the calm, editorial ending suitable for a design studio homepage.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked section; journal cards follow the established left-image/right-text pattern and expand vertically as more previews are added, while remaining within the centered main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error notice appears directly beneath the header navigation and above the main content column when needed, without disrupting the overall minimalist aesthetic. It spans the same centered 1100–1200px width as the main content and is aligned to that column rather than stretching full viewport width. The notice is presented as a slim horizontal bar with 12–16px of vertical padding and 16–24px of horizontal inset padding inside the column. The message text is aligned to the left, set in small but clear type, with any iconography kept very minimal and placed at the far left of the bar. If there is an action such as 'Retry', it appears as a subtle inline text link aligned to the right side of the bar. A very faint background tint or a thin border line at the top and bottom of the bar differentiates it from the white page background without breaking the clean editorial feel. There is a small vertical gap (16–24px) between the bottom of the error notice and the first main content section (typically the category shortcut grid).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, non-sticky bar that occupies space in the main flow only when an error is present; otherwise it collapses so that the main content sits directly below the header.

name: Product Listing & Category
title: Products
description: Product listing and category page for browsing, filtering, and sorting furniture and decor by category, subcategory, attributes, and style.

core_features: Determine the current browsing context from URL parameters (categoryId, subcategoryId, styleKey, styleQuizResultId) and expose it to all components.
Load available top-level categories for navigation and context using getCategories.
Load subcategories for the current category using getSubcategoriesForCategory and allow navigation between them via URL parameter changes.

Load available filter options for the current category and optional subcategory using getProductFilterOptions, and render them as interactive filters (seating capacity, style, color/fabric, material, table features, price range, rating, free shipping).
Build a filters object from URL parameters and call listProducts with categoryId, subcategoryId, styleKey, filters, sortOption, pageNumber, and pageSize to retrieve products.
Render a product grid with product cards that show key product attributes and link to product detail pages using product.html?id={productId}.
Provide sorting controls (e.g., price, rating, relevance) that map to the sortOption parameter passed to listProducts.
Provide pagination controls that use pageNumber and pageSize, synchronized with URL parameters, to request and display additional result pages from listProducts.
Provide breadcrumbs based on the current category and subcategory context, with links that update URL parameters (e.g., removing subcategoryId or categoryId).
Provide an active-filters summary allowing users to clear individual filters or all filters by updating URL parameters and re-calling listProducts.
user_workflows:
Browse by category from global navigation:
1. User clicks a category link in the header that navigates to products.html with categoryId in the URL and no subcategoryId.
2. On page load, url-state-manager parses categoryId from the URL and exposes it as currentCategoryId.
3. category-context-loader calls getCategories to resolve and mark the active category.
4. subcategory-nav calls getSubcategoriesForCategory with currentCategoryId and renders the returned subcategories.
5. filter-sidebar calls getProductFilterOptions with currentCategoryId and no subcategoryId and renders available filters.
6. product-grid-controller calls listProducts with categoryId=currentCategoryId, subcategoryId unset, styleKey from URL if present, filters derived from URL filter parameters, sortOption from URL, and pageNumber/pageSize from URL (or defaults) and renders the product grid.
7. User refines with filters, sort, or pagination; each interaction updates URL parameters and triggers a new listProducts call.

Narrow to a subcategory from within a category:
1. User clicks a subcategory in subcategory-nav.
2. The click handler constructs a new URL for products.html that includes categoryId=currentCategoryId and subcategoryId=clickedSubcategoryId, and preserves styleKey and styleQuizResultId if present.
3. The browser navigates to the new URL; url-state-manager parses the new parameters.
4. subcategory-nav re-calls getSubcategoriesForCategory with categoryId and marks the clicked subcategory as active based on subcategoryId.
5. filter-sidebar calls getProductFilterOptions with both categoryId and subcategoryId to load more specific filter options.
6. product-grid-controller calls listProducts with categoryId, subcategoryId, styleKey (if present), filters from URL, sortOption from URL, and pageNumber/pageSize, and renders the updated products.

Filter products within the current context:
1. User adjusts one or more filters in filter-sidebar (e.g., selects a seating capacity option or a material).
2. filter-sidebar updates the URL query parameters to represent the selected filters using a consistent naming scheme (e.g., filter_seatingCapacity, filter_material, filter_priceMin, filter_priceMax, filter_rating, filter_freeShipping) and resets pageNumber to the first page.
3. url-state-manager detects the URL change and updates the in-memory filter state.
4. product-grid-controller builds the filters object for listProducts from the current URL filter parameters and calls listProducts.
5. active-filters-bar reads the same URL filter parameters to display active filter chips.
6. If the user removes a filter chip, active-filters-bar updates the URL to remove that filter parameter, which again triggers url-state-manager and listProducts.

Sort the product listing:
1. User selects a sort option in sort-bar.
2. sort-bar updates the sort parameter in the URL (e.g., sortOption) and resets pageNumber to the first page.
3. url-state-manager updates its internal state and notifies product-grid-controller.
4. product-grid-controller calls listProducts with the updated sortOption and current filters, categoryId, subcategoryId, styleKey, and pageNumber/pageSize.
5. product-grid re-renders the list in the new order.

Paginate through results:
1. User clicks a pagination control (next page, previous page, or a specific page number) in pagination-controls.
2. pagination-controls updates the pageNumber parameter in the URL; pageSize remains in the URL or is managed via default logic.
3. url-state-manager updates pageNumber in page state.
4. product-grid-controller calls listProducts with the new pageNumber and the current categoryId, subcategoryId, styleKey, filters, sortOption, and pageSize.
5. product-grid renders the products for the new page; results-summary updates using totalResults, totalPages, and pageNumber from the listProducts response.

View product details from the listing:
1. User clicks a product card’s image or name in product-grid.
2. product-grid constructs a link to product.html with the productId as the id URL parameter.
3. Browser navigates to product.html?id={productId}, leaving products.html.

Style-based browsing (arrive from style results):
1. User arrives on products.html from style_results.html with URL parameters styleKey and optionally styleQuizResultId, and optionally categoryId/subcategoryId.
2. url-state-manager parses styleKey, styleQuizResultId, categoryId, and subcategoryId from the URL.
3. If categoryId is present, category-context-loader and subcategory-nav behave as in the category workflows; if categoryId is absent, subcategory navigation is hidden and getSubcategoriesForCategory is not called.
4. filter-sidebar calls getProductFilterOptions with categoryId and subcategoryId if they are present; otherwise, it omits them or passes neutral values as allowed by that interface.
5. product-grid-controller calls listProducts with styleKey from the URL, along with any present categoryId, subcategoryId, filters, sortOption, pageNumber, and pageSize.
6. A style-context-indicator (part of results-summary or header context) derives a human-readable style name either from the styleOptions returned by getProductFilterOptions or from the styleName field on products in listProducts, and displays the current style context.
7. As users navigate to other subcategories (if categoryId is present) or adjust filters/sort, styleKey and styleQuizResultId are preserved in all constructed URLs so style-based personalization remains active.

Use breadcrumbs to navigate up the hierarchy:
1. User sees a breadcrumb trail such as Home > [Category] > [Subcategory] based on current URL parameters and loaded category/subcategory data.
2. Clicking the Home breadcrumb navigates to index.html.
3. Clicking the category breadcrumb constructs a URL for products.html with only categoryId (removing subcategoryId and filter parameters) while optionally preserving styleKey and styleQuizResultId if they are part of the experience design.
4. Clicking the subcategory breadcrumb (if present and clickable) either keeps the user on the current page or allows switching between subcategories by updating subcategoryId in the URL.
5. Each breadcrumb click causes url-state-manager to refresh page state and product-grid-controller to re-call listProducts with the new context.
interactions: On page load (or when URL changes), url-state-manager parses categoryId, subcategoryId, styleKey, styleQuizResultId, all filter-related parameters, sortOption, pageNumber, and pageSize from the URL query string and exposes them as reactive state to other components.
category-header-context calls getCategories once on initialization (or when categoryId changes) and marks the category whose id or key matches the URL categoryId as active.

subcategory-nav listens for currentCategoryId changes; when present, it calls getSubcategoriesForCategory(categoryId) and renders a list of subcategories. Clicking a subcategory item navigates to products.html with categoryId set to currentCategoryId and subcategoryId set to the clicked subcategory’s key or id.

filter-sidebar listens for currentCategoryId and currentSubcategoryId changes; it calls getProductFilterOptions(categoryId, subcategoryId) when either changes and renders controls for each available filter option group, including price range and rating options only if those are present in the response.

When a filter control in filter-sidebar changes, filter-sidebar updates the corresponding filter_* parameters in the URL (and resets pageNumber) without directly calling product APIs; url-state-manager detects this and informs product-grid-controller.

active-filters-bar reads current URL filter parameters and displays them as removable chips. Clicking a chip removes the corresponding filter parameter from the URL, triggering url-state-manager and a new listProducts call.

sort-bar reads the current sortOption from the URL and highlights the active sort. When the user selects a different sort, sort-bar updates the sortOption parameter in the URL (and resets pageNumber) and does not directly call APIs.

results-summary listens to listProducts results (totalResults, totalPages, pageNumber, pageSize) and current filters/style context, and displays a summary such as the total number of items and the current range, based entirely on API data and URL state.

product-grid-controller subscribes to all relevant URL-derived state (categoryId, subcategoryId, styleKey, filters, sortOption, pageNumber, pageSize). Whenever any of these change, it builds the filters object for listProducts from the URL parameters and calls listProducts.

product-grid renders each product returned from listProducts as a card using only the API data (name, price, listPrice, isOnSale, imageUrl, ratingAverage, ratingCount, freeShipping, shippingCost, categoryName, subcategoryName, styleName, seatingCapacity, colorName, fabricMaterial, primaryMaterial, isExtendable, extendedLengthCm, keyFeatures). Clicking on a card image or title navigates to product.html?id={productId}.

pagination-controls reads pageNumber, totalPages, and totalResults from listProducts; clicking next/previous or a page number updates the pageNumber URL parameter, which then triggers a new listProducts call via product-grid-controller.

breadcrumb-trail listens to category and subcategory context from category-header-context and subcategory-nav, and constructs navigation links that modify categoryId and subcategoryId (and optionally filter and style parameters) in the URL; clicking these links performs full navigations rather than in-place state changes.

If styleKey is present in the URL, a style-context-indicator uses styleOptions from getProductFilterOptions or styleName from listProducts results to show which style is currently active, without assuming any fixed style labels.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is fully driven by URL query parameters; no client-side persistent storage is used. On every load or URL change, the url-state-manager parses the query string into a structured state object: (1) categoryId and subcategoryId define the hierarchical browsing context. If categoryId is present, getSubcategoriesForCategory and getProductFilterOptions are called with this value; if subcategoryId is also present, both interfaces receive it for more precise context. If categoryId is absent, subcategory navigation is disabled and getSubcategoriesForCategory is not called. (2) styleKey, when present, indicates that the listing should be filtered by style. styleKey is passed directly to listProducts. If styleKey is absent, style-based filtering is not applied. styleQuizResultId, when present, is preserved and propagated in internal navigational URLs for tracking and personalization but is not sent to any of the assigned interfaces. (3) Filter parameters are encoded into the URL using a stable naming convention, such as filter_seatingCapacity, filter_styleKey, filter_color, filter_fabric, filter_material, filter_tableFeature, filter_priceMin, filter_priceMax, filter_minRating, filter_freeShipping. url-state-manager collects all filter_* parameters and converts them into the filters object passed to listProducts. Only filter keys that are present in the URL are included in filters. getProductFilterOptions defines which filter groups and options are available; components never assume any fixed set of filters and only render or apply filters that are represented both in URL parameters and API responses. (4) The sortOption parameter in the URL encodes the current sort selection; sort-bar reads this parameter to determine the active sort and writes it when the user changes sorting. product-grid-controller passes it directly to listProducts. (5) Pagination state is encoded via pageNumber and pageSize URL parameters. When pagination-controls modifies pageNumber, url-state-manager updates the internal state and triggers product-grid-controller to call listProducts with the new pageNumber. If pageSize is not present, a default is used at runtime but is not hardcoded in this specification; if present, it is passed through to listProducts and used by pagination-controls. (6) Breadcrumb and navigation components do not maintain any separate state; they build links purely from the current URL state and the category/subcategory data returned by getCategories and getSubcategoriesForCategory. (7) All components that need data obtain it via the assigned interfaces or via URL-derived state; no static or hardcoded data values (such as fixed category names, style labels, or price ranges) are used within this page specification.

components.functionality: Parses the current URL query string into a structured state object (categoryId, subcategoryId, styleKey, styleQuizResultId, filter parameters, sortOption, pageNumber, pageSize) and exposes it as reactive state to other components. Updates the URL when requested by other components, ensuring that any state change is reflected in the query string and that components reinitialize correctly on page reload or navigation.

Loads all categories using getCategories, determines the active category from the categoryId URL parameter (matching either id or key from the API), and makes the list of categories and the active category available to navigation and breadcrumb components.

Builds a breadcrumb trail based on the current category and subcategory context. Uses data from category-header-context and subcategory-nav to display links for higher-level navigation. Clicking a breadcrumb updates URL parameters (e.g., removing subcategoryId or categoryId) and navigates accordingly.

When a categoryId is present, calls getSubcategoriesForCategory(categoryId) to retrieve subcategories and renders them as navigable items. Highlights the active subcategory based on the subcategoryId URL parameter. Clicking a subcategory item updates the URL with the corresponding subcategoryId (and preserves categoryId, styleKey, and styleQuizResultId) to change the product listing context.

Uses getProductFilterOptions with the current categoryId and subcategoryId to retrieve available filter groups and options (including seating capacity, style, color, fabric, material, table features, price range, rating options, and free shipping capability). Renders only those filter controls that are present in the response. When the user changes filters, it encodes the selections into filter_* URL parameters and requests url-state-manager to update the URL and reset pageNumber. It does not call listProducts directly.

Reads current filter-related URL parameters and displays them as removable filter chips or badges. Each chip corresponds to one filter_* parameter or group of parameters. When a chip is removed, it requests url-state-manager to remove the associated filter parameter(s) from the URL and reset pageNumber, which triggers new data loading via product-grid-controller.

Displays available sort options defined by the product listing design (such as price, rating, or relevance), and determines the active choice based on the sortOption URL parameter. When a user selects a sort option, it updates the sortOption parameter in the URL (and resets pageNumber) via url-state-manager, causing product-grid-controller to reload data using listProducts.

Shows a concise summary of the current listing state, including totalResults, current page, and pageSize from listProducts, and uses URL state to reflect the active category, subcategory, and style (if applicable). It does not store static labels and only uses names provided by the APIs (e.g., categoryName, subcategoryName, styleName) when available.

If a styleKey is present in the URL, this component resolves a human-readable style name by matching the styleKey with styleOptions from getProductFilterOptions or with styleName fields from products returned by listProducts. It then displays the active style context. It updates whenever styleKey or filter options/products change.

Acts as the central orchestrator for product retrieval. Listens to URL-derived state (categoryId, subcategoryId, styleKey, filter_* parameters, sortOption, pageNumber, pageSize), builds the filters object expected by listProducts from the filter_* parameters, and calls listProducts whenever relevant state changes. It passes the resulting products and pagination metadata down to product-grid, results-summary, and pagination-controls.

Receives the products array from product-grid-controller (populated via listProducts) and renders them as interactive product cards. Each card displays only data fields returned by the API (such as name, price, listPrice, isOnSale, imageUrl, ratingAverage, ratingCount, freeShipping, shippingCost, categoryName, subcategoryName, styleName, seatingCapacity, colorName, fabricMaterial, primaryMaterial, isExtendable, extendedLengthCm, keyFeatures). Clicking a card image or title navigates to product.html with id set to the product’s id.

Displays pagination UI based on totalResults, pageNumber, pageSize, and totalPages returned by listProducts. When the user selects a new page or clicks next/previous, it requests url-state-manager to update the pageNumber parameter in the URL. It does not call listProducts directly; product-grid-controller handles data fetching in response to URL changes.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a centered fixed-width layout on a 12-column grid (approx. 1100–1200px content width) with generous outer margins. Content within each major section appears to follow a simple 2-column grid (image vs text) for article listings.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a vertically stacked, centered main column following the editorial mockup: global site header (outside this spec) at the very top, then breadcrumb, then a quiet category header region (title + context), followed by a slim subcategory navigation row. Below that, introduce the main listing band as a two-column region: a narrow, refined filter sidebar on the left and the product content column on the right. Within the product column, stack: active filters bar, then a slim meta row combining results summary and sort controls, then the product grid, and finally pagination. Each zone is separated by generous vertical spacing and often a thin horizontal rule.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a vertically stacked, centered main column following the editorial mockup: global site header (outside this spec) at the very top, then breadcrumb, then a quiet category header region (title + context), followed by a slim subcategory navigation row. Below that, introduce the main listing band as a two-column region: a narrow, refined filter sidebar on the left and the product content column on the right. Within the product column, stack: active filters bar, then a slim meta row combining results summary and sort controls, then the product grid, and finally pagination. Each zone is separated by generous vertical spacing and often a thin horizontal rule.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Keep the primary content in a centered fixed-width container of about 1100–1200px, occupying roughly 60–70% of the viewport width. Breadcrumb, category header, and subcategory nav all span this central column. Inside the main listing region, allocate ~28–30% of the content width to the left filter sidebar and ~70–72% to the right product column. Maintain 32–48px vertical gaps between major sections (breadcrumbs → header, header → subcategory nav, nav → filters/products, product grid → pagination) and 12–20px between elements within a section, with occasional thin horizontal rules reinforcing separations.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low-density, editorial feel: a small number of elements per row, plenty of white space around all interactive controls, and no compact, dashboard-like packing. Filter categories are clearly separated, product cards are roomy with large imagery, and the meta rows (active filters, sort, results) feel airy and understated rather than cramped. The page should feel more like a contemporary interior design magazine layout than a typical crowded e-commerce catalog.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye in a gentle Z-pattern: starting at the top-left breadcrumb trail, moving right across any subtle meta in that row, then dropping down to the bold category title in the header. From the header, the gaze moves downward to the subcategory nav, then diagonally down-left to the filter sidebar as a structural anchor, and across to the right into the active filters and product meta row. From there, attention naturally flows downward through the product grid, ending at the centered pagination. Thin rules and aligned left edges reinforce a calm, linear reading rhythm.

layout.overall_description: The page sits within a centered, fixed-width column on a white background, evoking a minimalist design magazine. At the very top of this column, a thin, understated breadcrumb line anchors the user in the hierarchy. Beneath it, with generous breathing room, a large, left-aligned category title and short description form a quiet editorial header, subtly accompanied by a small style/context label. A slim horizontal subcategory navigation row follows, using light typography and ample spacing. Below a comfortable gap, the layout resolves into a calm two-column band: a narrow, vertically structured filter sidebar on the left and a wider product column on the right. Above the products, the active filters bar stretches across the product column with compact chips and a light rule beneath, followed by a refined meta row with results count on the left and sorting control on the right. The product grid itself is intentionally spacious, typically two products per row, each card with a large image and minimal text, mirroring the editorial feeling of the mockup’s article card. At the bottom, after another generous buffer and a thin rule, centered pagination controls appear as the final touch. The overall composition feels balanced, quiet, and image-led, with clean left alignments and controlled, consistent spacing tying the experience together.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual, structural component that lives logically at the top of the page structure, wrapping or sitting just above the main content column. It does not occupy visible space or alter the layout; instead, it silently coordinates URL parameters with filters, sorting, pagination, and category context behind the scenes. It should not introduce any margins, padding, or visual artifacts in the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; participates only in the document structure and state, with no rendered dimensions or positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Placed near the top of the centered content column, the category header spans roughly 80–100% of the central width (about 1100–1200px) and is left-aligned. It appears 24–32px below the breadcrumb trail, with no bounding box, just type on white. The main category title sits prominently at the top-left of this block, using a large editorial-style heading. Directly beneath, aligned to the same left edge, a short one- or two-line description runs across about 60–70% of the content width to keep the section light and readable. The style-context-indicator is visually associated with this block—either as a small, right-aligned meta element in the same horizontal band as the title or as a subtle pill or label immediately below or to the right of the title—so that the entire header reads as a unified editorial hero. A 32–40px gap below the description leads into the subcategory navigation, sometimes reinforced by a thin horizontal rule spanning the content width.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the normal document flow, defining the top of the main content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb sits at the very top of the main content column, directly beneath the global site header, and spans the same centered width of ~1100–1200px. It is left-aligned and occupies a single, slim row with a small typographic scale, visually understated. Vertically, it uses about 8–12px of line height plus 12–16px of padding above and below before the category header begins. There are no heavy backgrounds; breadcrumb links appear as simple text with subtle separators, creating a thin, horizontal line of text that anchors the user’s position without drawing too much attention.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupying the top-most visible row of the main content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The subcategory navigation appears directly beneath the category header context, separated by roughly 24–32px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the centered content column but remains visually light: a row of left-aligned text links or pill-like buttons with generous horizontal spacing between items. The nav height is modest, with 12–16px of vertical padding within the row. A thin horizontal rule either runs just above or just below this navigation row, reinforcing its role as a divider between the header and the main listing area. The nav items themselves do not form heavy tabs; they are subtle, typographic cues, in keeping with the editorial aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the normal document flow, forming a horizontal band between the header and the main filter/product region.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter sidebar forms the left column of the main listing band. It begins roughly 32–40px beneath the subcategory navigation and aligns with the left edge of the main content column. Horizontally, it occupies about 28–30% of the central content width (approximately 300–340px on a 1200px content width). Vertically, it extends down alongside the product grid, with its top aligned to the top of the active filters/sort area in the product column. Inside, filters are organized into groups with 16–24px internal padding and 12–16px vertical spacing between labels, controls, and options. Each filter group is separated by a thin horizontal rule and 16–24px of vertical space, emphasizing a calm, structured hierarchy rather than a dense sidebar. There is ample left padding to align text neatly, and no heavy borders or backgrounds—only subtle lines and typographic weight to define sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main two-column layout, but may adopt a sticky position on taller viewports so that the top of the sidebar remains visible as the user scrolls the product grid, while respecting the header and footer boundaries.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The active filters bar sits at the top of the right-hand product column, horizontally aligned with the top of the filter-sidebar but occupying only the product column’s width (about 70–72% of the main content column). It starts approximately 32–40px below the subcategory nav. Within this bar, active filter chips or labels are left-aligned, flowing in one or two lines as needed, with 8–12px gaps between each. On the far right of the same row, a minimal 'Clear all' link or similar control balances the composition. The bar uses 12–16px vertical padding and remains visually flat against the white background, with a thin horizontal rule at its bottom edge to separate it from the results-summary/sort row below. If no filters are active, this row may collapse or reduce to a single subtle text line, preserving the same vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the product column, sticking to the top of that column’s flow; does not overlay content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-bar shares a horizontal band with the results-summary, forming a slender meta row directly beneath the active-filters-bar within the product column. This row spans the full width of the product column. On the left side (occupying roughly 50–60% of the row), the results-summary appears; on the right side (about 40–50%), the sort control is right-aligned. The sort control itself is minimal—a simple dropdown or segmented control with subtle outlining and 8–12px padding, sized just large enough for clarity without dominating. The row has around 12–16px vertical padding and sits 8–12px below the active filters bar, with a thin horizontal rule either at its bottom or sharing the rule with the row above to maintain the layered editorial look. This positions sorting and meta information as a quiet, utility strip above the grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the product column, anchored just above the product grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results-summary occupies the left half of the shared meta row with the sort-bar, directly under the active-filters-bar in the product column. It is left-aligned and constrained to a single line of text such as '24 items' or '24 armchairs in Oak & Linen.' The typography is light and small-to-regular in size, matching the breadcrumb and other meta text styles. It uses the same 12–16px vertical padding as the sort-bar and sits within the same visual band, reinforcing that this is a supportive information row rather than a primary focal point.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; positioned inline within the shared meta row, without independent positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The style-context-indicator is a small, tertiary element visually attached to the category header block. It appears either as a subtle, outlined pill or a simple text label indicating a style context (e.g., 'Scandinavian', 'Minimal', or a current lookbook), placed to the right of the main category title on the same baseline or directly beneath the title aligned to the left. Its width is only as wide as its content, typically less than one-third of the header width. It uses minimal padding (6–10px) and light typography. The indicator sits within the vertical space already allocated to the category header, helping to contextualize the listing without adding extra height or drawing focus away from the main title.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; positioned inline within the category header context area, moving naturally with that block’s layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual orchestration layer that wraps the active-filters-bar, meta row (results-summary and sort-bar), product-grid, and pagination-controls. It spans the full width of the product column (about 70–72% of the content width) to the right of the filter-sidebar and aligns with its top edge. It ensures consistent spacing between its child components according to the editorial rhythm: 8–12px between the active filters and the meta row, 16–24px between the meta row and the product grid, and 24–32px between the grid and pagination. It does not render borders or backgrounds itself, but establishes the structural grouping of listing-related components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual structural container; participates in layout only by grouping and spacing its child components, following the two-column arrangement alongside the filter-sidebar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-grid occupies the main visual area of the right-hand product column, starting approximately 16–24px beneath the meta row (results-summary/sort). It spans the full width of the product column (about 70–72% of the central content width) and aligns its left edge with the left edge of the meta text above. To preserve the spacious, editorial feel, the grid uses two columns of product cards on desktop, each card taking roughly 48–50% of the product column width with a 16–24px gutter between them. Each product card has a large, top-aligned product image occupying the upper two-thirds of the card height, followed by a small block of text (product name, category, price) aligned to the left with 8–12px spacing between lines. Cards use 16–24px internal padding and remain on a white background, separated from each other by consistent gutters and, optionally, hairline rules at the card edges rather than heavy boxes. Vertically, rows of products are spaced with 24–32px between them, so the grid reads as a series of clean, image-led editorial tiles rather than dense thumbnails.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the product column, expanding vertically as more rows are loaded, and reflowing responsively to fewer columns on smaller screens while preserving generous spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-controls sit at the bottom of the product column, centered horizontally within that column. They appear approximately 24–32px below the last row of products. A thin horizontal rule spans the width of the product column just above the controls, visually signaling the end of the current grid. The controls themselves are compact—simple page numbers, next/previous arrows, or a minimal 'Load more' button—using light typography and unobtrusive outlines. They occupy only as much width as needed, leaving generous white space on both left and right so that the eye can rest after scrolling through the products.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the end of the product column’s flow, remaining below the grid and moving downward as more products are shown.

name: Product Detail
title: Product Detail
description: Displays a single product with full details, specifications, images, shipping information, and controls to select quantity and add the item to the cart.

core_features: Load full product details based on the URL parameter id using getProductDetail(productId).
Display a product image gallery with primary image and additional views driven by product.imageUrls.
Show key product information including name, SKU, price, sale price state, rating, style, color, materials, and feature list.
Expose detailed specifications including dimensions (length, width, height), optional extended length for extendable products (e.g., dining tables), and material composition for comparison.
Display shipping details derived from shippingInfo, including whether shipping is free and any shipping label text.
Allow users to select a quantity and add the current product to the cart using addToCart(productId, quantity), and reflect the result state (success/error) on the page.
Provide navigation back to product listings (products.html) via category/subcategory breadcrumb links using URL parameters, and navigation to cart.html after adding items.
Show related products returned from getProductDetail.relatedProducts as an alternative browsing path, each linking to its own product detail page via URL parameter id.
user_workflows: View a product from a listing: 1) User clicks a product card on products.html or a featured product on index.html, which navigates to product.html?id={productId}. 2) On page load, the route logic reads the id parameter and initializes the product detail loader. 3) The product detail loader calls getProductDetail with productId = id. 4) Once data is returned, the page populates the image gallery, main info, specifications, shipping information, and related products section. 5) User scrolls through content, views images, and can switch between overview and specifications sections.

Inspect specifications and extended length for comparison: 1) After the product loads, user clicks the Specifications tab or anchor. 2) The tab controller updates a URL parameter (e.g., section=specs) to reflect the active section. 3) The specifications panel reads specifications.dimensionsCm and specifications.extendedLengthCm (if provided) as well as materials list. 4) User compares lengthCm vs extendedLengthCm for extendable products and notes seatingCapacity if available. 5) User can copy or remember dimensions and navigate back to listings via breadcrumb if they want to compare with other products.

Select quantity and add product to cart: 1) User scrolls to the purchase area containing the add-to-cart form. 2) User adjusts the quantity field (e.g., via stepper or input) which updates the internal quantity state and may update a displayed line total (quantity × product.price) without calling any additional APIs. 3) User clicks the Add to Cart button, triggering addToCart(productId = product.id, quantity = chosen quantity). 4) While the request is in progress, the button shows a loading state and the form is disabled. 5) On success, the component reads the returned cart summary and success message and displays a confirmation with updated cart item count. 6) User can optionally click a “View Cart” control to navigate to cart.html or close the confirmation to continue browsing.

Browse and switch between related products: 1) After getProductDetail resolves, the related products strip is populated from relatedProducts. 2) User clicks on a related product card. 3) The click updates the URL to product.html?id={relatedProduct.id}. 4) The route logic detects the id change, re-runs getProductDetail with the new productId, and resets all product-specific state (images, description, specs, quantity) to the new product. 5) User continues browsing, repeating the process as needed.

Navigate back to category or subcategory listing: 1) Once product details have loaded, the breadcrumb component renders links for the category and subcategory derived from the product’s category/subcategory identifiers. 2) When the user clicks on the category breadcrumb, the page navigates to products.html with categoryId and subcategoryId URL parameters set based on the current product’s category/subcategory. 3) On the products.html page, the listing logic uses these parameters for pre-filtering; the user can then choose another product, which again links back to product.html?id={newProductId}.
interactions: On page load, read URL parameter id; if present, trigger data loading via getProductDetail; if missing or invalid, show an error state with an option to retry (if the URL changes) or navigate back.

Interaction with product image gallery: clicking a thumbnail updates the selected main image; optionally, keyboard navigation or next/previous controls update the current image index. The selected image index can be reflected in a URL parameter (e.g., imageIndex) so that reloading the page preserves the same visible image.

Interaction with overview/specifications sections: clicking section tabs updates the active section in the in-memory state and synchronizes it with a URL parameter (e.g., section=overview or section=specs), ensuring deep links restore the same section on reload.

Interaction with quantity input: users can increase or decrease the quantity using stepper controls or direct text input, with validation to prevent invalid quantities (e.g., non-numeric or less than 1); invalid input disables the Add to Cart button until corrected.

Interaction with Add to Cart button: clicking the button triggers addToCart, sets a loading state, and on completion either shows a success message with updated cart summary or an error message if addToCart.success is false or the call fails.

Interaction with cart navigation: within the add-to-cart confirmation or header area, clicking a cart-related control navigates to cart.html (no additional URL parameters), allowing the user to review their cart.

Interaction with breadcrumb navigation: clicking category or subcategory breadcrumb links navigates to products.html with appropriate categoryId and subcategoryId URL parameters derived from the current product’s category/subcategory identifiers.

Interaction with related products: clicking any related product card navigates to product.html?id={relatedProduct.id}; the page listens for URL changes and re-runs the getProductDetail flow without a full application reload if possible.

Error and retry interactions: if getProductDetail or addToCart returns an error or cannot reach the backend, an inline error is shown along with a Retry control; clicking Retry will re-execute the same interface call with the current parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary external state driver is the URL. The product.html page reads the id query parameter on load and on any subsequent URL change. If id is present, it is treated as the productId for getProductDetail. If id is missing or empty, the page enters a 'no product selected' error state and does not call getProductDetail. When id changes (for example, via navigation from related products or the browser back/forward buttons), the page resets all product-specific UI state (loading, selected image, selected section, quantity, error messages) and re-invokes getProductDetail with the new productId. Optional URL parameters are used for secondary state: 'section' controls which section is active (e.g., 'overview' or 'specs'); if absent, a default section is used. 'imageIndex' can represent the selected main image index; if present and valid (within the bounds of product.imageUrls), the gallery initializes with that image selected. These parameters are updated in the URL via push/replace state when the user switches sections or images, ensuring state is shareable and recoverable on refresh. The quantity control uses in-memory state only and resets whenever id changes to avoid carrying quantities between products. On successful addToCart, no new URL parameters are required; the cart summary is reflected in the UI only. Navigation to products.html uses URL parameters categoryId and subcategoryId derived from the current product’s category and subcategory identifiers (from the Product model obtained via getProductDetail), so that the listing page can pre-filter appropriately. Navigation to cart.html does not rely on additional parameters; the cart page is responsible for loading the current cart context.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a centered fixed-width layout on a 12-column grid (approx. 1100–1200px content width) with generous outer margins. Content within each major section appears to follow a simple 2-column grid (image vs text) for article listings.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps a strictly linear, top-to-bottom editorial flow. At the very top sits a full-width, slim header and navigation bar. Below it, the main content is centered within a fixed-width column (roughly 1100–1200px) and follows a stacked sequence: an initial product identity/introduction band, then a primary product hero block that mirrors the mockup’s article card (large product imagery on the left, textual details on the right). Under this hero block, a thin horizontal rule separates a meta-information strip (key specs, availability, and primary CTA area) that stretches horizontally, echoing the mockup’s date/category and ‘Read More’ row. Additional detail sections (extended description, materials, shipping, related items) stack vertically beneath, each clearly segmented with generous spacing and occasional thin rules to keep the editorial rhythm.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps a strictly linear, top-to-bottom editorial flow. At the very top sits a full-width, slim header and navigation bar. Below it, the main content is centered within a fixed-width column (roughly 1100–1200px) and follows a stacked sequence: an initial product identity/introduction band, then a primary product hero block that mirrors the mockup’s article card (large product imagery on the left, textual details on the right). Under this hero block, a thin horizontal rule separates a meta-information strip (key specs, availability, and primary CTA area) that stretches horizontally, echoing the mockup’s date/category and ‘Read More’ row. Additional detail sections (extended description, materials, shipping, related items) stack vertically beneath, each clearly segmented with generous spacing and occasional thin rules to keep the editorial rhythm.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header spans the full viewport width but remains shallow in height with minimal padding, keeping focus on the content below. The core content column occupies around 60–70% of the viewport width and is centered to create generous left and right margins. Within the main hero block, product imagery takes approximately 35–40% of the horizontal space on the left, while the right-hand column (product name, price, key details, and primary action) occupies 60–65%, closely echoing the mockup’s image-text split. Vertical spacing between these main bands (intro, hero, meta strip, and subsequent sections) follows the larger step of the scale (around 32–48px), while internal spacing within each block (e.g., product name to price, price to description, description to buttons) follows the smaller increments (around 12–20px).
layout_strategies.content_density: The content density remains intentionally low, resembling a design studio catalogue rather than a commercial storefront. Each section reveals only a curated subset of information at once: the hero area focuses on a single product image and essential purchase details, while deeper specification, material, and shipping panels are visually quiet, using short paragraphs, structured lists, and plenty of white space. Large swathes of negative space separate blocks so that no area feels crowded; the design avoids dense tables or long, uninterrupted text columns, preferring concise blocks with breathing room.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a refined Z-pattern adapted from the mockup. The journey begins at the top-left logo, travels across the navigation to any key utility links on the right, then drops into the main content column. The eye first anchors on the product’s hero image on the left, then sweeps right to the product name, price, and key attributes, where the primary action (Add to Cart or equivalent) is placed near the top of this text block. From there, the eye moves downward through the right-hand text column to the secondary details, then drops to the full-width meta strip beneath the hero: starting on the left with concise product metadata and moving along to the right where secondary actions or links (such as share, view more images, or expanded specs) live. Below this, the flow becomes a gentle vertical scroll through well-separated sections of detail, always anchored by the centered column and thin dividing rules.

layout.overall_description: The product detail page reads like a minimalist editorial spread. A slim, full-width header with the studio’s logotype on the left and a light, evenly spaced navigation on the right sits at the very top, with modest vertical padding and plenty of surrounding white space. Just below, the main content column appears centered on the page, clearly distinct from the browser edges thanks to generous horizontal margins. The hero section dominates the initial viewport: on the left, a large, cleanly framed product photo occupies roughly one third of the content width; on the right, aligned to its top edge, a vertical stack presents the product name in understated yet confident type, followed by price, a concise, well-spaced descriptive paragraph, and a small cluster of subtle controls (such as quantity and a calm but clear Add to Cart button). This text column does not feel cramped; each line has air, with consistent line spacing and 12–20px gaps between textual elements. Beneath this hero block, a fine horizontal rule stretches across the content width, acting as a visual pause before a low-profile meta row that runs from left to right: on the left, brief product metadata (e.g., category, dimensions snippet, availability) sits in a single line or two, while on the far right, inline links or light secondary CTAs balance the composition. As the user scrolls, additional sections—extended description, detailed specifications, materials and finishes, care instructions, shipping and returns, and potentially a modest related-products strip—stack one after another, each separated by 32–48px of vertical white space and, where appropriate, a thin rule. The entire experience feels calm and curated: a consistent centered column, careful image-to-text ratios, and minimal visual noise emphasize the craftsmanship of the furniture and the sophistication of the studio brand.

name: Cart
title: Cart
description: Displays the current shopping cart with editable items, shipping costs, totals, and optional budget comparison, and provides navigation to product details, product listing, and checkout.

core_features: Load the current cart using getCart on page load and after each modification, showing all line items, quantities, prices, and totals.
Display each cart item with product name, thumbnail, category and style labels (if available), unit price, quantity, and line subtotal.
Allow users to change line item quantities using controls bound to updateCartItemQuantity, automatically refreshing cart totals.
Allow users to remove items from the cart using a Remove/trash control bound to removeCartItem, updating the cart list and totals.
Show aggregated pricing information (subtotal, shippingTotal, total, currency) based on the cart returned by getCart and subsequent update/remove calls.
Indicate shipping costs at item level (freeShipping and/or shippingCost) and display the overall shippingTotal at cart level.

Support budget-based decisions by comparing the cart total against a user-defined budget value read from and written to a URL parameter, highlighting over/under budget status without hardcoding amounts.
Provide navigation from each cart item to its product detail page via product.html?id={productId}.
Provide navigation back to product listing via a Continue Shopping control that links to products.html with the lastBrowsedCategoryId query parameter when available.
Provide navigation forward to the checkout flow via a Proceed to Checkout control (checkout implementation is out of scope).
user_workflows: View cart contents: 1) User navigates to cart.html. 2) On page load, the cart-page-init component calls getCart. 3) The cart-items-list component renders each returned item with its details. 4) The cart-summary-panel shows subtotal, shippingTotal, total, and currency. 5) If no items are returned or itemCount is zero, the empty-cart-state component is shown instead of the items list.

Change item quantity: 1) User clicks the quantity control (e.g., stepper or input) for a cart line. 2) cart-items-list triggers onQuantityChange with the cartItemId and new quantity value. 3) The quantity-control component validates that the quantity is a positive integer; if valid it calls updateCartItemQuantity(cartItemId, quantity). 4) On a successful response, cart-page-init (or a shared cart state) updates the cart data with the returned cart object. 5) cart-items-list re-renders line items with updated quantities and line subtotals. 6) cart-summary-panel re-renders subtotal, shippingTotal, and total using the updated cart data. 7) If the operation fails (success is false), an error message is shown inline and the quantity reverts to the last known valid value.

Remove an item: 1) User clicks the Remove/trash control for a specific item in cart-items-list. 2) onClickRemove calls removeCartItem(cartItemId). 3) On success, the returned cart object replaces the previous cart state. 4) cart-items-list re-renders, no longer showing the removed item. 5) cart-summary-panel re-renders totals. 6) If the cart becomes empty, empty-cart-state is displayed and the items list is hidden. 7) If the remove operation fails, an error message is shown and the item remains visible.

Review shipping and budget status: 1) On page load, cart-page-init calls getCart to obtain shippingTotal and per-item shipping data. 2) cart-items-list shows per-item shipping indicators using freeShipping and shippingCost. 3) cart-summary-panel displays shippingTotal next to subtotal and total. 4) budget-control reads an optional budget parameter from the cart.html URL (e.g., ?budget={number}). 5) budget-control compares the budget value to cart.total and exposes derived data (difference and over/under flag) to cart-summary-panel. 6) cart-summary-panel shows the user whether the cart is within or over the specified budget and by how much, without relying on hardcoded budget values.

Adjust budget for decision-making: 1) User types a budget amount into the budget input field in budget-control or clears it to remove the constraint. 2) onBudgetInputChange updates the cart.html URL query string with the new budget value (or removes it) using navigation logic; the page state is updated without localStorage. 3) budget-control parses the updated budget from the URL and recalculates the comparison with cart.total. 4) cart-summary-panel re-renders the budget status, allowing the user to see how quantity and removal changes affect staying within the chosen budget.
