Navigate to product details from the cart: 1) User clicks on a product name or thumbnail in cart-items-list. 2) onClickProductLink navigates to product.html?id={productId} where productId comes from the cart item data returned by getCart (or updated cart state). 3) The product detail page can then use that id parameter as needed (outside the scope of this page).

Continue shopping from the cart: 1) User clicks the Continue Shopping control in cart-actions-bar or empty-cart-state. 2) cart-actions-bar reads an optional lastBrowsedCategoryId parameter from the cart.html URL. 3) If lastBrowsedCategoryId is present, the user is navigated to products.html?categoryId={lastBrowsedCategoryId}. 4) If lastBrowsedCategoryId is not present, the user is navigated to products.html without a categoryId parameter, letting that page apply its own default behaviour.

Proceed to checkout: 1) User clicks Proceed to Checkout in cart-summary-panel. 2) onClickCheckout navigates the user to checkout.html (or another defined checkout URL) without calling additional interfaces from this page. 3) The checkout flow itself is handled on separate pages and is out of scope for this specification.
interactions: Page load interaction: When cart.html loads, cart-page-init triggers an initial onPageLoad event, which calls getCart. The response is stored in a client-side state object referenced by other components. Any subsequent updateCartItemQuantity or removeCartItem calls replace this state with the returned cart object so that all components stay in sync.

Quantity change interaction: Changing the quantity field for an item triggers onQuantityChange with the new quantity and the item's cartItemId. If the new quantity is valid, updateCartItemQuantity is called. UI feedback (e.g., disabled controls or loading indicators) can be tied to the pending state of this call. Upon successful completion, the cart state is updated and all bound components re-render.

Item removal interaction: Clicking Remove triggers onClickRemove with the cartItemId. removeCartItem is invoked, and on success, the cart state is replaced with the returned cart, instantly updating line items and totals. If the cart becomes empty, the empty-cart-state component is activated.

Product navigation interaction: Clicking a product thumbnail or name fires onClickProductLink, which generates a URL in the form product.html?id={productId} using the productId from the cart item, and navigates the browser there.

Continue shopping interaction: Clicking Continue Shopping fires onClickContinueShopping. The handler reads lastBrowsedCategoryId from the current cart.html search parameters. If present, it constructs products.html?categoryId={lastBrowsedCategoryId}; if absent, it constructs products.html. The browser is then navigated to that URL.

Budget input interaction: Entering or editing a budget value fires onBudgetInputChange. The handler updates the budget query parameter in the cart.html URL (for example by pushing/updating state in the browser history). budget-control then re-parses the budget parameter and exposes derived data (remaining amount or overage) to cart-summary-panel for display.
Checkout navigation interaction: Clicking Proceed to Checkout fires onClickCheckout, which navigates to a designated checkout URL such as checkout.html without interacting with additional interfaces.

Empty cart interaction: If getCart returns no items or an itemCount of zero, empty-cart-state becomes visible. The user can then click Continue Shopping from that state to navigate to products.html (with an optional categoryId, as described above).
page_functionality.state_logic:
The cart page state is driven by API responses and URL query parameters, without using localStorage. On every load of cart.html, cart-page-init parses URLSearchParams for recognized parameters and then invokes getCart.

URL parameters used on this page:
- budget: Optional numeric parameter representing the user's target budget in the same currency as the cart. When present (e.g., cart.html?budget=1200), budget-control treats its value as the current budget and compares it against cart.total from the latest cart state. If budget is omitted, no budget comparison is displayed. When the user updates the budget via budget-control, the new value is written back to the URL, either adding or updating the budget parameter, or removing it when cleared. This allows sharing or revisiting the same budget-constrained view via the URL.
- lastBrowsedCategoryId: Optional parameter representing the category that the user last browsed on products.html. When present (e.g., cart.html?lastBrowsedCategoryId=home_office), cart-actions-bar and empty-cart-state use this value to construct the Continue Shopping link: products.html?categoryId={lastBrowsedCategoryId}. When absent, Continue Shopping navigates to products.html without additional parameters, leaving category selection to the products page.

The cart contents, including items, subtotal, shippingTotal, total, and currency, are not encoded in the URL. They are always sourced from the latest responses of getCart, updateCartItemQuantity, and removeCartItem. After each successful updateCartItemQuantity or removeCartItem call, the page replaces its in-memory cart state with the cart object returned by the interface and re-renders all components bound to Cart or CartItem. This ensures the URL remains focused on navigational and budget-related state while pricing and items remain authoritative from the backend.

components.functionality: Initializes the cart page by parsing URL parameters, invoking getCart to retrieve the current cart contents and totals, and exposing the resulting cart state to other components. After any successful updateCartItemQuantity or removeCartItem call, this component updates the shared cart state with the returned cart object.

Renders the list of cart items from the current cart state. For each item, displays product thumbnail, product name, categoryName, styleName, unitPrice, quantity, lineSubtotal, and per-item shipping indicators (using freeShipping and shippingCost). Provides user controls to change quantity and to remove the item.

Provides an editable quantity field (e.g., stepper or numeric input) for each cart item. Validates the new quantity (e.g., ensuring it is a positive integer) and, when valid, calls updateCartItemQuantity with the associated cartItemId and new quantity. Handles loading and error states specific to quantity changes.

Displays aggregated pricing information for the current cart, including subtotal, shippingTotal, total, and currency. Integrates with budget-control to show whether the user is within or over a budget (when defined via the budget URL parameter) and by how much. Shows a clear indication of shippingTotal, including when it is zero. Provides a Proceed to Checkout control to navigate to the checkout flow.

Manages the budget-based decision feature. On initialization, reads the budget value from the cart.html URL query string (if present). Provides an input where the user can set or adjust a numeric budget. When the budget changes, updates the URL query parameter and exposes derived values (budget amount, difference between budget and cart total, and whether the cart is over or under budget) for use by cart-summary-panel.

Provides global cart actions such as Continue Shopping. Reads the lastBrowsedCategoryId parameter from the cart.html URL if available and constructs the Continue Shopping URL to products.html with or without the categoryId parameter accordingly. May also include a secondary Proceed to Checkout button that duplicates the action found in cart-summary-panel for convenience.

Displays a dedicated state when the cart has no items (determined by itemCount from getCart or by cart.items length after updates). Shows a message explaining that there are no items in the cart and provides a Continue Shopping control that uses the same navigation logic as cart-actions-bar to direct the user to products.html.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a centered fixed-width layout on a 12-column grid (approx. 1100–1200px content width) with generous outer margins. Content within each major section appears to follow a simple 2-column grid (image vs text) for article listings.
layout.layout_pattern: The cart page follows a single, centered main content column beneath the full-width site header and navigation. Within this column, the primary cart content is grouped into one main "cart card" that uses a 2-column layout: the cart items list on the left and the monetary summary + budget tools stacked on the right. Below this card sits a slim full-width actions bar in the style of the mockup’s meta row. When the cart is empty, the entire main card is replaced by a centered empty state that still respects the same column width and vertical rhythm.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The cart page follows a single, centered main content column beneath the full-width site header and navigation. Within this column, the primary cart content is grouped into one main "cart card" that uses a 2-column layout: the cart items list on the left and the monetary summary + budget tools stacked on the right. Below this card sits a slim full-width actions bar in the style of the mockup’s meta row. When the cart is empty, the entire main card is replaced by a centered empty state that still respects the same column width and vertical rhythm.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content column is constrained to roughly 1100–1200px and centered in the viewport, leaving generous side margins. Within the main cart card, the left column (cart items list) occupies about 60–65% of the card’s width, and the right column (summary + budget) occupies about 35–40%. Major vertical separations—between the page title area, the cart card, and the actions bar—use ~32–48px spacing, often reinforced with a thin horizontal rule. Inside each component, padding follows a 16–24px inset with 8–16px spacing between text lines and controls.
layout_strategies.content_density: The cart mirrors the mockup’s editorial feeling with low information density. Only essential cart information is shown at once: a tidy list of items, a clean summary stack, an optional budget comparison, and a minimal actions bar. Rows and blocks have ample breathing room, with no heavy borders or dense tables; information is separated by white space, thin rules, and aligned typography rather than boxes.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow adopts a mild Z-pattern. The eye starts at the page title at the top-left of the main column, then scans right across any lightweight cart meta (like item count) and back down to the upper-left of the main cart card where the items list begins. From there, attention moves horizontally to the right column where totals and budget information sit. Finally, the eye drops down to the bottom actions bar, reading navigation options from left to right, echoing the mockup’s article meta row with date/category on the left and a key action link on the right.

layout.overall_description: Below the full-width site header and navigation, the cart page content sits in a single, centered column that feels like an editorial spread—lots of white space, thin dividers, and a restrained monochrome palette. At the top of this column is a slim page title row (e.g., “Cart”) with understated typography, followed by a generous vertical gap and a thin horizontal rule that introduces the main cart area. The primary cart content is contained in a wide, low-profile card that fills most of the column width. Inside this card, the layout splits into two columns: on the left, a vertical stack of cart items that each read like a mini editorial listing (thumbnail image on the left, details and quantity controls on the right); on the right, a clean, stacked summary panel with totals at the top and an optional budget comparison module directly beneath it. Both columns share the same top alignment and generous internal padding, producing a calm, balanced feel. Below the card, another subtle horizontal rule spans the content column and introduces a low, slim actions bar where secondary navigation (back to products, update cart) aligns left and the primary checkout action aligns right. If the cart is empty, the two-column card and actions bar disappear, replaced by a centered empty state message and icon that sit in the same column with plentiful white space around them, preserving the page’s minimal, editorial character.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data-loader appears as a minimalist overlay anchored to the main content column, not the full browser window. When active, it sits directly on top of the cart content area, starting just below the site header and any page title row, and extending to cover the full width and height of the central content column (approximately 1100–1200px wide, centered). The overlay is a semi-opaque white or very light tint that subtly mutes the underlying cart items and summary without obscuring the overall page structure. At the center of this column, both horizontally and vertically, a small, unobtrusive loading indicator and a single line of thin, uppercase status text appear, spaced with about 16–24px padding around them to maintain the airy editorial feel. The rest of the page (header/nav and background margins) remains visible but inactive, reinforcing that the loader belongs to the content region rather than the global UI.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an absolute-positioned overlay bound to the main content column; it scrolls with the page but typically covers the immediately visible cart content until loading completes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart items list forms the left column within the main cart card. This card is centered within the page’s main column and uses a 2-column grid inside; the items list takes roughly 60–65% of the card’s width, starting flush from the card’s left inner edge. There is 24px of padding between the card border and the first item row, and the same on the right before the vertical alignment with the summary column. Each item row reads like a horizontal editorial teaser: on the far left, a product thumbnail image occupies about one-third of the row width, aligned vertically centered within the row; to its right, a text-and-controls block occupies the remaining two-thirds. This right block contains the product name at the top, short descriptive text or attributes beneath, and pricing and the quantity-control component aligned toward the lower right. Vertical spacing between rows is generous (24–32px gap with a subtle horizontal rule between items), ensuring that each item feels like a distinct article. The list stacks downward, maintaining a consistent left edge with the card and a clear alignment with the summary column on the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main cart card; scrolls as part of the page. On very narrow viewports, it can collapse to occupy 100% width above the summary stack, maintaining its linear order.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quantity control is an inline, compact element embedded within each row of the cart-items-list, placed in the right side of the item’s details block so it is visually tied to the price. Within an item row, it sits towards the bottom-right corner of the text-and-controls block, horizontally aligned with the item’s line-price text and vertically spaced about 8–12px beneath the descriptive copy. The control itself is a small horizontal group (minus button, quantity value, plus button) occupying roughly one-quarter of the width of the right-side block, with generous internal padding (8px) and thin 1px rules to delineate interactive areas instead of heavy borders. It does not break the editorial feel; it appears as a refined, typographic control, spaced with ample breathing room above and below so it doesn’t crowd the item title or price. Multiple quantity controls in the list align along an invisible vertical axis, creating a clean column of interaction points when several items are present.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline-block element within each cart item row’s right-hand text area; flows with the item layout and does not affect global page structure. On smaller screens, it may stack directly under the price within the same column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart summary panel occupies the right column within the main cart card, aligned to the top edge of the card and directly opposite the first item in the cart-items-list. It takes about 35–40% of the card’s width, starting from the card’s right inner edge and separated from the items list by a comfortable internal gutter of 24–32px. The panel itself is a vertical stack of summary lines—subtotal, shipping estimate, taxes, and grand total—each line separated by 8–12px of vertical space. A thin horizontal rule divides subtotal-related entries from the final total, echoing the mockup’s subtle dividers. The typography is restrained: smaller labels aligned left, numeric values aligned right within the panel’s width. The whole summary block has 16–24px padding on all sides, matching the items list’s top padding, so the top of the first summary line aligns horizontally with the top of the first item title, strengthening the two-column editorial rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block forming the right column of the main cart card. On narrower viewports, it reflows below the cart items list as a full-width block, maintaining its internal vertical stacking and spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The budget control sits directly beneath the cart-summary-panel within the same right-hand column of the main cart card, maintaining the column width and horizontal alignment established by the summary. It is separated from the summary by a modest vertical gap of about 24px and a light horizontal rule that spans only the width of the right column, visually grouping the financial tools while still keeping them distinct. The budget control presents a short label and an input or slider for the target budget, followed by a discreet comparison line (e.g., how current total compares to the budget). Elements are laid out vertically with 8–12px spacing, aligned flush left for labels and flush right for numerical values, echoing the summary alignment above. Overall, it feels like an optional analytical add-on—visibly connected to the summary but visually lighter and slightly de-emphasized through smaller text size or lighter weight.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block stacked immediately below the cart-summary-panel in the right column. On narrow screens where the layout becomes single-column, it appears directly under the summary panel, preserving the same vertical order.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart actions bar appears immediately below the main cart card, separated from it by 32–40px of vertical white space and introduced by a thin horizontal rule that spans the full width of the main content column, matching the mockup’s meta row treatment. The bar itself is a low-height horizontal strip that fills the full content column width but uses minimal vertical padding (around 12–16px) to stay visually light. Within this bar, actions are arranged in a left-right configuration: to the left, secondary navigation links such as “Continue shopping” or “Back to collection” align with the left edge of the main card; to the right, the primary action (e.g., “Proceed to checkout >”) aligns with the right edge of the main card, mirroring the mockup’s right-aligned ‘Read More >’ link. The center remains largely open, preserving white space and emphasizing the linear flow from left (secondary choices) to right (primary path forward).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static full-width bar within the main content column. It scrolls with the page and is not sticky; its position is anchored just beneath the main cart card. On narrow screens, left and right actions may stack vertically while maintaining left alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the cart has no items, the empty-cart-state occupies the same central content column where the main cart card would normally appear, ensuring visual continuity. It is centered within this column both horizontally and, as much as page height allows, vertically in the viewport area beneath the site header. The content consists of a simple icon or illustration at the top, a short title (e.g., “Your cart is empty”) beneath it, and an understated explanatory line followed by a single primary action button (e.g., “Browse furniture”). The group is constrained to a narrow width—about 40–50% of the main column—creating a tall cushion of white space around it that reinforces the minimalist, editorial aesthetic. The primary action is center-aligned under the text, but the overall block still respects the left and right margins of the main column, so it feels like a deliberate, designed empty page rather than a missing component.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the main cart card and actions bar entirely when active; it is a static, centered block within the main content column and scrolls normally with the page.

name: Design Services Overview
title: Design Services
description: Displays available interior design services, allows users to filter and review service details, and provides booking entry points with the selected service prefilled.

core_features: Fetch and display a list of active design services using getDesignServicesOverview.
Allow users to filter services by appointment type (e.g., video_call, in_home, in_studio) via on-page controls and URL parameters.
Allow users to sort services (e.g., by estimated base price or duration) using URL parameters.
Provide a clear "Book a Consultation" action for each service that navigates to booking.html with the corresponding serviceId URL parameter.
Summarize how consultations work by aggregating available appointment types from the loaded services and explaining each type.
Offer navigation links back to the Home page (index.html) and to the Design Packages page (design_packages.html) for users wanting structured offerings.
Support deep-linking and sharable URLs for pre-filtered views or specific highlighted services using URL parameters.
user_workflows:
Browse services and book:
1) User navigates to design_services.html (optionally with URL parameters).
2) Page calls getDesignServicesOverview and loads the list of services.
3) User scrolls through the service list and reviews names, short descriptions, duration, price estimates, and available appointment types.
4) User clicks "Book a Consultation" on a chosen service.
5) Browser navigates to booking.html?serviceId={id-of-selected-service}.

Filter by consultation type:
1) User lands on design_services.html (with or without existing URL parameters).
2) Page loads services via getDesignServicesOverview.
3) User selects an appointment type filter (e.g., video_call) in the filter controls.
4) Component updates the URL to design_services.html?appointmentType={selectedType}[&otherParams].
5) The service list re-renders to show only services whose availableAppointmentTypes include the selected type.
6) User can clear the filter, which removes appointmentType from the URL and restores the full list.

Sort services by price or duration:
1) User arrives on design_services.html and services are loaded via getDesignServicesOverview.
2) User selects a sort option (e.g., basePriceEstimate ascending or durationMinutes descending) in the sort control.
3) Component updates the URL to include sort={sortKey}.
4) The service list re-renders in the new sort order without calling the API again.
5) If user shares the URL, another visitor sees the same sorted view.

Understand how consultations work:
1) User opens design_services.html.
2) Page fetches services via getDesignServicesOverview and aggregates all distinct values in availableAppointmentTypes.
3) Consultation overview component displays a short explanation for each appointment type present in the data (e.g., video_call, in_home, in_studio).
4) User can click within the page (if available) to filter the service list by a specific appointment type from this overview, updating the URL accordingly.

Navigate to structured design packages:
1) User is browsing design_services.html and wants more structured, multi-room or predefined options.
2) User clicks a text link or navigation element labeled for design packages.
3) Browser navigates to design_packages.html (no extra parameters required from this page).

Return to Home:
1) User is on design_services.html.
2) User clicks the site logo or a breadcrumb/home link.
3) Browser navigates to index.html.
interactions: On page load, trigger getDesignServicesOverview, then store the returned array of services in page state and apply any filters/sorts defined by URL parameters.
Click on appointment type filter (e.g., a checkbox, button, or dropdown) updates the appointmentType parameter in the URL and re-filters the in-memory services list.
Change sort order control updates the sort parameter in the URL and re-sorts the in-memory services list.
Click on an individual service card header or dedicated control can toggle visibility of extended service details (within the available fields), without additional API calls.
Click on "Book a Consultation" within a service card navigates to booking.html?serviceId={id} using the id from the selected DesignService item.

Click on a consultation type label in the consultation overview panel (if implemented as a filter shortcut) updates the appointmentType URL parameter and synchronizes the filter state in the filter component.
Click on the "Design Packages" link navigates to design_packages.html.
Click on the logo or breadcrumb "Home" link navigates to index.html.
If filters result in zero matching services, an empty-state message is shown with an option to clear filters (which removes specific URL parameters) and reloads the full list.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is derived entirely from the combination of getDesignServicesOverview results and URL parameters. On each load (or history navigation), the page reads URL query parameters and applies them to the in-memory services list:

- No required URL parameters: design_services.html with no query parameters shows all services returned by getDesignServicesOverview, using a default sort (e.g., by name) defined in the client.

- serviceId: If present (design_services.html?serviceId={id}), the page attempts to locate the corresponding service in the loaded array. If found, the matching service card is programmatically highlighted and optionally scrolled into view. This does not filter out other services; it only affects highlighting. If no service matches the given id, no special highlight is applied.

- appointmentType: If present (design_services.html?appointmentType={typeKey}), the page filters the service list to services where availableAppointmentTypes includes the given typeKey. Valid values correspond to the appointmentType enum in AppointmentSlot and the availableAppointmentTypes entries returned by getDesignServicesOverview (e.g., video_call, in_home, in_studio). If the provided value does not match any available type among the loaded services, the list becomes empty and the empty-state component is shown.

- sort: If present (design_services.html?sort={sortKey}), the page sorts the filtered list client-side based on the chosen criterion. Example sortKey values could include name_asc, price_asc, price_desc, duration_asc, duration_desc. If sort is omitted or invalid, a default sort is applied. Sorting is always applied after filtering.

- multiple parameters: Parameters can be combined (e.g., design_services.html?appointmentType=video_call&sort=price_asc&serviceId={id}). The load sequence is: 1) load all services via getDesignServicesOverview; 2) apply appointmentType filter (if present); 3) apply sort (if present); 4) apply serviceId highlight (if present); 5) render results.

URL updates are performed using standard navigation or history APIs so that any filter or sort change updates the URL. This ensures that reloading the page or sharing the current URL reproduces the same filtered and sorted view with the same highlighted service.

components.functionality: Initializes the Design Services page, calls getDesignServicesOverview once on load, parses URL parameters, maintains the main in-memory collection of services and derived view state (filtered, sorted, highlighted).

Displays interactive controls for filtering the list of services by appointment type and for clearing filters. It reads availableAppointmentTypes from the loaded services to determine which filter options to show, and keeps its UI in sync with the current URL parameters.

Provides a dropdown or button group allowing users to choose how services are ordered (e.g., by basePriceEstimate or durationMinutes). Updates the sort parameter in the URL and triggers re-sorting of the already-loaded services.

Aggregates all distinct appointment types from the availableAppointmentTypes fields of loaded services and presents a brief explanation of how each type of consultation works. Optionally provides clickable labels to apply the corresponding appointmentType filter.

Renders the filtered and sorted collection of design services as a list or grid of cards. Receives an already-processed array (after filtering and sorting) from the page-init component and passes each item into a service-card component. Handles the empty-state when there are no services to show.

Displays a single design service with its name, shortDescription, imageUrl (if available), durationMinutes, basePriceEstimate, and availableAppointmentTypes. Contains the primary "Book a Consultation" button and optional details toggles. Highlights itself if its id matches the serviceId URL parameter.

Shown when the filtered service list is empty (for example, due to an appointmentType value with no matches). Explains that no services match the current filters and offers a control to clear those filters, which removes related URL parameters.
Provides a prominent text link or button that navigates to design_packages.html for users who want structured design packages instead of or in addition to individual services.
Displays a simple breadcrumb path including a link to the Home page and the current Design Services page label. Clicking the home element navigates to index.html.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a centered fixed-width layout on a 12-column grid (approx. 1100–1200px content width) with generous outer margins. Content within each major section appears to follow a simple 2-column grid (image vs text) for article listings.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom editorial flow within a centered fixed-width content column. Immediately under the global site header, the breadcrumb navigation anchors the user’s position. Below it, a single, spacious consultation-types overview acts as the hero/author analogue, introducing the design services. Beneath a separating rule, the filter and sort controls form a slim control band, followed by the main services list where each service is presented as a horizontally split card (image on the left, text on the right) with a meta/CTA row beneath, mirroring the article card pattern from the mockup. An empty state panel replaces the list area when no services match the current filters, while the packages navigation link sits subtly in the control/meta region as a contextual alternative path.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom editorial flow within a centered fixed-width content column. Immediately under the global site header, the breadcrumb navigation anchors the user’s position. Below it, a single, spacious consultation-types overview acts as the hero/author analogue, introducing the design services. Beneath a separating rule, the filter and sort controls form a slim control band, followed by the main services list where each service is presented as a horizontally split card (image on the left, text on the right) with a meta/CTA row beneath, mirroring the article card pattern from the mockup. An empty state panel replaces the list area when no services match the current filters, while the packages navigation link sits subtly in the control/meta region as a contextual alternative path.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content column is centered and constrained to roughly 1100–1200px wide, occupying about 60–70% of the viewport on desktop with generous side margins. The breadcrumb, consultation overview, control band, and service list all align to this same column, creating a clean vertical stack separated by 32–48px of whitespace and occasionally a thin horizontal rule. Within each service card, the left image column takes approximately 35–40% of the width and the right text column 60–65%, maintaining comfortable 16–24px padding inside the card. Vertical spacing within components (e.g., title to summary, summary to meta row) is moderate at 12–20px, preserving the airy editorial tone.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout keeps information density intentionally low, showing only essential elements at each level: a single breadcrumb line, one prominent overview block, a slim control strip, and a vertical stack of well-separated service cards. There are no dense grids or multi-column card mosaics; instead, each service card has ample breathing room, allowing images and typography to stand out. Filters and sort options are restrained in number and visually light, so the services themselves remain the focal content.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow adopts a soft Z-pattern inspired by the mockup. The eye starts at the top-left breadcrumb and moves right along its line, then drops down into the consultation overview copy. From there it travels down to the first service card image on the left (the dominant visual anchor) and then sweeps right across the service title and description. Finally, attention falls to the bottom of each card where a meta row runs left-to-right, from structured details (duration, category, price) to the primary booking or details link aligned to the far right. The filter/sort band above the list echoes this left–right structure, with filters on the left and sorting/secondary navigation (packages link) on the right.

layout.overall_description: The Design Services Overview page reads like a minimalist interior design magazine spread: a centered, fixed-width column floats within generous white space, framed by a monochrome palette and thin horizontal rules. At the top of this column, a slim breadcrumb row quietly orients the user, set flush left with plenty of space above and below. Below it, a single, wide consultation-types overview panel introduces how the studio works, occupying the full column like an editorial intro. After a clear visual break via spacing and a fine rule, an understated control band presents filters on the left and sorting on the right, with a subtle text link to service packages tucked into the right edge. The main body of the page is a vertical stack of service cards, each using a two-column layout: a landscape service image sits on the left as a strong visual anchor, while the right side carries a bold title, short description, and concise details. A slim meta row under each card extends across the width, balancing service metadata on the left with a ‘Book’ or ‘View details’ link on the right, guiding the eye to the primary action. When no services match, a centered empty state panel occupies the list’s space, maintaining the same margins and alignment so the overall composition stays calm and coherent.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data-loader component sits conceptually on top of the main content column, aligned to the same centered, fixed-width container as the rest of the page. When active, it overlays the area from just below the breadcrumb navigation down to the bottom of the service list region, dimming or replacing service cards with lightweight skeleton rows that mimic the left-image/right-text card structure. It should not affect the position of the breadcrumb or global header, preserving the vertical spacing rhythm even while loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a full-width overlay within the main content column, positioned relative to it and scrolling with the page; appears and disappears without shifting underlying layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter controls form the left half of a slim control band directly above the service list, aligned within the centered main column. They are positioned approximately 32–40px below the consultation-types overview (and any separating rule), with their left edge flush to the column’s left edge. The controls—such as dropdowns or pill toggles—are arranged horizontally, starting from the far left and extending toward the middle, but they maintain plenty of breathing room, with 8–16px gaps between individual controls so the strip looks minimal rather than crowded. Vertically, the band is compact, about the height of a single line of text plus 8–12px padding above and below, echoing the mockup’s slim meta row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the normal document flow, forming a horizontal row with the sort controls; scrolls naturally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort controls sit on the same horizontal line as the filter controls but are right-aligned within the main content column, occupying roughly the rightmost third of the control band. They appear about 32–40px below the consultation-types overview and in visual line with the top of the filters, so the entire band reads as one continuous rule-like strip. Typically rendered as a single dropdown or a minimal segmented control, they hug the right edge with 16–24px inset from the column boundary, leaving clear negative space between the rightmost filter and the leftmost sort element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline with the filter controls, right-aligned within the same flex row; scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The consultation-types overview is placed near the top of the main content column, directly beneath the breadcrumb navigation with a generous vertical gap of about 32–48px, mirroring the author block’s position in the mockup. It spans the full width of the central column and reads as a single editorial panel: optionally, a small icon or minimal graphic can appear on the left, with the section heading and descriptive paragraphs to its right, but all within one cohesive block rather than a hard split. Inside, the text is left-aligned with 16–24px padding around the content and 12–16px between heading, subheading, and body copy. The overall height is modest but taller than the control band and breadcrumb, clearly establishing it as the main introductory content for the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level component centered in the main content column; scrolls with the page without sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-list component occupies the full width of the centered main column and begins approximately 24–32px below the filter/sort control band, often separated by a thin horizontal rule running the width of the column. It is a purely vertical stack of service-card components, each card separated from the next by a generous 32–40px of whitespace to maintain the low-density editorial feel. The list’s left and right edges align perfectly with the rest of the column, so all card images and text start and end on the same vertical grid lines. The list’s vertical extent flexes with content, forming the main scrollable body of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a vertical container for service-card items within the main column; scrolls naturally and expands with content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each service-card fills the width of the main content column and uses a two-column horizontal split mirroring the article card from the mockup. The left side is a landscape-oriented image area, occupying roughly 35–40% of the card’s width, aligned flush with the column’s left edge and maintaining a fixed aspect ratio for visual consistency. The right side, taking about 60–65% of the width, holds the textual content: the service title at the top in a slightly larger, bold type; beneath it a concise description paragraph; and optionally a short note about scope or audience. Inside the card, there is about 16–24px padding from the card edges to the content, with 12–16px vertical spacing between title, description, and any secondary text. Directly below this main body, separated by a subtle horizontal rule, runs a slim meta row extending the full width of the card: on the left, key details such as duration, price, and category are laid out in a single line or compact cluster; on the far right, aligned with the card’s right edge, is the primary action link or button (e.g., “Book consultation >” or “View details >”). The entire card sits on a white or near-white background with a thin outline or a soft shadow kept very subtle to maintain the minimalist editorial aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the service-list, using an internal two-column grid for image and text; no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-state-message appears in place of the service-list when filters return no results, occupying the same horizontal space as the list and aligning with the main content column. It is centered both horizontally and visually within that region, with its content (icon, title, and supporting text) stacked vertically and padded generously—24–32px inside the panel and at least 40–56px above it from the control band—to create a moment of calm. The message block spans roughly 60–70% of the column width, leaving comfortable margins on both sides, and is kept visually light with simple typography and perhaps a single thin rule or outline, echoing the editorial minimalism of the rest of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the service-list area conditionally; centered within the main column and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The packages-navigation-link is positioned in close proximity to the sort controls, forming part of the same right-aligned control zone above the service list. It typically appears as a subtle inline text link (e.g., “View design packages >”) either directly to the right of the sort dropdown or just beneath it, right-aligned and separated by 4–8px vertical spacing to keep the band visually compact. Its placement mirrors the ‘Read More >’ link pattern from the mockup’s meta row, drawing the eye to the far right as an alternative navigation path while remaining visually lighter than primary actions within the service cards.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the control band, right-aligned in the same zone as the sort controls; scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb-navigation sits at the very top of the main content column, directly beneath the global site header. It is a single, slim horizontal line of text aligned to the left edge of the column, spanning only as wide as needed for the breadcrumb labels, with ample white space above (about 24–32px from the global header) and below (about 32–40px to the consultation-types overview). The typography is understated and compact, echoing the mockup’s slim navigation feel, and no heavy background or container is used, allowing the breadcrumb to float lightly in the whitespace while still clearly anchoring the user’s location.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard inline navigation row within the main column, fixed in place only by document flow; scrolls away as the user moves down the page.

name: Service Booking
title: Service Booking
description: Page for selecting a design consultation appointment (service, appointment type, date, time) and submitting contact details to book the service.

core_features: Load and display details of the selected design service using the serviceId URL parameter and the getDesignServiceDetail interface.
Allow users to confirm (and, if needed, change via navigation) the design service being booked, with the currently selected service shown in a dropdown-style control.

Allow users to select an appointment type (e.g., video, in-home, in-studio) from the options returned by getDesignServiceDetail.availableAppointmentTypes, optionally preselected via the appointmentType URL parameter.
Provide a date picker that lets users navigate across weeks (forward and backward within allowed constraints) and choose a specific date for the consultation.
Fetch and display available appointment time slots for the chosen service, appointment type, and date via getAppointmentSlotsForDate, and allow the user to select a single slot.
Collect contact details (name, email, phone, notes/project details) in a booking form, validating required fields before submission.
Submit a booking request for the selected slot via bookServiceAppointment and show an on-page booking confirmation with reference details returned by the interface.
Provide navigation back to the service overview (design_services.html) via a clear link or breadcrumb.
Persist key selections (serviceId, appointmentType, date, selected slot) in the URL as parameters so that the booking state can be reloaded or shared without using localStorage.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Start booking from a specific service
1. User clicks a "Book a Consultation" link on design_services.html or index.html, which opens booking.html with a serviceId URL parameter (and optionally appointmentType).
2. On page load, the page-state-manager reads serviceId and appointmentType from the URL.
3. service-loader calls getDesignServiceDetail with serviceId and shares the result with dependent components.
4. service-selector displays the service in a dropdown-style control so the user can confirm they are booking the correct service.
5. appointment-type-selector determines the initially selected appointment type: it uses the appointmentType URL parameter if present and contained in DesignService.availableAppointmentTypes; otherwise it falls back to a default (for example, the first available type in the list).
6. date-picker initializes its selected date: if a date URL parameter is present and valid, it uses that; otherwise it uses the current date (or the next available date according to business rules).
7. Once serviceId, appointmentType, and date are all set, slot-loader (within time-slot-list) calls getAppointmentSlotsForDate with these values.
8. time-slot-list displays the available slots. The user clicks a slot to select it; this updates the selected slot state and sets a slotId URL parameter.
9. booking-summary-panel shows the combined selection: service name, appointment type, date, and selected time slot.
10. The user fills in name, email, phone, and notes in contact-details-form. Required fields are validated on blur and on submit.
11. On submit, if a slot is selected and required contact fields are valid, contact-details-form calls bookServiceAppointment with appointmentSlotId, appointmentType, customerName, customerEmail, customerPhone, and notes.
12. On success, booking-confirmation-panel displays confirmation details using the returned appointment object. page-state-manager updates the URL with a status parameter (e.g., status=requested or status=confirmed) and the appointmentId.
13. The user can click a link in booking-confirmation-panel to go back to design_services.html or stay on the page and review the confirmation.

Workflow 2: Change appointment type during booking
1. The user arrives on booking.html with serviceId set, and the page loads the service and default appointment type.
2. After seeing the initially loaded slots for a given date, the user uses appointment-type-selector to choose a different appointment type from the availableAppointmentTypes array.
3. appointment-type-selector updates the appointmentType URL parameter and clears any previously selected slotId from the URL.
4. page-state-manager observes the change, and slot-loader calls getAppointmentSlotsForDate again with the updated appointmentType and the current date.
5. time-slot-list refreshes with the new set of slots for the new appointment type; booking-summary-panel updates to reflect the new type, and the user selects a new time slot.
6. The user proceeds to submit contact-details-form as in Workflow 1.

Workflow 3: Change date and reload available slots
1. The user has chosen a service and appointment type; time-slot-list shows slots for the currently selected date.
2. The user clicks navigation controls in date-picker to move to a different day or week (e.g., next week) and selects a new date.
3. date-picker updates the date URL parameter and clears any previously selected slotId.
4. slot-loader calls getAppointmentSlotsForDate with serviceId, current appointmentType, and the newly selected date.
5. time-slot-list shows available slots for the new date; booking-summary-panel updates the date, and the user selects a slot and continues to booking.

Workflow 4: Reload/return to an in-progress booking via URL
1. A user or staff member opens a URL to booking.html that includes serviceId and some combination of appointmentType, date, and slotId parameters.
2. On load, page-state-manager parses all parameters and initializes internal state.
3. service-loader retrieves the service via getDesignServiceDetail and passes it to dependent components.
4. appointment-type-selector validates appointmentType against availableAppointmentTypes. If valid, it uses it; if not, it selects a default and updates the URL.
5. date-picker validates the date parameter. If valid, it uses it; if not, it chooses the current or next available date and updates the URL.
6. slot-loader calls getAppointmentSlotsForDate for the resolved serviceId, appointmentType, and date, then time-slot-list marks the slot matching slotId as selected if it exists and is available.
7. booking-summary-panel shows the restored selection so the user can complete the contact details and submit.

Workflow 5: Post-booking review and navigation
1. After completing bookServiceAppointment, booking-confirmation-panel displays the confirmation message and details returned by the API.
2. page-state-manager sets or updates URL parameters to reflect the confirmed appointment (e.g., appointmentId and status).
3. If the user reloads the page with these parameters, the page can display the confirmation state again instead of the empty form, or prefill a summary section while still allowing the form to be visible in read-only or disabled mode, depending on requirements.
4. The user may click the 'Back to Services' or breadcrumb link, which navigates to design_services.html without additional parameters, allowing them to choose another service to book.
interactions: On initial page load, page-state-manager reads serviceId and appointmentType from the URL. If serviceId is missing, the page displays an error message component and a link back to design_services.html instead of the booking form.

On service data load, service-loader calls getDesignServiceDetail(serviceId). On success, it populates service-selector, appointment-type-selector (with availableAppointmentTypes), booking-summary-panel, and any service-related hints such as durationMinutes and basePriceEstimate. On failure, it shows an error message and disables booking actions.

On appointment type selection or change in appointment-type-selector, the component validates the selection against DesignService.availableAppointmentTypes, updates the appointmentType URL parameter, clears any previously selected slotId from the URL, and triggers a refresh of available slots for the currently selected date.

On date change via date-picker (either by selecting a specific day or using previous/next week navigation controls), the component updates the selected date state, writes it to the date URL parameter, clears any selected slotId, and triggers slot-loader to call getAppointmentSlotsForDate with the new date.

On loading appointment slots for the current service, appointment type, and date, slot-loader calls getAppointmentSlotsForDate(serviceId, appointmentType, date). While loading, time-slot-list shows a loading state; on success, it displays all slots where isAvailable is true; on failure or if no slots are returned, it displays appropriate status messages and disables the booking form submit button.

On time slot selection in time-slot-list, the user clicks one of the available slots. The component sets that slot as selected, stores its slotId in internal state and in the slotId URL parameter, and passes the slot details to booking-summary-panel and contact-details-form for validation and display.

On any change in service, appointment type, date, or slot, booking-summary-panel updates in real time to reflect the current selection, including formatted date-time using the slot's startDateTime and timeLabel.

On contact form input in contact-details-form, the component updates local form state for customerName, customerEmail, customerPhone, and notes. Required fields (customerName, customerEmail, and a selected slot) are validated on blur and on submit; invalid fields show inline error messages and prevent submission.

On contact form submission via contact-details-form, if validation passes and a slot is selected, the form invokes bookServiceAppointment with appointmentSlotId (from selected slot), appointmentType (current selection), customerName, customerEmail, customerPhone, and notes. The submit button shows a loading state and is disabled until the request completes.

On successful booking from bookServiceAppointment, booking-confirmation-panel displays confirmationMessage and any returned details (appointmentId, status, serviceName, appointmentType, startDateTime). page-state-manager updates the URL with appointmentId and status, and contact-details-form may be reset or disabled to avoid duplicate submissions.

On booking error from bookServiceAppointment, contact-details-form clears the loading state, shows an error message (with a retry or change-selection hint), and leaves the form filled so the user can retry after adjusting details or choosing another slot.

On click of the 'Back to Services' or breadcrumb link in breadcrumb-navigation, the page navigates to design_services.html (optionally preserving no additional parameters), allowing users to choose another service. The booking.html page does not call any additional interfaces during this navigation.

On changes to URL parameters due to user actions (serviceId, appointmentType, date, slotId, appointmentId, status), page-state-manager listens to changes (e.g., via history API and popstate) and re-synchronizes component state, ensuring the UI always reflects the URL without using localStorage.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is fully derived from URL parameters and live API data, without using localStorage or other persistent client-side storage. The primary URL parameters are: (1) serviceId (required for booking): used by service-loader to call getDesignServiceDetail. If missing or invalid, the booking UI is replaced with an error and a link back to design_services.html. (2) appointmentType (optional): if present, appointment-type-selector attempts to match it against DesignService.availableAppointmentTypes. If it matches, it is used as the initial appointment type; if not, appointment-type-selector chooses a default available type and updates the appointmentType parameter in the URL accordingly. (3) date (optional): represents the currently selected appointment date, formatted according to the requirements of getAppointmentSlotsForDate (e.g., an ISO 8601-compatible date string). If absent or invalid, date-picker initializes the date to an appropriate default (such as the current date or the earliest bookable date) and writes that value back into the URL. (4) slotId (optional): represents the id of a selected AppointmentSlot. After getAppointmentSlotsForDate returns, time-slot-list attempts to find a slot with this id and mark it selected if it is still available. If not found, the selection is cleared and slotId is removed from the URL. (5) appointmentId and status (optional, post-booking): after bookServiceAppointment returns, booking-confirmation-panel can set appointmentId and status in the URL. When the page loads and finds these parameters, it can show the confirmation panel immediately or treat the page as being in a 'post-booking' state. All components subscribe to changes in these URL parameters through page-state-manager, which acts as the single source of truth for client-side state. Whenever a user action modifies booking-relevant state (changing appointment type, changing date, selecting a slot, or a successful booking), the corresponding parameter(s) in the URL are updated using the browser history API so that navigation and refresh correctly restore the booking state.

components.functionality: Central controller that reads booking-related URL parameters on load, maintains an in-memory representation of the current booking state (serviceId, appointmentType, date, slotId, appointmentId, status), and updates the URL whenever this state changes. It also notifies other components when relevant state fragments change so they can reload data (e.g., slots) or update their display.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: parse URLSearchParams and initialize internal state, then emit events to service-loader, appointment-type-selector, date-picker, and time-slot-list.
onUrlChange: handle browser navigation events (e.g., popstate) to re-sync state and notify dependent components.
onStateChange: when any component updates booking state (appointmentType, date, slotId, status), update the corresponding URL parameter using the history API.

components.functionality: Loads detailed information for the selected design service using serviceId from page-state-manager and getDesignServiceDetail. Provides the DesignService object to other components such as service-selector, appointment-type-selector, booking-summary-panel, and breadcrumb-navigation.
event_handlers: onServiceIdAvailable: call getDesignServiceDetail(serviceId) and handle loading, success, and error states.
onLoadSuccess: cache and broadcast the DesignService data to dependent components.
onLoadError: notify error-display components and signal that booking cannot proceed until a valid serviceId is provided.

components.functionality: Displays the currently selected design service in a dropdown-like control so the user can verify which service they are booking. Since the page only has the details of a single service (from getDesignServiceDetail), the dropdown is effectively a single-option selector showing the service name. It also exposes a control or link to navigate back to design_services.html to choose a different service.
event_handlers: onServiceDataChange: update the displayed service option label and value when DesignService data is loaded.
onChangeServiceClick: navigate to design_services.html so the user can choose another service (no API calls from this component).

components.functionality: Lets the user select the appointment type (e.g., video call, in-home, in-studio) from the list of types provided by DesignService.availableAppointmentTypes. It uses the appointmentType URL parameter to determine the initial selection if valid. When the user changes the selection, it updates the URL parameter and triggers a reload of available slots for the current date.
event_handlers: onServiceDataChange: read availableAppointmentTypes, validate the existing appointmentType URL value against them, and set the initial selection. If the URL value is invalid or missing, select a default type and update the appointmentType URL parameter via page-state-manager.

onAppointmentTypeChange: when the user selects a different type, update the internal state, notify page-state-manager to write the new appointmentType into the URL and clear slotId, and trigger time-slot-list to refresh slots via getAppointmentSlotsForDate.

components.functionality: Provides calendar-like controls to choose the desired appointment date. The user can navigate across weeks (forward/back) and select a specific day. It initializes its value from the date URL parameter when possible; otherwise it chooses a suitable default date. On date change, it updates the date URL parameter and triggers slot reloading.
event_handlers: onInit: parse the date URL parameter. If valid according to the expected format, set it as the selected date; otherwise, select a default date (e.g., today or the earliest available bookable date) and push it to the URL via page-state-manager.
onDaySelect: when the user clicks a specific date, set it as the selected date, update the date URL parameter, clear any existing slotId parameter, and notify time-slot-list to reload slots.
onNavigateWeek: when the user uses previous/next week controls, adjust the currently displayed calendar range. If the user selects a new day in that range, handle it via onDaySelect.

components.functionality: Responsible for fetching available appointment slots for the current service, appointment type, and selected date. Calls getAppointmentSlotsForDate and passes the results to time-slot-list.
event_handlers: onDependenciesChange: when serviceId, appointmentType, or date changes (as notified by page-state-manager), call getAppointmentSlotsForDate(serviceId, appointmentType, date).
onLoadSuccess: supply the returned array of slots to time-slot-list and clear any stale error messages.
onLoadError: signal to time-slot-list and contact-details-form that no slots are available due to an error, and provide error details for display.

components.functionality: Displays the list of available appointment slots for the selected date and appointment type. Shows loading and empty states appropriately. Allows the user to select exactly one available slot, and synchronizes the selected slot with the slotId URL parameter and dependent components such as booking-summary-panel and contact-details-form.
event_handlers: onSlotsDataChange: when slot-loader provides a new list of slots, render them, then check if the current slotId URL parameter corresponds to any isAvailable slot. If it does, mark that slot as selected; otherwise, clear selection and remove slotId from the URL via page-state-manager.

onSlotClick: when the user clicks on an available slot, set that slot as selected, update the slotId URL parameter through page-state-manager, and notify booking-summary-panel and contact-details-form.
onNoSlots: if the slots array is empty or contains no isAvailable slots, show an informational message and signal contact-details-form to disable submission.

components.functionality: Shows a concise summary of the current booking selection: service name, appointment type, selected date, and selected time slot. It updates dynamically as the user changes any of these inputs, and it uses the AppointmentSlot.startDateTime and timeLabel values to show human-readable date and time.
event_handlers: onSelectionChange: when service details, appointmentType, date, or selected slot changes, recompute and display the summary text, including formatted date-time and duration if durationMinutes is available.
onBookingConfirmed: when booking-confirmation-panel notifies that a booking succeeded, optionally switch to a read-only state where the summary reflects the confirmed appointment.

components.functionality: Collects user contact information and project notes required to book the appointment. Validates that a time slot is selected and that required fields (customerName and customerEmail) are filled and correctly formatted before submitting a booking request. Calls bookServiceAppointment and handles success and error states.
event_handlers: onInputChange: update internal state for customerName, customerEmail, customerPhone, and notes whenever the user types.
onBlurValidate: on leaving each required field, validate its value (e.g., non-empty and properly structured email) and show inline error messages if invalid.

onPreSubmitValidate: when the user clicks submit, check that a slot is selected (slotId present and corresponds to a current available slot), that customerName and customerEmail are valid, and that there are no blocking validation errors; if any issues exist, prevent submission and focus the first invalid field.

onSubmit: if validation passes, call bookServiceAppointment with appointmentSlotId (from the selected slot), the current appointmentType, and the form's contact details. Show a loading state on the submit button and disable the form until a response is received.

onSubmitSuccess: receive the object returned by bookServiceAppointment, which includes appointmentId, status, serviceName, appointmentType, startDateTime, and confirmationMessage. Notify booking-confirmation-panel with this data, clear or disable the form to prevent duplicate bookings, and ask page-state-manager to update appointmentId and status in the URL.
onSubmitError: if bookServiceAppointment fails, display an error message, reset the loading state, and keep the form inputs so the user can correct information or select another slot and try again.

components.functionality: Displays booking confirmation details after a successful call to bookServiceAppointment. Shows the confirmationMessage and key appointment details (serviceName, appointmentType, startDateTime, appointmentId, status). It also provides clear actions such as staying on the page to review the confirmation or returning to design_services.html.
event_handlers: onBookingSuccess: when contact-details-form reports a successful booking, display the confirmation panel with data from the API response and update internal state to reflect that the booking is complete.

onInitFromUrl: if appointmentId and status parameters exist on page load (e.g., the user returns via a shared link), show the confirmation panel immediately, using whatever appointment data is available in the current session state.
onBackToServicesClick: navigate the user to design_services.html so they can browse or book another service.

components.functionality: Provides a minimal breadcrumb or back-link navigation element, allowing the user to return to design_services.html from the booking page. It may include the name of the current service for context.
event_handlers: onServiceDataChange: update the breadcrumb label to include the loaded service name.
onBreadcrumbClick: navigate to design_services.html without modifying any server data or calling additional interfaces.

components.functionality: Shows contextual error and information messages related to loading service details, loading appointment slots, form validation, or booking submission errors. It receives error signals from other components and presents them in a consistent way.
event_handlers: onServiceLoadError: show a message indicating that the service could not be loaded and offer a link back to design_services.html.
onSlotsLoadError: show a message indicating that slots could not be retrieved for the selected date and appointment type and suggest trying a different date or type.
onFormValidationError: display summaries of form validation issues received from contact-details-form.
onBookingError: display an error banner when bookServiceAppointment fails, with guidance to retry or adjust selections.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a centered fixed-width layout on a 12-column grid (approx. 1100–1200px content width) with generous outer margins. Content within each major section appears to follow a simple 2-column grid (image vs text) for article listings.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a vertical, editorial-style stack: a full-width site header (outside the listed components), then a slim breadcrumb row, followed by an optional error strip, then a full-width booking summary panel (acting like the 'author block' in the mockup). Below that sits the primary booking 'card' as a two-column layout: left column for selecting service, appointment type, date, and time slot; right column for entering contact details. Finally, a thin horizontal rule spans the content width, followed by a confirmation panel that echoes the mockup's bottom meta row. All components are centered within a fixed-width content column on a 12-column grid.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a vertical, editorial-style stack: a full-width site header (outside the listed components), then a slim breadcrumb row, followed by an optional error strip, then a full-width booking summary panel (acting like the 'author block' in the mockup). Below that sits the primary booking 'card' as a two-column layout: left column for selecting service, appointment type, date, and time slot; right column for entering contact details. Finally, a thin horizontal rule spans the content width, followed by a confirmation panel that echoes the mockup's bottom meta row. All components are centered within a fixed-width content column on a 12-column grid.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content column is centered and constrained to about 1100–1200px, occupying roughly 60–70% of a typical desktop viewport with generous lateral white space. Vertically, major sections (breadcrumb, summary panel, booking card, confirmation panel) are separated by 32–48px. Within the booking card, the two columns split the width asymmetrically: the left selection column takes about 35–40% of the content width; the right contact form column takes about 60–65%. Internal component padding is around 16–24px, and vertical spacing between related fields within a component is 8–16px.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout keeps information density low, surfacing only the essential steps at each stage. The top of the page shows at most breadcrumb, a subtle error (if needed), and a concise booking summary, with ample white space between them. The booking card focuses on a single flow—selecting a service and time on the left while filling in contact details on the right—without additional decorative content. The confirmation area at the bottom remains hidden until needed, maintaining a calm, spacious editorial feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a gentle Z-pattern inspired by the mockup. The eye starts at the top-left with the site logo (outside these components) and moves right across the global navigation, then drops down to the breadcrumb at the top-left of the content column and gently scans right. From there it flows down into the full-width booking summary panel. Next, the gaze is pulled down to the left column of the booking card (service and date selection as the strong functional anchor), then travels horizontally to the right column (contact details). Finally, the eye drops to the thin rule and the confirmation panel below, reading left-to-right across the final booking status information.

layout.overall_description: The Service Booking page appears as a restrained, editorial layout centered in the browser, with a fixed-width content column floating within generous white margins. Beneath the slim site header and navigation, a single-line breadcrumb quietly orients the user. A subtle error strip may appear below it when needed, but otherwise the space remains clean. Next, a full-width booking summary panel spans the content column, resembling a magazine-style author block: a compact icon or service marker on the left and crisp text summarizing the consultation on the right. Below this, the main booking card dominates the page: a horizontally split two-column layout where the left 35–40% is a vertical stack of selectors for service, appointment type, date, and time slots, and the right 60–65% is a calm, structured contact details form. The entire card is framed by generous white space and thin typographic hierarchy instead of heavy borders. A fine horizontal rule then runs across the page, creating a clear visual break. Under this rule, a full-width confirmation panel appears once the booking is submitted, echoing the mockup’s meta row by providing concise booking status and key details. Invisible state and data loader components orchestrate loading and transitions without adding visual clutter. Overall, the page feels like a refined design studio booking form presented as an editorial feature rather than a typical transactional interface.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-state-manager is a non-visual controller that conceptually sits at the root of the main content column. It does not occupy any visible space on the page and introduces no margins or padding of its own. Instead, it governs which visible components (such as the booking card or confirmation panel) are shown at any given time. From a layout perspective, it behaves like an invisible wrapper around the booking area—from the breadcrumb down to the confirmation panel—without altering the centered, fixed-width editorial layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered structural element; behaves as an invisible parent container with no visual footprint or effect on spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-loader is tied to the area where the service-selector appears. When loading, it temporarily occupies the left column region at the top of the booking card, spanning approximately 35–40% of the content width and extending just enough vertically to center a slim loading indicator within that column. The loader’s horizontal position aligns with the left edge of the main content column, respecting the same left indent as the service-selector. Once data is loaded, the loader disappears, yielding its footprint entirely to the service-selector without affecting the 2-column structure or the generous white space around it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears inline in place of the service-selector, matching its column width; otherwise non-visible and non-impacting.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-selector sits at the top of the left column within the main booking card, starting roughly 32–40px below the bottom edge of the booking-summary-panel. It occupies about 35–40% of the content column width, aligned flush with the left content margin. Vertically, it is treated like the first module in a columnar stack, with 16–24px padding inside its boundaries and comfortable 12–16px vertical spacing between its label, dropdown, and any helper text. The right edge of the service-selector aligns with the vertical gutter dividing the left and right columns, reinforcing the editorial grid. Ample blank space surrounds it on the top and bottom so it reads as a clear starting point for the booking flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the left column of the main booking card; its height grows with content but it remains anchored to the top of that column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The appointment-type-selector is placed directly below the service-selector in the left column of the booking card, maintaining the same width (35–40% of the content column) and horizontal alignment. There is a vertical gap of about 24px between the bottom of the service-selector and the top of the appointment-type-selector, creating a clear separation between these steps while preserving a clean vertical rhythm. Internal padding mirrors the service-selector (around 16–24px) with 8–12px between its label and control. Visually, it feels like the second block in a stack of editorial modules aligned along a single left column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element stacked beneath the service-selector inside the left column, expanding vertically as needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The date-picker continues the vertical stack in the left column of the booking card, sitting below the appointment-type-selector with approximately 24–32px of vertical separation. It aligns exactly with the left and right edges of the components above, preserving a strong column edge. If the date-picker includes a calendar grid, the grid fills most of this column’s width but leaves a slender inner margin on both sides for breathing room, echoing the thin editorial style. The overall block (label plus calendar) reads as a concise, self-contained module that does not bleed into the right column, maintaining the 2-column hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block occupying the same left column as the selectors above; its height may be larger due to calendar content but it remains vertically stacked with consistent spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The slot-loader is associated with the region where the time-slot-list appears. When active, it sits directly beneath the date-picker, occupying the same width and horizontal alignment as the other left column modules. It uses just enough vertical height to center a subtle loading indicator in that area, generally matching the expected height of a compact list of time slots so that layout shifts are minimized. When loading completes, the slot-loader disappears and the time-slot-list is revealed in its place, preserving the established spacing between the date-picker above and the next element below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily occupies the footprint of the time-slot-list; otherwise non-visible and does not alter the two-column structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The time-slot-list sits at the bottom of the left column stack within the booking card, directly below the date-picker (or slot-loader when loading) with about 24px of vertical separation. It maintains the same left and right alignment as the previous components in this column and fills most of the column width. Individual time slots are arranged vertically as a lightly spaced list, with about 8–12px between each row so the list stays readable and airy. If the list grows taller than the right column content, it can extend downward, with the overall card height increasing accordingly while preserving the clean columnar split.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically expanding list in the left column; may scroll internally if very long, but remains anchored in place relative to the other left-column components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-summary-panel is positioned as a full-width band within the main content column, directly below any error-display and above the main booking card. It spans nearly 100% of the fixed content width (about 1100–1200px) and sits approximately 32–40px below the breadcrumb-navigation. Inside this band, content is arranged left-to-right in a way that mirrors an author info block: a compact square or circular visual marker (e.g., an icon or small service thumbnail) at the far left, taking roughly 10–15% of the panel’s width, and to its right a vertical stack of text summarizing the booking context (title, selected service, duration, etc.) occupying the remaining 85–90% of the width. Padding of around 24px on all sides creates a generous cushion of white space.
