The bottom of the panel sits about 32–40px above the top of the two-column booking card, often separated by a subtle horizontal rule or simply by the generous gap, giving the summary panel strong prominence as the introduction to the booking flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed in the vertical stack between breadcrumb (and errors) and the booking card; it does not scroll independently but moves with the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-details-form anchors the right column of the main booking card. It begins horizontally aligned with the top of the service-selector on the left, with the vertical dividing line between columns positioned so the form occupies roughly 60–65% of the content width. The form is left-aligned within its column, with 24px padding from the column’s top edge and side margins. Fields are arranged in a simple single-column vertical layout, with 12–16px between label and input, and 16–20px between different form fields, maintaining a light, editorial rhythm. The right edge of the form aligns with the right edge of the main content column, reinforcing the centered, fixed-width feel. The bottom of the form roughly lines up with or slightly extends below the bottom of the left column’s time-slot-list, creating a cohesive rectangular booking card that feels balanced but not rigid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element occupying the right column of the booking card; it expands downward as more fields are included, influencing the total card height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-confirmation-panel appears below the main booking card and serves as the bottom meta row, analogous to the date/category line in the mockup but with more content. It is separated from the booking card by a thin horizontal rule and approximately 24–32px of vertical space. The panel spans the full content width, aligned with the same left and right margins as the rest of the content column. Within the panel, content is laid out in a single row on larger screens: key confirmation details (e.g., booked date/time, service name) are placed on the left two-thirds, starting flush with the content’s left margin, while a concise status message or secondary action (such as a link to manage the booking) aligns to the right one-third. The panel maintains generous internal padding (around 16–24px) and sits with plenty of white space below it before the page footer, ensuring the final state feels calm and considered rather than crowded. When not in use, the panel is hidden, allowing the page to end cleanly at the bottom of the booking card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the vertical flow that only becomes visible after a booking attempt; it scrolls with the rest of the page and does not overlay other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb-navigation is the first component within the main content column, positioned just below the global site header and navigation. It is left-aligned within the centered content column, occupying the full internal width but with a very low visual height—typically a single line of small, light-weight text. There is approximately 32–40px of white space between the bottom of the global header and the top of the breadcrumb, and another 16–24px between the breadcrumb and any error-display or the booking-summary-panel below. This placement mirrors the subtle top text line in the mockup and gently introduces the page context without drawing too much attention away from the main booking flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline text row at the top of the content stack; it does not scroll independently or stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error-display occupies a slim band across the full width of the main content column and sits directly below the breadcrumb-navigation. It appears only when there is a global or form-level error to communicate. Horizontally, it aligns perfectly with the content column’s left and right edges, preserving the centered layout. Vertically, it uses modest height—enough for one or two lines of text—with 12–16px padding on all sides to keep the message legible yet unobtrusive. There is roughly 16–24px of vertical space between the bottom of this error band and the top of the booking-summary-panel, so that the error feels related to the current flow but does not visually merge with the summary content. When no errors are present, this band collapses entirely and does not occupy any space, keeping the layout crisp.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered block in the vertical stack; appears and disappears without affecting the relative ordering of other sections, simply closing the gap when hidden.

name: Design Packages Overview
title: Design Packages Overview
description: Displays available interior design packages, allows basic filtering and comparison, and links to detailed package configuration.

core_features: Fetch and display a list of active design packages using getDesignPackagesOverview, including each package's name, roomType, shortDescription, imageUrl, and basePriceEstimate.
Group and/or filter packages by roomType to help users quickly find relevant packages for a specific room type.
Highlight packages that match an incoming roomType URL parameter to guide users arriving from other pages.
Allow users to select one or more packages for side‑by‑side comparison using the fields available from getDesignPackagesOverview.
Provide a clear navigation action (e.g., “View Details” / “Choose Package”) on each package to navigate to package_detail.html with the corresponding packageId URL parameter.
Maintain filter and comparison selections in the URL (via query parameters) so the state can be preserved on reload, back/forward navigation, or when sharing the URL.
Handle loading, empty, and error states when fetching data from getDesignPackagesOverview.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Landing with a specific roomType
1. User arrives on design_packages.html with a URL parameter roomType set (e.g., design_packages.html?roomType=<value>). 
2. The page controller reads the roomType parameter and calls getDesignPackagesOverview without parameters to retrieve the full list of active design packages. 
3. Once data is loaded, the roomType filter component sets its initial selection based on the URL roomType, if that value exists in the returned data. 
4. The package list displays all active packages, visually highlighting those whose roomType matches the URL parameter. 
5. The user may adjust filters or comparison selections; the URL is updated accordingly without reloading the page.

Workflow 2: Filtering by room type from within the page
1. User lands on design_packages.html with no roomType parameter. 
2. The page controller calls getDesignPackagesOverview without parameters and stores the returned array of design packages. 
3. The roomType filter component analyzes the loaded list and derives available roomType values from the data. 
4. The user selects a roomType option in the filter component. 
5. The filter component updates the URL parameter roomType to the selected value (e.g., design_packages.html?roomType=<selectedRoomType>) using history.pushState or history.replaceState. 
6. The page controller reacts to the URL change and updates the visible subset of packages (e.g., by filtering or by changing highlight state). No additional API call is required because the full dataset is already in memory. 
7. If the user clears the filter, the roomType parameter is removed from the URL, and the list returns to an unfiltered view.

Workflow 3: Selecting packages for comparison
1. After packages are loaded and displayed, the user sees a selection control (e.g., a checkbox or toggle) on each package summary card to mark it for comparison. 
2. When the user selects a package for comparison, the comparison component adds the packageId to an internal selected list and updates a compare URL parameter containing a comma‑separated list of packageIds (e.g., design_packages.html?compare=<id1,id2>). Existing parameters, such as roomType, are preserved. 
3. The comparison panel becomes visible (or updates) to show selected packages in a structured layout, using only the data available from getDesignPackagesOverview (e.g., name, roomType, shortDescription, basePriceEstimate). 
4. If the user deselects a package from comparison, the comparison component removes that packageId from its internal list and updates the compare parameter accordingly. 
5. If the compare list becomes empty, the comparison panel hides and the compare parameter is removed from the URL.

Workflow 4: Navigating to package details
1. User identifies a suitable package in the overview list. 
2. User clicks the package’s action button (e.g., “View Details” or “Choose Package”) on the package summary card. 
3. The click handler reads the package’s id from the bound DesignPackage data. 
4. The browser navigates to package_detail.html with a packageId URL parameter (e.g., package_detail.html?packageId=<id>). 
5. The package detail page (out of scope here) uses the packageId parameter for its own logic.

Workflow 5: Returning to home via breadcrumb
1. User is on design_packages.html, possibly with filters or comparison selections applied. 
2. The breadcrumb navigation component displays a parent link representing the home page. 
3. The user clicks the breadcrumb link. 
4. The browser navigates to index.html, without preserving design_packages.html‑specific state, as the home page does not rely on those parameters.

Workflow 6: Restoring state from a shared URL
1. A user shares a URL such as design_packages.html?roomType=<value>&compare=<id1,id2>. 
2. Another user opens this URL. 
3. On page load, the controller reads roomType and compare from the URL. 
4. The controller calls getDesignPackagesOverview without parameters, receives the package list, and passes it to child components. 
5. The filter component applies the roomType selection based on the URL if it matches any roomType in the dataset. 
6. The comparison component parses the compare parameter and selects any packages whose ids appear in the returned dataset, displaying them in the comparison panel. Invalid or missing ids are ignored.
interactions:
Interaction: Page load
- Trigger: Browser loads design_packages.html. 
- Behavior: The page controller parses the current URL for roomType and compare parameters, then calls getDesignPackagesOverview (without parameters). While awaiting the response, a loading state is exposed to dependent components. Once data is returned, inactive packages (isActive=false) are filtered out, and initial filter/comparison state is derived from URL parameters.

Interaction: Room type filter selection change
- Trigger: User selects or changes a roomType option in the roomType filter component. 
- Behavior: The component updates internal state to reflect the selected roomType and updates the URL parameter roomType. The page controller or the filter component then recalculates the visible subset of packages or the highlighting state based on the new selection. No additional API call is necessary, as the full dataset is already available.

Interaction: Clearing room type filter
- Trigger: User activates a control to clear the roomType filter (e.g., selecting a neutral option that represents no specific room type). 
- Behavior: The component removes the roomType parameter from the URL and resets any filter-specific state. All active packages become visible/unhighlighted according to the default view.

Interaction: Selecting a package for comparison
- Trigger: User toggles the comparison control on a package card. 
- Behavior: If the package is newly selected, its id is added to the comparison selection list. The compare URL parameter is updated to include this id, preserving other parameters. The comparison panel displays the newly selected package alongside others already selected.

Interaction: Deselecting a package from comparison
- Trigger: User toggles the comparison control on a selected package card, or removes it from within the comparison panel. 
- Behavior: The package’s id is removed from the comparison selection list. The compare URL parameter is updated to remove this id. If no selected packages remain, the comparison panel hides and the compare parameter is removed from the URL.

Interaction: Clicking package action (View Details / Choose Package)
- Trigger: User clicks the package’s main action button on a summary card. 
- Behavior: The click handler reads the package’s id from the bound package data and navigates to package_detail.html?packageId=<id> using a standard browser navigation (e.g., window.location). No API call is made on click.

Interaction: Breadcrumb navigation to Home
- Trigger: User clicks the breadcrumb link representing the home page. 
- Behavior: The browser navigates to index.html. The current page’s URL parameters are not carried over, as the home page does not depend on them.

Interaction: Sorting packages
- Trigger: User selects a sort option (such as sorting by basePriceEstimate or by name) in a sorting control. 
- Behavior: The sorting component updates an internal sort state and reorders the in-memory list of packages before rendering. No additional API calls are made, and URL parameters may include a sort indicator if desired (e.g., sort=<criteria>) to preserve the sort order on reload.

Interaction: Handling API error or empty result
- Trigger: getDesignPackagesOverview returns an error or an empty array of active packages. 
- Behavior: The controller sets an error or empty state flag. A state indicator component displays a corresponding message and may offer a retry action (which re‑calls getDesignPackagesOverview).
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page relies on URL query parameters to manage and restore state; no localStorage or similar browser storage mechanisms are used. The primary URL parameters are: 

- roomType (string, optional): 
  - Source: Can be provided by index.html or design_services.html when linking to this page, or modified in-place by the roomType filter component. 
  - Purpose: Indicates the currently selected room type filter or highlight. On initial load, the controller reads roomType and passes it to the filter component as the initial selection. After data load, visible packages or their highlight state are updated to reflect this selection. When a user changes the roomType filter, the component updates this parameter using history.pushState or history.replaceState. If the filter is cleared, roomType is removed from the URL. 
  - Effect on data loading: The controller calls getDesignPackagesOverview without including roomType so the page always has the complete list of active packages and can support cross-room comparisons. roomType affects only client-side filtering and highlighting, not the API request. 

- compare (string, optional): 
  - Format: A comma-separated list of package ids, e.g., compare=id1,id2,id3. 
  - Source: Managed exclusively by the comparison selector component. 
  - Purpose: Represents the set of packages currently selected for comparison. On page load, after getDesignPackagesOverview resolves, the comparison component parses compare, identifies packages whose ids match the loaded data, and marks them as selected; other ids are ignored. When the user selects or deselects packages, the component updates the compare parameter to reflect the current selection. If the selection becomes empty, the compare parameter is removed. 

- Optional sort parameter (string, optional, if implemented): 
  - Example format: sort=name_asc or sort=price_desc (actual values are not hardcoded in this specification). 
  - Source: Managed by the sort control component. 
  - Purpose: Indicates how the package list is currently sorted. On load, the controller or sort component can read this parameter and apply the corresponding sort to the in-memory package list. When the user changes sort criteria, the parameter is updated in the URL. Sorting is applied client-side only; getDesignPackagesOverview is not re-called for sorting. 

State transitions: 
- Initial load: The controller reads the current URL, extracts roomType, compare, and optional sort, then calls getDesignPackagesOverview without parameters. Once the data is available, it applies filter/highlight logic based on roomType, comparison selection based on compare, and sorting based on the sort parameter. 
- URL updates: Whenever the roomType, compare, or sort parameters change (via user interactions), the components responsible update the URL using history APIs. The controller may respond to these changes either by listening for popstate events (for back/forward navigation) or by a centralized state management pattern, ensuring that in-memory state stays synchronized with URL parameters. 
- Navigation away: Navigating to other pages (e.g., index.html or package_detail.html) uses standard URLs with the required parameters (e.g., packageId) for those pages; the state of design_packages.html is encoded fully in its URL and thus restorable when returning to the page.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading and overall state management for the Design Packages Overview page. On page load, it parses URL parameters (roomType, compare, optional sort), calls getDesignPackagesOverview to fetch all active design packages, filters out inactive entries, and passes the resulting dataset and derived state (selected roomType, selected comparison package ids, sort order) to child components. It also handles loading, error, and empty states and listens for URL changes (e.g., browser back/forward) to keep UI state synchronized with URL parameters.

Displays navigational breadcrumbs for the page, including a link back to the home page and a non-clickable label for the current Design Packages Overview page. The home breadcrumb links to index.html.

Provides controls to select or clear a roomType filter. It derives available roomType options from the unique roomType values present in the loaded DesignPackage list. On initialization, it reads the initial roomType value passed by the controller (derived from the URL). When the user selects a roomType option, it updates the internal selection state and updates the roomType URL parameter. When the user clears the filter, it removes the roomType parameter from the URL.

Allows the user to change the ordering of displayed packages (e.g., by name or by basePriceEstimate). It exposes the selected sort criteria to the controller or list component. It can also read and write an optional sort URL parameter to preserve the chosen sort order across reloads and shared URLs, without calling the API again.

Renders the list of design packages returned by the controller, applying current filter, highlight, and sort states. It receives the full dataset and a subset of ids to highlight (e.g., those matching the selected roomType). It also receives the set of packageIds currently selected for comparison to visually indicate which items are in the comparison set.

Represents a single design package within the list. Displays fields returned by getDesignPackagesOverview (such as name, roomType, shortDescription, imageUrl, basePriceEstimate) and reflects highlight state (e.g., when its roomType matches the selected filter). Includes two key controls: a main action button to navigate to package_detail.html for that package, and a comparison toggle to add/remove the package from the comparison selection.

Maintains the set of packageIds selected for comparison. It listens to compare toggle events from package-summary-card components and updates an internal selection list. It synchronizes this selection to the URL via the compare parameter (as a comma-separated list of ids). On initialization, it reads the compare parameter from the URL and marks matching packages as selected. It also notifies the comparison panel when the selection changes.

Displays a side-by-side comparison of the currently selected packages. It receives the list of selected packageIds from the comparison selector and the full list of DesignPackage entities from the controller, then constructs a structured comparison view using only fields available from getDesignPackagesOverview (e.g., name, roomType, shortDescription, basePriceEstimate). Provides controls to remove packages from the comparison (which delegates to the comparison selector) and hides itself when no packages are selected.

Displays feedback for loading, error, and empty states. While getDesignPackagesOverview is pending, it shows a loading indicator. If the API call fails, it shows an error state and can expose a retry control that triggers the controller to re-call getDesignPackagesOverview. If the API returns no active design packages, it shows an empty-state message indicating that there are no packages to display.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a centered fixed-width layout on a 12-column grid (approx. 1100–1200px content width) with generous outer margins. Content within each major section appears to follow a simple 2-column grid (image vs text) for article listings.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a single centered content column beneath the global site header. Inside this column, components follow a linear, top‑to‑bottom stack: breadcrumb navigation at the top; a concise page header area (handled by the page controller) that establishes context; then a slim, horizontal filter/sort band; directly below that, a light state indicator line; followed by a vertical list of package summary cards, each using a left image / right text split reminiscent of the article card in the mockup. At the bottom of the content stack, a comparison selector strip and a comparison panel sit as the final section, echoing the mockup’s meta row as a subtle, horizontally organized control zone.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a single centered content column beneath the global site header. Inside this column, components follow a linear, top‑to‑bottom stack: breadcrumb navigation at the top; a concise page header area (handled by the page controller) that establishes context; then a slim, horizontal filter/sort band; directly below that, a light state indicator line; followed by a vertical list of package summary cards, each using a left image / right text split reminiscent of the article card in the mockup. At the bottom of the content stack, a comparison selector strip and a comparison panel sit as the final section, echoing the mockup’s meta row as a subtle, horizontally organized control zone.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content sits in a centered, fixed-width container (~1100–1200px) that occupies roughly 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, with generous left/right margins. The breadcrumb, filters, sort control, state indicator, list, and comparison components all align to this same width. Within each package summary card, the image on the left takes approximately 35–40% of the card width, and the text column on the right uses 60–65%. Vertical spacing between major sections (breadcrumb block, filter/sort band, list, comparison area) is generous (around 32–48px), while spacing inside each component (e.g., title to description inside cards, controls inside filters) is tighter but still roomy (12–20px).
layout_strategies.content_density: Overall density is deliberately low and editorial: few elements per viewport, lots of white space, and no heavy visual chrome. Controls (filters, sort, comparison) are visually slim and text‑forward, allowing imagery and package titles to carry most of the visual weight. The list shows one primary card fully and parts of the next, rather than a dense grid, to preserve the airy, magazine‑like feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the top left on the breadcrumb and page title, scans right across any auxiliary header copy, then drops to the filter/sort band, reading from left (filters) to right (sort). From there, attention is drawn downward to the first package image on the left, then sweeps horizontally to the package title and summary on the right. The gaze then moves down to each card’s meta row (pricing, key attributes, subtle CTAs), and finally to the bottom comparison selector and panel, scanning from selected‑items information on the left to the 'Compare' action on the right. This creates a mild Z‑pattern repeated per card and concluded at the comparison area.

layout.overall_description: The Design Packages Overview page presents as a centered, minimalist editorial layout beneath the site’s full‑width header. A slim breadcrumb line sits at the top of the content column, followed by a restrained page header block with the page title and a single‑sentence description, all surrounded by generous white space. A thin horizontal rule then introduces a compact filter/sort strip: on the left, room‑type filtering options; on the right, a subtle sort dropdown, both expressed with light typography and minimal borders. Directly below, a light state indicator line quietly reports how many packages are shown. The main body is a vertical sequence of large package summary cards, each stretching the full content width. Within each card, a wide landscape image anchors the left third of the card, while the right two‑thirds hold the package name, a short descriptive paragraph, and key highlights, breathing within ample internal padding. At each card’s bottom, a slim meta row mirrors the mockup’s date/category bar, balancing price and essentials on the left with a 'View Details >' link on the right. After the list, a low‑profile comparison selector strip spans the column, leading into an understated comparison panel that appears as a refined table when active. Thin rules, consistent spacing, and a monochrome palette tie everything together into a calm, design‑studio aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller defines a centered, fixed-width content column (max-width ~1120–1200px) aligned to the middle of the viewport, starting just below the global site header. It adds generous top and bottom padding (about 48px from the header and 48px before the footer) to create the airy editorial feel. All other components (breadcrumb, filters, list, comparison) are laid out vertically inside this column, following a strict top-to-bottom flow. The controller also reserves a shallow horizontal band near the top of the column for the page title 'Design Packages Overview' and a one- or two-line description, positioned between the breadcrumb nav above and the filter/sort section below, separated by thin horizontal rules and 24–32px vertical gaps.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static container; defines the central grid and vertical rhythm but does not scroll independently from the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb navigation sits at the very top of the main content column, flush with the left edge of the centered container, occupying the full width of that column but remaining visually lightweight. It uses a single line of small, thin type with minimal vertical height and 8–12px padding above and below. There is approximately 16–24px of whitespace separating it from the page title area below, optionally reinforced by a subtle horizontal rule that spans the full column width. The breadcrumb aligns visually with the left column of content that follows, helping anchor the user’s starting point in the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolls with the page; always appears at the top of the content stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The room-type filter bar is positioned below the page title/description block and a thin horizontal rule, forming the left side of a shared control row with the sort control. It starts at the left edge of the main content column and extends roughly 60–65% of that width. Filter controls (e.g., pills or dropdowns) are arranged horizontally with modest spacing (8–16px) between them, maintaining a low visual profile through light borders or underlines rather than heavy filled buttons. There is 16–24px of vertical spacing above and below the filter row, ensuring it reads as a slim editorial band rather than a bulky toolbar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; aligned on a single row with the sort control on larger screens, stacking above it on smaller viewports while preserving vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control shares its row with the room-type filter bar but is right-aligned within the main content column. It occupies approximately 20–25% of the column width, appearing as a single, discreet dropdown or segmented control with a label such as 'Sort by'. Its baseline aligns with the filter controls on the left, creating a clear left–right tension similar to the logo vs navigation alignment in the mockup’s header. The area above and below matches the filter’s vertical spacing (16–24px), and the entire row is framed by subtle horizontal rules (one above, one optional below) to visually separate it from the list of packages.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; right-aligned within the shared control row, wrapping below the filter bar in narrower viewports while maintaining alignment with the right edge of the content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The state indicator appears directly beneath the filter/sort row, occupying the full width of the central column but with a very low visual footprint. It presents as a single line of small, muted text (e.g., 'Showing 6 of 12 packages' or 'No packages match these filters'), aligned to the left, with approximately 8–12px of vertical padding. It may be accompanied by a thin horizontal rule immediately below when transitioning into the package list, echoing the mockup’s use of slim lines between sections. The overall height is minimal so that it reads as a supporting informational strip, not a primary section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; always located directly above the packages list, updating content but retaining the same spatial footprint.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The packages list forms the main vertical body of the page and starts immediately beneath the state indicator and its separating rule. It occupies the full width of the central content column and stacks package summary cards in a single column, one after another. Each card is separated from the next by a generous vertical gap of roughly 32–40px, with thin horizontal rules optionally running full width between cards to reinforce separation while preserving the minimalist feel. Only one full card (and part of the next) is visible per screen height, maintaining the low-density, editorial rhythm. The left edge of the cards aligns perfectly with the left edge of the breadcrumb and filter bar, reinforcing the central grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical stack; the list scrolls as part of the page, with no independent scrolling container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each package summary card is a horizontally split block spanning the full width of the main content column, with 16–24px of internal padding on all sides and either a very light border or a subtle bottom rule to define its boundary. The card height adjusts to its content but is visually anchored by a landscape image on the left occupying about 35–40% of the card width. This image maintains a wide, cinematic aspect ratio (e.g., 16:9) and aligns top with the text column. To the right, the text column takes up roughly 60–65% of the width, starting with the package name set in a larger, bold but refined type. Below the title, there is 8–16px of vertical space, then a 2–3 line summary paragraph, followed by 8–12px spacing before any key bullet points or highlight line (e.g., 'Includes furniture selection, layout, and styling'). At the bottom of the card, a slim meta row spans from left to right, separated from the body copy by 12–16px of spacing and a light horizontal rule. In that meta row, price and key attributes (e.g., duration, room types covered) sit on the left, aligned with the text column, while the primary inline CTA 'View Details >' is right-aligned, mimicking the mockup’s date/category vs 'Read More >' pattern. The overall density within the card is low, with meaningful white space around each text block and no heavy buttons, maintaining the magazine-like presentation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the list; each card maintains its left–image/right–text layout down to medium screen widths, then gracefully stacks the image above the text with preserved vertical spacing on small screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison selector is placed immediately after the final package card in the list, with a generous 32–40px vertical gap and a thin horizontal rule separating it from the list above. It spans the full width of the main content column but has a slim vertical profile. Inside, the left side shows a small line of text indicating how many packages have been selected for comparison (e.g., '2 packages selected'), while the right side offers a subtle text-based action 'Clear selection' or similar, plus a minimally styled 'Compare >' link or button aligned to the far right. The arrangement echoes the meta row structure: informational content on the left, a key action on the right. The spacing within the selector is moderate (8–12px vertical padding), ensuring it feels like a refined control strip rather than a bulky banner.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in document flow; when no packages are selected, it can visually downplay itself (lighter text, possibly collapsed height) but stays in the same location at the bottom of the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison panel sits directly beneath the comparison selector, occupying the same full column width. When inactive or empty, it may appear as a collapsed, very thin bar or remain hidden, preserving the space and allowing the selector to be the primary element. When active (with 2+ packages selected), it expands into a structured but visually light comparison table: package names across the top as columns and attributes down the left as rows. The panel uses a grid of thin horizontal and vertical rules to define cells, with ample cell padding (12–16px) and a lot of negative space, avoiding heavy shading or thick borders. The top row of the panel aligns its column headers with the left/right edges of the central content. There is 24–32px padding at the top and bottom of the panel to separate it from surrounding content. On desktop, once expanded and when the user scrolls past it, the panel may adopt a subtle sticky behavior near the bottom of the viewport, remaining pinned with only its content scrolling within the page, to keep comparisons accessible without overwhelming the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Primarily static in the document flow, but when expanded it can optionally become sticky to the bottom of the viewport on larger screens, maintaining its alignment with the centered content column; on smaller screens it remains fully inline and scrolls normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The state indicator appears directly beneath the filter/sort row, occupying the full width of the central column but with a very low visual footprint. It presents as a single line of small, muted text (e.g., 'Showing 6 of 12 packages' or 'No packages match these filters'), aligned to the left, with approximately 8–12px of vertical padding. It may be accompanied by a thin horizontal rule immediately below when transitioning into the package list, echoing the mockup’s use of slim lines between sections. The overall height is minimal so that it reads as a supporting informational strip, not a primary section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; always located directly above the packages list, updating content but retaining the same spatial footprint.

name: Package Detail & Quote Request
title: Package Detail & Quote Request
description: Page for viewing a specific design package, configuring options, and submitting a quote request based on the configuration.

core_features: Load full details for a specific design package using the packageId URL parameter and the getDesignPackageDetail interface.
Display package information including name, room type, descriptions, image, and base price estimate returned by getDesignPackageDetail.
Display and manage configuration controls for room size, color palette, and tier using the options returned by getDesignPackageDetail.
Dynamically calculate and display an indicative price or scope summary based on the selected tier option and the package basePriceEstimate.
Allow the user to trigger the quote request flow via a primary "Request a Quote" action that scrolls or reveals the quote request form.
Collect user details (name, email, message, preferred contact method) and the current configuration to submit a quote request via submitPackageQuoteRequest.
Handle quote submission responses by displaying a confirmation message using the data returned by submitPackageQuoteRequest.
Provide navigation back to the package list (design_packages.html) with the current package’s roomType propagated as a roomType URL parameter.
user_workflows:
View package and configure options:
1. User arrives on package_detail.html with a packageId URL parameter.
2. On page load, the system calls getDesignPackageDetail with packageId.
3. When the response arrives, the page displays the package’s main information and configuration options (room sizes, color palettes, tiers).
4. Default selections are determined based on URL parameters (if present) or the first option in each returned list.
5. As the user selects a room size option, color palette option, and tier option, the current configuration state is updated.
6. The indicative price/summary display updates when the tier selection changes, using the package basePriceEstimate and the selected tier’s indicativePriceAdjustment.

Request a quote based on current configuration:
1. After setting their preferred configuration, the user clicks the main "Request a Quote" button.
2. The page scrolls to or reveals the quote request form, with the current configuration summarized near the form.
3. The user fills in required fields: customer name, customer email, and preferred contact method, and optionally enters a message describing their project.
4. The user submits the form; the page triggers submitPackageQuoteRequest with:
   - packageId from the URL / loaded package,
   - roomSizeOptionId, colorPaletteOptionId, and tierOptionId from the current configuration state,
   - customerName, customerEmail, message, and preferredContactMethod from the form.
5. While the request is in progress, the submit button is disabled and a pending state is shown.
6. On success, the page displays the confirmationMessage returned by submitPackageQuoteRequest and may show a non-editable summary of the submitted configuration and status.
7. On error, the page shows an error message and keeps the form and current configuration intact so the user can retry.

Change configuration before or after opening the quote form:
1. The user may adjust configuration options (room size, color palette, tier) at any time before submitting the quote.
2. If the user changes configuration after the quote form is visible but before submission, the configuration summary near the form updates immediately.
3. When the user submits, the most recent configuration values are used in submitPackageQuoteRequest, regardless of when the form was opened.

Navigate back to design package list:
1. The user clicks a breadcrumb link or "Back to Packages" link on the page.
2. The page reads the current package’s roomType from the loaded package data.
3. The user is navigated to design_packages.html with roomType set as a URL parameter, so the list page can pre-filter or highlight relevant packages.
interactions:
Initial load:
- Event: Page load with URL containing packageId.
- Interaction: Component responsible for loading data triggers getDesignPackageDetail(packageId).
- Result: Page-level state is populated with package, roomSizeOptions, colorPaletteOptions, and tierOptions; configuration controls are initialized.

Missing or invalid packageId:
- Event: Page load without a packageId parameter or with an invalid value.
- Interaction: Loader component detects missing/invalid packageId and shows a functional error state (e.g., message and a link back to design_packages.html).
- Result: No call to getDesignPackageDetail is made in this case, or error handling is shown if the API signals an error.

Selecting room size option:
- Event: User selects a room size option in the configuration panel.
- Interaction: onChange handler updates current roomSizeOptionId in the page’s state and, optionally, in the URL query string.
- Result: Configuration summary updates to reflect the selection.

Selecting color palette option:
- Event: User selects a color palette option (e.g., via radio button or card selection).
- Interaction: onChange handler updates current colorPaletteOptionId in page state and URL (if used).
- Result: Configuration summary and any palette-related descriptions update accordingly.

Selecting tier option (affecting indicative price):
- Event: User selects a tier option.
- Interaction: onChange handler updates current tierOptionId; any client-side logic recalculates indicative price by combining the loaded basePriceEstimate with the selected tier option’s indicativePriceAdjustment.
- Result: Indicative price display and scope summary update immediately.

Click "Request a Quote" button:
- Event: User clicks primary call-to-action button.
- Interaction: onClick handler scrolls to or reveals the quote request form section and focuses the first input field.
- Result: User is guided to complete the quote request form with current configuration shown.

Fill and submit quote request form:
- Event: User enters customerName, customerEmail, message, and preferredContactMethod and submits the form.
- Interaction: onSubmit handler validates required fields client-side, then calls submitPackageQuoteRequest using current configuration and form data.
- Result: During submission, show loading state; on success, display confirmation; on failure, display error feedback.

View confirmation and optionally keep editing:
- Event: submitPackageQuoteRequest returns successfully.
- Interaction: Confirmation component receives quoteRequestId, status, and confirmationMessage and displays them.
- Result: User sees a clear confirmation. Form fields may reset or remain read-only depending on design; configuration controls remain available if a new quote is desired (using a new submission).

Back to packages navigation:
- Event: User clicks breadcrumb or "Back to Packages" link.
- Interaction: onClick handler reads current package.roomType and constructs a URL to design_packages.html with roomType as a URL parameter.
- Result: Browser navigates to design_packages.html?roomType=<roomType>.
page_functionality.state_logic:
Primary state is driven by URL parameters and API responses.

URL parameters:
- packageId (required):
  - Determines which design package to load via getDesignPackageDetail.
  - On change (e.g., if the user manually edits the URL), the page reloads package details and configuration options.

Optional URL parameters for configuration (recommended):
- roomSizeOptionId (optional):
  - If present, after getDesignPackageDetail returns, the configuration panel attempts to match this value to one of the returned roomSizeOptions.id.
  - If a match is found, that option is selected initially; otherwise, a default selection strategy is used (e.g., the first option in the list).
- colorPaletteOptionId (optional):
  - Handled similarly to roomSizeOptionId; used to preselect a color palette.
- tierOptionId (optional):
  - Handled similarly; used to preselect a tier, which directly affects indicative price.

Configuration state and URL synchronization:
- When the user changes any configuration option, the page updates the in-memory state for the current configuration.
- Optionally, the page updates the corresponding URL parameters (roomSizeOptionId, colorPaletteOptionId, tierOptionId) using pushState/replaceState without full reload, so that:
  - The current configuration is shareable via URL.
  - Reloading or revisiting the URL restores the same configuration.

Form state:
- Quote form values (customerName, customerEmail, message, preferredContactMethod) are maintained in component state and are not required to be placed in the URL.
- Form validation errors and submission status (idle, submitting, success, error) are also managed in component state.

API-driven state:
- getDesignPackageDetail response is the single source of truth for:
  - package basic info and basePriceEstimate.
  - available roomSizeOptions, colorPaletteOptions, and tierOptions.
- submitPackageQuoteRequest response is the source of truth for:
  - quoteRequestId.
  - status (e.g., submitted, in_review, etc., as returned).
  - confirmationMessage.

Error states:
- If getDesignPackageDetail fails (e.g., network error or invalid packageId), the page shows an error message and may offer navigation back to design_packages.html.
- If submitPackageQuoteRequest fails, the form remains visible with user input preserved, allowing retry.

components.functionality: Reads packageId from the URL, calls getDesignPackageDetail, and provides the loaded package and configuration options to child components.
Displays package name, room type, short description, and an image, plus a back navigation control to return to the package list.
Shows the long description and any additional descriptive fields of the current design package.
Provides interactive controls for the user to select room size, color palette, and tier options for the current package.
Displays an indicative price or scope summary based on the package basePriceEstimate and the currently selected tier’s indicativePriceAdjustment.
Shows a compact summary of the current configuration (selected room size, color palette, and tier) that updates as the user changes options and is also reused near the quote form.
Primary button that initiates the quote request flow by scrolling or revealing the quote request form section.
Collects user information and submits a quote request for the currently configured package using submitPackageQuoteRequest.
event_handlers: onSubmit:validateFormFields;submitPackageQuoteRequest({packageId, roomSizeOptionId, colorPaletteOptionId, tierOptionId, customerName, customerEmail, message, preferredContactMethod});setFormStatus('submitting')

components.functionality: Displays the result of a successful quote submission, including the confirmation message and status returned by submitPackageQuoteRequest.
Shows an error message when getDesignPackageDetail fails or packageId is invalid, and offers navigation back to the package list.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a centered fixed-width layout on a 12-column grid (approx. 1100–1200px content width) with generous outer margins. Content within each major section appears to follow a simple 2-column grid (image vs text) for article listings.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom, single-column layout centered within the viewport, mirroring the editorial pattern from the mockup. At the top sits the package header, followed by an editorial-style description block. Below that, the main functional 'card' is a horizontal split: configuration panel on the left and price estimate on the right, using a similar proportion to the mockup’s image/text split. Under this card, a thin rule separates a compact meta row containing the configuration summary on the left and the quote request trigger on the right, echoing the date/category vs 'Read More >' pattern. The quote request form and any status/confirmation messaging stack beneath these primary sections. Loader and error states occupy the same central column, either replacing or overlaying the core content as needed.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom, single-column layout centered within the viewport, mirroring the editorial pattern from the mockup. At the top sits the package header, followed by an editorial-style description block. Below that, the main functional 'card' is a horizontal split: configuration panel on the left and price estimate on the right, using a similar proportion to the mockup’s image/text split. Under this card, a thin rule separates a compact meta row containing the configuration summary on the left and the quote request trigger on the right, echoing the date/category vs 'Read More >' pattern. The quote request form and any status/confirmation messaging stack beneath these primary sections. Loader and error states occupy the same central column, either replacing or overlaying the core content as needed.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The content column is centered and constrained to roughly 1100–1200px width (about 60–70% of typical desktop viewports), with generous left/right margins. Major sections—header, description, configuration/price card, summary/CTA row, form, and status banners—are separated by 32–48px of vertical space, often reinforced with thin horizontal rules. Within the main configuration card, the configuration panel occupies about 60–65% of the horizontal space on the left, while the price estimate panel takes 35–40% on the right, closely following the mockup’s card proportion. Internal padding within components stays around 16–24px, with 8–16px between text lines and form fields to keep the layout airy.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout favors low information density, with ample white space around each block and limited concurrent elements on screen. Each major component (header, description, config/price card, meta row, form) reads as a distinct editorial section, with clear separation and minimal ornamentation. Forms and configuration controls are grouped into short, scannable sections with breathing room, avoiding dense e-commerce-style control clusters. The monochrome, minimalist aesthetic and thin dividing rules emphasize clarity and hierarchy rather than volume.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a gentle Z-pattern inspired by the mockup. The eye begins at the package title and header area at the top-left, moves right across any supplementary header metadata, then drops down to the start of the configuration card. Within the card, the eye is first anchored by the left-aligned configuration controls, then moves across to the right-hand price estimate panel. From there, it drops to the thin rule and meta row below, scanning from the configuration summary on the left to the prominent quote request trigger on the far right. If the quote form is expanded, the eye continues down through a clean, single-column form and any confirmation or status messages at the end.

layout.overall_description: The page appears as a centered, editorial-style detail view inside a fixed-width column, reminiscent of a design magazine rather than a typical product configurator. At the top, a slim, typographic package header introduces the design package with plenty of white space above and below. Beneath it, a refined description section reads like an article intro, framed by a thin horizontal rule that gently anchors the content. The main functional area is a horizontal split card: on the left, a spacious configuration panel where options are adjusted; on the right, a compact but visually strong price estimate panel, echoing the mockup’s image-versus-text layout. A subtle horizontal divider then introduces a meta row where a concise configuration summary sits on the left, balanced by a right-aligned quote request button that behaves like an editorial ‘Read More >’ link. When activated, the quote request form unfolds below as a full-width, airy form with generous spacing and minimal visual noise, concluding in a clean confirmation banner. Error and loading states sit within the same central column, preserving the minimal, monochrome, editorial aesthetic throughout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears centered within the main content column, occupying roughly the middle third of the viewport height. It aligns with the same fixed-width, centered column as the rest of the page, maintaining side margins identical to the package header. Visually, it takes the form of 2–3 horizontal skeleton bars that mimic the future positions of the header and the split configuration/price card: a wider bar near the top suggesting the title area, then a wide bar on the left and a shorter bar on the right to indicate the upcoming two-column card. There is ample white space above and below the loader, with no additional framing elements, so it feels light and non-intrusive within the clean editorial layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Centered within the normal document flow while loading; optionally can appear as a semi-opaque overlay above the existing content, but still constrained to the main content column and not full-bleed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The package header is the first substantive block within the main centered column, starting approximately 48px below the global site navigation. It spans the full width of the content column (about 1100–1200px) but remains relatively shallow vertically to match the mockup’s slim header. The package name is left-aligned and visually dominant, occupying the top-left, with any supporting metadata (e.g., category, estimated duration, starting price label) aligned horizontally to the right in lighter-weight text, echoing the logo-plus-navigation flow. Internal padding of roughly 24px top and bottom gives the header breathing room. A thin, light horizontal rule sits 32–40px below the header, clearly separating it from the subsequent description section while preserving the minimalist editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the normal document flow at the top of the page’s main content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Directly beneath the header’s horizontal rule, the package description section spans the full width of the main column but reads as a compact editorial block. The content is left-aligned, with a section label or subtle heading (e.g., “About this package”) at the top, followed by a short paragraph or two of description. To keep the airy, magazine-like style, the text lines do not stretch edge-to-edge; instead, the paragraph width visually feels like about 70–80% of the content width, centered within the column, leaving comfortable side margins. Vertical spacing within this section is modest: around 12–16px between heading and paragraphs, and 24–32px of padding above and below the entire block. Another thin horizontal rule sits 32–40px below the description, introducing the main configuration/price card and reinforcing the section break.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section that occupies the full content width and pushes subsequent sections downward in normal flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The configuration panel forms the left side of the main functional 'card' immediately below the description section’s rule. The card itself is centered in the main column and uses a two-column split: the configuration panel occupies approximately 60–65% of the card’s width on the left. It has uniform internal padding of about 24px on all sides and is visually framed by the card’s subtle outline or implied by contrast against the background, keeping the look minimal. Within the panel, configuration controls are arranged in a vertical stack, grouped into logical subsections with 16–24px spacing between groups and 8–12px between individual fields, echoing the text grouping in the mockup’s article content. The top of the configuration panel aligns horizontally with the top of the price estimate panel on the right, reinforcing the editorial card structure and the left-to-right scan of the user’s eye.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as the left column of a split card within the main content column. On narrower viewports, this panel stacks above the price estimate panel, becoming a full-width block while maintaining the same internal padding and spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The price estimate panel sits immediately to the right of the configuration panel as part of the same unified card. It occupies about 35–40% of the card’s width, echoing the mockup’s image column proportion. The panel shares the same vertical height and top alignment as the configuration panel, giving the card a cohesive horizontal silhouette. Inside, key pricing information—estimated total, range, and any key notes—is presented in a clean vertical stack, centered or left-aligned with 12–20px between elements, and surrounded by roughly 24px of internal padding. The overall effect is similar to the mockup’s left-aligned image: a visually dense but compact block that anchors the right side of the composition without feeling heavy. A little more white space is allowed at the top and bottom of this panel so the numbers can breathe and stand out against the otherwise minimal layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Right column of the split card, aligned to the right edge of the main content column. On smaller screens, it moves below the configuration panel and expands to full width. Optionally, on large screens it can behave as a non-intrusive sticky panel that gently stays in view while the left-side configuration scrolls within the card’s area, as long as it remains within the main column boundaries.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The configuration summary appears directly below the configuration/price card, separated from it by 32–40px of vertical space and a slim horizontal rule that spans the full content width. This section reads as a concise meta row, similar to the publication date and category row in the mockup. The summary text is left-aligned and constrained to a single or double line, occupying roughly the left two-thirds of the content width. It may list key chosen options in a light, small typographic style. The rightmost third of the row is reserved for the request-quote trigger, which aligns horizontally with the summary text, creating a balanced, two-part meta bar. Overall height is modest (about 56–72px), giving the sense of a neat, compact information strip beneath the main card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline meta row within the normal flow, spanning the full width of the main column and sharing a horizontal baseline with the request-quote trigger component.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The request quote trigger is positioned on the far right of the same horizontal line as the configuration summary, mimicking the mockup’s 'Read More >' link. It aligns vertically with the baseline of the summary text and is right-aligned to the main content column’s edge. Visually, it may appear as a minimalist, outlined button or a strong text link with a small chevron or angle (e.g., 'Request Quote >'), occupying no more than 15–20% of the row’s width. There is a comfortable margin of 16–24px between the text of the summary and the trigger, maintaining the airy feel. The element is not overly tall; its vertical padding is modest (8–12px), so the row retains a slim, editorial meta-strip appearance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, right-aligned control within the configuration summary row. It stays in the normal document flow and shifts below the summary text on very narrow viewports, maintaining a top margin of about 8–12px when stacked.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When visible, the quote request form is revealed directly beneath the configuration summary and trigger row, starting about 32–40px below it. The form spans the full width of the main content column but retains an editorial lightness through generous whitespace and clean grouping. A subtle section heading (e.g., 'Request a detailed quote') appears at the top left, with 12–16px of space below it before the first fields. Form fields are arranged in a single column to preserve the linear reading experience, each group separated by 16–24px vertically and with 8–12px between label and input. The left alignment of labels and fields follows the established text alignment of the page. The bottom of the form ends with a primary submit button aligned to the left, leaving generous white space on the right to maintain the airy, magazine-like layout rather than a dense, full-width control bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Full-width block within the main content column that appears or collapses in place, pushing content below it downward. It scrolls naturally with the page and does not use fixed positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote confirmation banner appears as a slim, full-width strip within the main content column, typically positioned directly above the quote request form or, after submission, in the same area where the form previously sat. It spans the entire width of the content column with 16–24px of internal padding and is framed by a thin border or background tint consistent with the monochrome palette. The message text is left-aligned and concise, occupying no more than two lines, and there is ample white space above and below (24–32px) to keep it visually distinct from adjacent sections. In terms of vertical hierarchy, it feels like a polished editorial notice rather than a heavy alert box, integrated seamlessly into the page’s flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline status block in the normal flow. It appears in place of or directly above the quote request form, pushing content downward without overlaying other elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error state occupies the same central column as the rest of the content but generally replaces most of the main sections (header, configuration card, form) when a critical package load failure occurs. It is vertically centered within the visible content area where possible, with the error message block occupying roughly the middle third of the viewport height. The message is left-aligned within a narrow text column (about 60% of the content width) so it feels like editorial copy rather than a system dialog, with 16–24px of padding around it and plenty of empty space surrounding. A simple retry or back link may appear beneath the message, left-aligned with moderate spacing. No heavy backgrounds or overlays are used, keeping the experience consistent with the minimalist, white-space-driven design.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces the main content within the same centered column. It is part of the normal document flow but can be vertically centered using flexible spacing above and below for a balanced composition.

name: Journal / Blog Listing
title: Journal
description: Journal listing page that lets users search, filter, and browse design articles, then navigate to full article details.

core_features: Load and display a paginated list of journal articles using getJournalArticles based on the current URL parameters (query, tag, topic, pageNumber).
Provide a search input that updates the query URL parameter and reloads the article list from getJournalArticles.
Provide tag and topic filters that update corresponding URL parameters (tag, topic) and reload the article list from getJournalArticles.
Display a result summary (total results and active filters) using the response metadata from getJournalArticles and current URL parameters.
Provide pagination controls that use pageNumber URL parameter and getJournalArticles metadata (totalPages, totalResults, pageSize).
Allow navigation to the Article Detail page when a user clicks on an article card from the listing.
Optionally surface a featured or trending strip of articles using an additional call to getJournalArticles with specialized filters.
user_workflows:
Browse all articles:
1. User opens blog.html without URL parameters.
2. Page controller reads empty query, tag, and topic and sets pageNumber to a default of 1.
3. Page controller calls getJournalArticles with query undefined/empty, filters containing no tag or topic, and pageNumber from URL.
4. Article list renders the returned articles.
5. Pagination controls render using totalPages and pageNumber from the response.

Search for articles by keyword:
1. User types a keyword into the search field in the journal-search-form component.
2. User submits the form.
3. Page navigates to blog.html?query={encodedKeyword}&pageNumber=1 (existing tag/topic parameters can be preserved or cleared according to design; see state_logic).
4. Page controller reads URL parameters and calls getJournalArticles with the query from the URL and corresponding filters and pageNumber=1.
5. Article list updates with new results and result summary shows totalResults for that query.

Filter by tag:
1. User clicks a tag chip in the journal-filter-bar or within result summary.
2. Page navigates to blog.html?tag={encodedTag}&pageNumber=1 (optionally preserving query/topic where applicable).
3. Page controller reads URL parameters and calls getJournalArticles with filters including the selected tag.
4. Article list updates to show articles that match the tag.
5. Active tag is highlighted in the filter bar and reflected in the result summary.

Filter by topic:
1. User selects a topic chip or control in the journal-filter-bar.
2. Page navigates to blog.html?topic={encodedTopic}&pageNumber=1 (optionally preserving query/tag where applicable).
3. Page controller reads URL parameters and calls getJournalArticles with filters including the selected topic.
4. Article list updates to show articles that match the topic.
5. Active topic is highlighted and reflected in the result summary.

Combine search with tag/topic filters:
1. User performs a search, then clicks a tag or topic filter.
2. Navigation updates URL parameters to include query, tag, and/or topic together with pageNumber=1.
3. Page controller calls getJournalArticles with query and filters including both tag and topic where present.
4. Result summary shows combined criteria and article list reflects the combined filtering.

Paginate through results:
1. User scrolls to the pagination controls and chooses a page (next/previous or specific page number).
2. Page navigates to blog.html with the same query, tag, topic, but an updated pageNumber.
3. Page controller calls getJournalArticles with that pageNumber.
4. Article list updates to show articles for the selected page; pagination controls highlight the active page.

Open an article detail:
1. User clicks on an article card image/title within journal-article-list or journal-featured-strip.
2. Page navigates to article.html?id={articleId}.
3. The blog listing state remains encoded in the URL so the user can navigate back and preserve their search/filter context.

Discover featured or trending content:
1. On page load, the page controller optionally makes a second call to getJournalArticles with query undefined/empty and filters configured to represent a featured or trending set (for example, by passing a filter key that the backend understands).
2. journal-featured-strip component renders returned articles, independent from the main listing pagination.
3. Clicking any featured article card navigates to article.html?id={articleId}.
interactions: Search submission (journal-search-form.onSubmit): Reads the current input value, updates the URL with query and pageNumber=1 (and optionally retains existing tag/topic parameters), then triggers a full page reload or client-side route change; the page controller then re-calls getJournalArticles with the new query.
Clear search (journal-search-form.onClear, if implemented): Removes the query parameter from the URL (and resets pageNumber=1), then triggers re-loading of getJournalArticles without a query filter.

Tag selection (journal-filter-bar.onTagClick): When a tag chip is clicked, updates the tag parameter in the URL, resets pageNumber=1, preserves or clears query/topic as per the chosen behavior, and triggers re-calling getJournalArticles with filters including the selected tag.

Topic selection (journal-filter-bar.onTopicClick): When a topic chip is clicked, updates the topic parameter in the URL, resets pageNumber=1, and triggers a call to getJournalArticles with filters including the selected topic.

Clear filters (journal-filter-bar.onClearFilters): Removes tag and/or topic parameters (and optionally query) from the URL, resets pageNumber=1, then re-calls getJournalArticles to show unfiltered results.

Pagination (journal-pagination.onPageChange): Clicking a page control updates the pageNumber parameter in the URL while preserving current query, tag, and topic parameters, then the page controller re-calls getJournalArticles with the new pageNumber.
Article card click (journal-article-list.onCardClick): Clicking an article card image or title reads the associated article id and navigates to article.html?id={articleId}.
Featured article click (journal-featured-strip.onFeaturedClick): Similar to article card click but from the featured content strip; navigates to article.html?id={articleId}.

URL parameter change (journal-page-controller.onURLChange): Any navigation that changes query, tag, topic, or pageNumber triggers the controller to recompute arguments and call getJournalArticles, then update dependent components.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is fully encoded via URL parameters and the data returned from getJournalArticles; no local storage is used.

- URL parameters:
  - query (string): If present, this value is passed directly as the query argument to getJournalArticles. If absent, query is treated as empty/undefined and not used to constrain the search.
  - tag (string): If present, included within the filters argument to getJournalArticles (for example, filters.tag = tag). If absent, no tag-specific filtering is applied.
  - topic (string): If present, included within the filters argument to getJournalArticles (for example, filters.topic = topic). If absent, no topic-specific filtering is applied.
  - pageNumber (number): If present and valid, passed to getJournalArticles.pageNumber. If absent or invalid, the controller treats the effective pageNumber as 1 and ensures pagination controls reflect this.

- Mapping to getJournalArticles:
  - On each load or URL change, the page controller builds a filters object that incorporates tag and topic when they exist. The controller then calls getJournalArticles with:
    - query: taken from the query URL parameter or left undefined.
    - filters: object derived from tag, topic, and any additional internal flags (for example, for featured content in a separate call), without hardcoded filter values tied to specific tags or topics.
    - pageNumber: derived from the pageNumber URL parameter, defaulting logically to 1 if not provided.
    - pageSize: determined by layout or configuration outside this specification; the value is passed through without being hardcoded in this design.

- Result handling:
  - The main article list, result summary, and pagination components all derive their display strictly from the getJournalArticles response (articles, totalResults, pageNumber, pageSize, totalPages) and current URL parameters.
  - If getJournalArticles returns zero articles, the article list shows an empty state based on dynamic values (e.g., reflecting the current query and filters) without any baked-in content examples.

- Featured/trending strip (optional):
  - If implemented, the controller makes an additional call to getJournalArticles with a different filters object (for example, filters.featured or filters.trending) and a fixed pageNumber for that strip. These filters are internal to the controller and not reflected in URL parameters, as the featured strip is not directly user-filterable in this specification.

- Back/forward navigation:
  - Using the browser's back and forward buttons simply changes the URL parameters; the journal-page-controller listens for these changes, reconstructs the query/filters/pageNumber, and re-calls getJournalArticles accordingly, restoring the user's previous view without extra state storage.

components.functionality: Central controller for the journal listing page. Reads URL parameters (query, tag, topic, pageNumber), orchestrates one or more calls to getJournalArticles, and distributes the data and state to child components such as search, filters, article list, featured strip, and pagination.
Provides a text input and submit control for searching journal articles. Initializes its input value from the current query URL parameter and triggers navigation updates when submitted or cleared.

Displays and manages tag and topic filters for the article list. Highlights active tag/topic based on URL parameters and triggers URL updates when a user selects or clears filters. Available tags/topics can be derived from the aggregated tags/topics in the current getJournalArticles results or from configuration.

Displays a concise summary of the current result set and active criteria, such as total results count and which query, tag, and topic filters are active. Uses getJournalArticles.totalResults and current URL parameters to generate its content.

Renders the main list or grid of articles returned by getJournalArticles for the current state. Each article card shows data such as title, excerpt, hero image, tags, topics, publication date, and estimated read time, all sourced from the Article model via the interface.

Displays pagination controls based on pageNumber, pageSize, totalResults, and totalPages from getJournalArticles. Updates the pageNumber URL parameter when the user selects a new page, while preserving other URL parameters.

Optionally shows a horizontal strip or separate block of featured or trending articles, based on a dedicated call to getJournalArticles with a specialized filters object distinct from the main listing. Not affected by the main pageNumber but may still be influenced by high-level criteria if desired.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a centered fixed-width layout on a 12-column grid (approx. 1100–1200px content width) with generous outer margins. Content within each major section appears to follow a simple 2-column grid (image vs text) for article listings.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a centered, fixed-width main column (approx. 1100–1200px) beneath the full‑width site header/navigation. Inside this column, components are stacked in a linear top‑to‑bottom order: (1) the journal-page-controller acts as the page header/introduction. (2) Immediately below it, the search and filtering cluster appears as journal-search-form, then journal-filter-bar, then a thin yet clear journal-result-summary row. (3) Below the controls, an optional, low-profile journal-featured-strip highlights selected articles in a horizontal band. (4) The main journal-article-list follows, using the mockup’s horizontal split (image left, text right, meta below) for each article row. (5) At the very bottom of the content column, the journal-pagination component is centered and separated by generous whitespace. All components align to the same central column and respect the editorial, stacked-section rhythm from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a centered, fixed-width main column (approx. 1100–1200px) beneath the full‑width site header/navigation. Inside this column, components are stacked in a linear top‑to‑bottom order: (1) the journal-page-controller acts as the page header/introduction. (2) Immediately below it, the search and filtering cluster appears as journal-search-form, then journal-filter-bar, then a thin yet clear journal-result-summary row. (3) Below the controls, an optional, low-profile journal-featured-strip highlights selected articles in a horizontal band. (4) The main journal-article-list follows, using the mockup’s horizontal split (image left, text right, meta below) for each article row. (5) At the very bottom of the content column, the journal-pagination component is centered and separated by generous whitespace. All components align to the same central column and respect the editorial, stacked-section rhythm from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content column occupies roughly 60–70% of the viewport width and is centered, with comfortable side margins on larger screens. Major vertical gaps between sections (page-controller to search cluster, search cluster to featured-strip, featured-strip to article-list, article-list to pagination) are in the 32–48px range, often reinforced by a thin horizontal rule. Within components, padding is around 16–24px, while spacing between text elements is around 8–16px. In the article list, each card uses a 2‑column split: the image on the left takes about 35–40% of the main column width, and the text block to the right uses about 60–65%. Meta rows and slim bars (filter, summary) are low in height, with tight vertical padding but full-width rules to maintain structure.
layout_strategies.content_density: The overall density is intentionally low and editorial. Each section shows only a handful of elements at once. The search and filter controls are visually light and occupy minimal vertical height, leaving dominant space to the imagery and typographic hierarchy in the article cards. There is liberal use of white space around and within each component, and no heavy containers or large blocks of dense UI. The featured strip is kept shallow and spacious, and the pagination is unobtrusive. This ensures the page feels like a modern design journal, not a crowded blog index.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a mild Z-pattern adapted from the mockup. Users start at the site header/logo on the top-left, move across the top navigation, then drop into the journal-page-controller where the page title anchors the left side and optional supporting text balances the right. The eye then moves slightly toward the center for the search bar, before returning to the left alignment for filters and the result summary. From there, the gaze drops into the article region where each article’s image on the left forms a strong visual anchor; the eye then scans right to the article title and summary text. At the bottom of each article row, the gaze starts on the left meta (date/category) and finishes on the right-aligned ‘Read More >’ link, reinforcing the Z-shaped sweep. Finally, attention moves down to the centered pagination controls.

layout.overall_description: The Journal page presents as a clean, centered editorial column floating in generous white space beneath the full‑width site header. At the top of this column, a refined journal header block introduces the page with a confident title and a short line of copy, echoing the feel of an ‘About’ or editor’s note. Below, a wide but visually light search field sits near the center, flanked by understated filter chips and a slim summary bar with a hairline rule, forming a compact, minimalist control band. Beneath this, a horizontal featured strip quietly highlights a few curated pieces without overwhelming the layout.
