The main body is a calm stack of article rows: each one shows a large, landscape image anchored on the left and a spacious text column on the right with the article title, a brief description, and subtle ellipsis; a thin horizontal rule and a meta row below each card neatly separate articles, with date and category on the left and a ‘Read More >’ call to action aligned to the far right. The page ends with centered, minimalist pagination controls, surrounded by ample white space so they feel like a soft full stop rather than a hard boundary. The result is a restrained, magazine-like journal listing where images and typography breathe and the interaction controls stay elegantly in the background.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The journal-page-controller sits directly below the global site header/navigation, centered within the fixed-width main content column. It spans the full width of this column (approx. 1100–1200px) with 32–48px of vertical margin separating it from the header above and from the search-form below. Inside this block, the content is arranged in a loose 2-column split: on the left (about 55–60% of the column) sits the page title ‘Journal’ in large, light-weight type, optionally accompanied by a small overline (e.g., ‘Studio Journal’) above it. On the right (about 40–45% of the column), a short 1–2 line paragraph explains the intent of the journal (e.g., ‘Notes on furniture, interiors, and process’), set in a smaller, calm body type. Both sides sit on the same baseline or close to it, creating a balanced but asymmetric composition. The block has 16–24px internal padding on top and bottom, but no heavy container; it floats on the white background with only a very thin horizontal rule (hairline, full column width) rendered at its bottom edge to subtly mark the transition into the search/filter region. There are no large graphic elements here—just typography and space, keeping it aligned with the minimalist editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard document flow within the main content column; scrolls with the page and is not sticky or fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The journal-search-form appears immediately below the bottom rule of the page-controller, separated by roughly 24–32px of white space. It is visually centered within the main content column and occupies about 60–70% of that width, leaving equal white margins on both left and right sides inside the column. The search field itself is a single horizontal input bar with a low-profile outline or bottom rule rather than a heavy boxed style, respecting the minimalist aesthetic. The input stretches almost the full width of the search-form area, with a small search icon aligned to the far right inside the field. Vertical height is modest, with internal padding around 12–16px. Above or inside the field, a light placeholder or label such as ‘Search journal’ appears in subdued text. Generous white space (16–24px) is placed below this search-form before the filter-bar, so the controls feel airy rather than stacked tightly.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard document flow. On larger screens, the search-form remains in place and scrolls with the rest of the content, with no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The journal-filter-bar sits directly below the search-form, with a vertical gap of about 16–20px. It aligns to the left edge of the main content column (not centered like the search bar), visually re-establishing the left margin that aligns with article titles and body copy. The bar is relatively slim in height and stretches nearly the full width of the content column. At its top, an optional ultra-thin horizontal rule can run across the bar to visually tie it to the editorial grid. Within the bar, a row of filter controls (e.g., ‘All’, ‘Projects’, ‘Process’, ‘Inspiration’) are displayed as simple text labels or pill-shaped chips, starting at the left edge and flowing to the right, with 8–12px of horizontal spacing between each. They sit on a single line where possible; on smaller screens they can wrap onto multiple lines while maintaining left alignment. Vertical padding above and below the filters is light (8–12px), so the bar feels like a slim, typographic toolbar rather than a heavy control strip. An optional ‘Sort by’ dropdown can be right-aligned on the same line, visually balancing the filter labels, while still keeping the overall appearance clean and low-density.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard document flow. Optionally, on large screens the filter-bar may become sticky to the top of the viewport once scrolled into view, but by default it scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The journal-result-summary sits immediately below the filter-bar, separated by a modest 8–12px gap to keep the controls visually connected but not crowded. It takes the full width of the main content column and appears as a single, thin horizontal row of text. On the left side, a concise summary such as ‘12 articles • Showing latest first’ is left-aligned with the article text column. On the right side of the same row, there may be a subtle secondary element (e.g., a compact sort selector or view toggle) aligned to the far right, mirroring the meta row structure seen in the mockup. The row is underlined by a fine horizontal rule running the full width of the column, clearly delineating the end of the control cluster and the beginning of the content region. Vertical padding above and below the text in this row is quite light (around 8px), making it comparable in visual weight to a caption line rather than a full section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard document flow; sits as a discrete divider row between filters and the featured/article list and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The journal-featured-strip appears just below the result-summary, with a comfortable 32–40px of white space separating it from the summary’s bottom rule. It spans the full width of the main content column but is intentionally shallow in height to feel like a horizontal ‘band’ rather than a full section. At the top-left of this strip, a small, uppercase label such as ‘Featured’ or ‘Editor’s Picks’ is aligned with the main text column, set in a subtle but clear style. Directly beneath this label, a row of 2–4 featured article thumbnails is laid out horizontally. Each featured item uses a vertically stacked mini-card: a small landscape image at the top (taking about 60–70% of each card’s height) and a short title line below it, with 8–12px spacing between the image and text. These cards share equal widths and are separated by generous horizontal gaps (16–24px) to keep the band airy. The entire strip’s total height is roughly 200–260px, depending on content, and there is no heavy surrounding box—only space and possibly a very subtle top or bottom rule if needed for alignment. The eye can skim this band quickly before dropping down to the main article list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard document flow. On narrower screens, the featured items may wrap to a second row or be implemented as a horizontal scroll strip, but they remain within the same centered column and scroll with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The journal-article-list occupies the main body of the page and begins 32–48px below the featured-strip. It spans the full width of the centered content column and consists of a vertical stack of article rows, each following the mockup’s editorial card pattern. Each article row is arranged in a 2-column layout: on the left, a large landscape image block occupies roughly 35–40% of the content width; on the right, a text column occupies about 60–65%. Both columns share the same vertical center. The image sits flush with the left edge of the column and has a fixed aspect ratio (e.g., 16:9), with no overlaid text or buttons, emphasizing clean photography. To its right, the text column begins with the article title in a bold yet refined type style, followed by 8–12px of space, then a short body paragraph (2–3 lines) summarizing the article, then another small gap (8–12px) and a subtle ellipsis or ‘…’ to indicate continuation. Beneath this 2-column content area, extending across the full column width, a thin horizontal rule separates the card content from its meta row. Immediately below that rule, a slim meta row mirrors the mockup: on the left, the publication date and category are aligned with the left edge of the text column; on the right, a ‘Read More >’ text link is right-aligned to the column edge. Vertical spacing between consecutive articles (from one meta row to the next card’s image top) is generous—around 32–40px—so each article feels like a distinct editorial entry rather than part of a dense list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard document flow. Article cards are stacked vertically and scroll naturally; there is no fixed positioning or masonry behavior—just a clean, linear editorial list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The journal-pagination sits at the very bottom of the main content column, beginning approximately 32–48px below the final article’s meta row. It is horizontally centered within the column and relatively compact in height. The pagination controls—previous/next arrows and page numbers—are laid out in a single line, with equal spacing (8–12px) between numbers and slightly larger spacing around the arrows, all set in the same restrained typographic style as the rest of the page. There are no heavy buttons; instead, active and hover states are indicated through subtle weight or underline changes. The area above and below the pagination row is generous (roughly 24px above, 40–48px below), leaving the controls visually isolated and easy to find without feeling heavy. No additional boxes or backgrounds are used; the pagination rests directly on the white background, aligned to the central axis of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard document flow at the bottom of the content. The pagination is not sticky; it is reached naturally after scrolling through the article list.

name: Article Detail
title: Article Detail
description: Displays a single journal article with full content, metadata, save-to-favorites, and navigation to related articles and the main Journal listing.

core_features: Load and display full article content and metadata based on the URL parameter `id` using `getArticleDetail`.
Show article metadata including title, author, publication date, estimated reading time, tags, and topics.
Provide a save-to-favorites control that calls `saveArticleToFavorites` for the current article.
Indicate whether the current article is already in favorites using the `isFavorited` value from `getArticleDetail`.
Display a list of related articles returned by `getArticleDetail` and allow navigation to their detail pages.
Offer navigation back to the Journal listing (blog.html) and support browser back to any prior search results.
Handle loading and error states for article retrieval and saving to favorites, with clear user feedback.
user_workflows:
View an article from the Journal listing:
1. User is on blog.html and clicks an article link which navigates to article.html?id={selectedArticleId}.
2. On load, the Article Detail page reads `id` from the URL.
3. The page calls `getArticleDetail` with `articleId = id`.
4. While waiting, a loading state is shown and content is hidden.
5. On success, the article title, metadata, hero image, and content are rendered.
6. The save-to-favorites control is initialized based on `isFavorited`.
7. The related articles list is populated from `relatedArticles`.
8. User can click "Back to Journal" to go to blog.html or use the browser back button to return to the exact listing or search results state.

Save an article to favorites:
1. User is viewing an article on article.html?id={articleId}.
2. The page has already called `getArticleDetail(articleId)` and received `isFavorited`.
3. If `isFavorited` is false, the save control shows a primary state (e.g., label like "Save" or a hollow heart icon).
4. User clicks the save control.
5. The control triggers `saveArticleToFavorites` with `articleId` equal to the current article's id and enters a pending state (disabled or with a spinner).
6. On success (response `success = true`), the page updates its local favorite state to true and may display a brief confirmation message.
7. The control switches to a saved state (e.g., "Saved" or filled heart, non-clickable or idempotent).
8. A "View My Favorites" link becomes available or remains visible; clicking it navigates to account.html?section=favorites.
9. If `saveArticleToFavorites` returns `success = false`, the page shows an error message and allows the user to retry.

Open a related article:
1. User scrolls to the related articles section on article.html.
2. The page shows a list of related article cards using `relatedArticles` from `getArticleDetail`.
3. User clicks a related article card.
4. The browser navigates to article.html?id={relatedArticleId} for the selected card.
5. The Article Detail page reads the new `id` from the URL and repeats the load workflow: calls `getArticleDetail`, shows loading, then renders the new article and updated related articles.
6. Browser back navigates between previously viewed articles by changing the URL and re-triggering `getArticleDetail` as needed.

Handle missing or invalid article:
1. User accesses article.html without an `id` parameter or with an invalid/non-existent article id.
2. On load, the page checks the URL for `id`.
3. If `id` is missing or empty, the page does not call `getArticleDetail` and instead shows an "article not found" error state with a link to blog.html.
4. If `id` is present but `getArticleDetail` returns an error (e.g., article not found), the page shows an error message and a "Back to Journal" link to blog.html.
5. User can click the link to return to the Journal listing.

Navigate back to Journal or search results:
1. User arrives at article.html from blog.html (possibly with filters or search applied).
2. To return to browsing, user can either:
   - Click the "Back to Journal" link, which navigates directly to blog.html, or
   - Use the browser back button to return to the exact previous state (including any search terms or filters on blog.html).
3. No additional API calls are needed on the Article Detail page for this navigation; it is handled by standard URL navigation.
interactions: On page load or when the URL `id` parameter changes, automatically call `getArticleDetail` with `articleId` set to the current `id` value.
Click on the "Back to Journal" control navigates to blog.html.
Click on the save-to-favorites control (when not already favorited) calls `saveArticleToFavorites(articleId)` and updates the interface based on the response.
Click on the "View My Favorites" link navigates to account.html?section=favorites.
Click on a related article card navigates to article.html?id={relatedArticleId}.

Optional: Click on tags or topics under the article metadata can navigate to blog.html with appropriate query parameters to filter articles by that tag or topic (e.g., blog.html?tag={tagName}), without additional interfaces.
If the article load fails, click on a retry control will re-call `getArticleDetail(articleId)` using the same URL `id`.
If saving to favorites fails, click on a retry action inside the error message will re-call `saveArticleToFavorites(articleId)`.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state of the page is determined by the URL parameter `id` and the results of the `getArticleDetail` and `saveArticleToFavorites` interfaces. On initialization, the page reads `id` from the URL: if present and non-empty, it calls `getArticleDetail(articleId = id)`; if missing or empty, it shows an error state and no API call is made. The page maintains internal UI state flags such as `isLoadingArticle`, `loadError`, `isSavingFavorite`, `saveError`, and `isFavorited`. `isLoadingArticle` is true from the moment `getArticleDetail` is initiated until it resolves or rejects. On a successful response, `isLoadingArticle` is set to false, `loadError` is cleared, and article-related UI components bind to the returned `article`, `isFavorited`, and `relatedArticles` values. If `getArticleDetail` fails, `loadError` is set and content components remain hidden, showing only the error component. The favorite state is initialized from the `isFavorited` field of the `getArticleDetail` response. When the user clicks save, `isSavingFavorite` becomes true, `saveError` is cleared, and `saveArticleToFavorites(articleId)` is called; on success, `isSavingFavorite` becomes false, `isFavorited` is set to true, and the favorite control switches to its saved state. On failure, `isSavingFavorite` becomes false, `saveError` is set, and the user is informed with an option to retry. When the URL `id` parameter changes (for example via navigation to a related article), the page resets its state (clears previous article data, resets errors, sets `isLoadingArticle` to true) and calls `getArticleDetail` again with the new `articleId`. All navigation to related articles, the Journal listing, or the favorites section is performed by updating the browser URL with the appropriate parameters; no localStorage or persistent client-side storage is used to manage state.

components.functionality: Coordinates page initialization and data loading for the article detail view. Reads the URL parameter `id`, validates it, calls `getArticleDetail` with the corresponding `articleId`, and manages loading, success, and error states for the article data. Listens for URL changes (e.g., when navigating to another article via related articles) and re-triggers data loading when the `id` parameter changes.
event_handlers: onInit: Read URL.id; if present and non-empty, call getArticleDetail({ articleId: URL.id }); otherwise set a not-found error state.
onUrlChange: If the `id` parameter value changes, reset article-related state and call getArticleDetail({ articleId: newId }).
onLoadSuccess: Receive the response from getArticleDetail, set isLoadingArticle = false, store article, isFavorited, and relatedArticles, and notify dependent components to render.
onLoadError: Set isLoadingArticle = false, store the error details, and notify the error component to display the appropriate message.

components.functionality: Provides simple navigation controls at the top of the article detail page, allowing users to go back to the Journal listing and orient themselves within the site structure.
event_handlers: onClickBackToJournal: Navigate the browser to blog.html without additional parameters.
onClickTitle (optional): If the article title is shown in the breadcrumb, it is non-interactive for the current page; any click is ignored or simply does not change state.

components.functionality: Displays the primary article metadata, including the title, author name, publication date, estimated reading time, hero image, and any tags or topics. This component is responsible for showing all high-level information the user needs before reading the main content.
event_handlers: onTagClick (optional): When a tag is clicked, navigate to blog.html with a query parameter representing the tag (e.g., blog.html?tag={selectedTag}).
onTopicClick (optional): When a topic is clicked, navigate to blog.html with a query parameter representing the topic (e.g., blog.html?topic={selectedTopic}).
onHeroImageLoad: Update internal state to indicate the hero image has loaded; can be used to hide any loading placeholder for the image.
onHeroImageError: Handle hero image load failures by hiding the image area or showing a non-blocking alternative.

components.functionality: Renders the full article body content in a scrollable area. Supports the text and any embedded media as provided in the `content` field from `getArticleDetail`. Does not modify the content; it simply renders it according to whatever structure is returned (e.g., HTML or a structured rich text format).
event_handlers: onContentRender: Called after content is injected into the DOM to enable any necessary client-side enhancements (such as enabling scroll-to anchors).
onInternalAnchorClick (optional): If the content includes internal links or anchors, handle them by scrolling within the page rather than reloading, where appropriate.
onMediaInteraction (optional): If embedded media (such as images) support enlargement or modal viewing, handle clicks to open those views without affecting the article state.

components.functionality: Allows the user to save the current article to their favorites list and indicates whether it is already saved. Initially reflects the `isFavorited` status from `getArticleDetail`. When the user clicks to save an article that is not yet favorited, it calls `saveArticleToFavorites` and updates the UI based on the outcome. Also provides navigation to the favorites section of the account page after a successful save.
event_handlers: onInit: Read initial favorite state from getArticleDetail.isFavorited and configure the control (saved vs. unsaved appearance).

onClickSave: If current isFavorited is false and no save operation is in progress, set isSavingFavorite = true, clear any previous saveError, and call saveArticleToFavorites({ articleId: getArticleDetail.article.id }). If isFavorited is true, ignore the click or show a non-blocking indication that the article is already saved.

onSaveSuccess: When saveArticleToFavorites returns with success = true, set isSavingFavorite = false, update isFavorited to true, optionally store favoriteArticleId and savedAt for display, and show a confirmation message.

onSaveError: When saveArticleToFavorites returns with success = false or an error occurs, set isSavingFavorite = false, set saveError with the returned message (if available), and show an error state or tooltip with an option to retry.
onClickViewFavorites: Navigate the browser to account.html?section=favorites after a successful save (or at any time if the control is present).

components.functionality: Displays a list of related articles provided by `getArticleDetail`. Each related article is shown as a clickable card or list item that includes at minimum the title and optional hero image. Clicking a related article navigates to its own Article Detail page using the appropriate `id` in the URL.
event_handlers: onRelatedItemRender: For each related article, bind its id, title, and heroImageUrl fields to the UI element representing that related article.

onClickRelatedArticle: When a related article is clicked, navigate the browser to article.html?id={relatedArticle.id}. This change in URL `id` triggers the article-detail-controller to call getArticleDetail for the new article.
onEmptyRelatedList: If getArticleDetail.relatedArticles is empty or undefined, hide or disable this section without affecting other page functionality.

components.functionality: Shows a loading state while article data is being fetched via `getArticleDetail`. It ensures that incomplete or inconsistent article content is not displayed while the request is in progress.
event_handlers: onLoadingStateChange: When isLoadingArticle changes from true to false, hide the loading indicator and allow the main article content, header, and related components to render.

components.functionality: Displays error messages when the article cannot be loaded, such as when the `id` URL parameter is missing, invalid, or when `getArticleDetail` fails. Provides simple navigation options back to the Journal listing and, where possible, a retry action.
event_handlers: onInit: If URL.id is missing or empty, set a specific 'article not found' message and hide the main content components.
onLoadErrorChange: When article-detail-controller.loadError is set, show the appropriate error message instead of the article content.
onClickBackToJournal: Navigate the browser to blog.html to allow the user to select another article.
onClickRetry: If URL.id is present and article-detail-controller.loadError is due to a transient issue, trigger article-detail-controller to re-call getArticleDetail({ articleId: URL.id }).

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a centered fixed-width layout on a 12-column grid (approx. 1100–1200px content width) with generous outer margins. Content within each major section appears to follow a simple 2-column grid (image vs text) for article listings.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a single, centered column that sits inside a fixed-width content container (~1100–1200px) on a 12-column grid. From top to bottom within this main column: the breadcrumb sits just below the global site header; next comes the article header block using a horizontal split (hero image on the left, title and intro copy on the right); directly beneath that, a thin rule spans the full content width, followed by a low-profile meta row where publication metadata appears on the left and the favorite control anchors to the right. The rich article body flows below as a full-width text column, and the related articles list is stacked at the bottom as a distinct section. Loading and error states take over this central column, replacing the main content when active.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a single, centered column that sits inside a fixed-width content container (~1100–1200px) on a 12-column grid. From top to bottom within this main column: the breadcrumb sits just below the global site header; next comes the article header block using a horizontal split (hero image on the left, title and intro copy on the right); directly beneath that, a thin rule spans the full content width, followed by a low-profile meta row where publication metadata appears on the left and the favorite control anchors to the right. The rich article body flows below as a full-width text column, and the related articles list is stacked at the bottom as a distinct section. Loading and error states take over this central column, replacing the main content when active.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content column occupies roughly 60–70% of the viewport width and is centered with wide, even side margins. The article header’s image uses about 4 of 12 columns (35–40% of the content width) on the left, while the right column with title and intro text uses the remaining 8 columns (60–65%). Vertical spacing between major sections (breadcrumb to header, header to meta row, meta row to body, body to related list) uses generous 32–48px gaps, often reinforced by a thin horizontal rule. Within components, typography and controls use tighter 12–20px spacing between lines and elements, with 16–24px of internal padding where needed.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains a light, editorial feel with low information density. Each major block (breadcrumb, header, meta row, body, related list) is clearly separated with ample whitespace and minimal decoration. There are no dense sidebars; instead, text and imagery are allowed to breathe, emphasizing the hero image, the article title, and the furniture/interior photography. Metadata and actions are slim and unobtrusive, supporting the content rather than competing for attention.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow continues the mild Z-pattern from the mockup: the eye enters at the top-left breadcrumb and page title direction, then drops into the article header where the hero image on the left anchors attention. From the image, the gaze moves across to the right-hand title and intro copy, then down to the horizontal rule and meta row. In the meta row, the eye reads from left (date/category metadata) to right (favorite control). From there, it naturally drops straight down into the full-width article body, and finally to the related articles block at the bottom, where individual related items may again follow image-left, text-right patterns consistent with the original card design.

layout.overall_description: Inside a centered, fixed-width container beneath the site’s slim global header, the Article Detail page reads like a modern design magazine spread. A subtle breadcrumb sits at the top-left of the main column, surrounded by generous white space. Below it, the article header forms a calm two-column composition: on the left, a large landscape photograph of an interior or furniture vignette occupies about one-third of the content width; on the right, the article’s category label, title, and short introductory paragraph are stacked with measured spacing and refined typography. A fine horizontal rule stretches edge-to-edge below this header, grounding the section. Immediately under this line, a slim meta row displays the publication date and category on the left, while on the far right a minimalist ‘save to favorites’ control aligns with the same baseline, echoing the mockup’s date-and-CTA rhythm. The main article body flows underneath as a single, airy text column, with comfortable line height and wide margins that keep the reading experience relaxed and editorial. At the bottom of the page, separated again by white space and a thin rule, a related articles strip presents a small number of suggested stories in a clean list, echoing the image-left, text-right pattern of the original article card design. Loading and error states temporarily occupy this central column, centered and uncluttered, without disturbing the overall minimalist structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This non-visual controller component occupies the logical scope of the main content column, starting just below the global site header and spanning the full width of the centered content container (~1100–1200px). It determines whether the loading indicator, error state, or the full article stack (breadcrumb, header, meta row with favorite control, body, related list) is rendered. When loading or error states are active, the controller effectively hides the regular content layout and instead centers the corresponding feedback component within the same vertical zone, preserving the page’s clean, single-column structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual wrapper controlling which child components occupy the main content region; does not itself render UI but governs stacking order and visibility.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb is positioned at the top of the article detail content, directly beneath the site’s full-width header/navigation bar. It is left-aligned within the centered main column, starting at the same left edge as the article title and body text. The breadcrumb text runs in a single line, spanning only as much width as needed (typically 20–40% of the content width), with generous white space surrounding it: about 32–40px of space above (to the global header) and 24–32px of space below (before the article header block). Its visual weight is kept light—small, all-caps or thin-ruled separators—so it orients the user without overpowering the article title that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls naturally with the page as part of the main content column; no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article header sits directly below the breadcrumb, separated by approximately 32–40px of vertical space. It forms a two-column layout within the 12-column grid of the centered container. On the left, the hero image spans about 4 columns (~35–40% of the content width), set in a fixed aspect ratio (e.g., wide landscape) and aligned flush with the left edge of the main text column. On the right, occupying the remaining 8 columns (~60–65% width), the textual header content is vertically centered relative to the image or slightly top-aligned for a more editorial feel. Within this right column, a small category label or overline appears at the top, followed by the main article title in a larger, bold serif or refined sans-serif, then an optional short intro paragraph. Spacing between these text rows uses 12–20px increments to maintain airiness. The entire header block is bounded below by a very thin horizontal rule that runs the full width of the content container, positioned about 24–32px beneath the last line of header text. The article’s primary metadata (such as date and category) is conceptually tied to this header and appears immediately below the rule on the left side of the meta row, aligning with the left edge of the text column to maintain a clean visual column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section within the main column, maintaining its two-column split on standard desktop widths; on narrower viewports, it may stack into image-first, text-below while preserving generous vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article body begins just below the header’s meta row, separated by about 24–32px of vertical space. It occupies the full width of the centered content column (all 12 grid columns), with no competing sidebars, emphasizing a clean reading experience. Paragraph text lines are comfortably wide but not full-bleed—using the same column width as the header’s right-hand text block—creating a subtle vertical alignment of text from top to bottom. Line height is generous, and 16–24px of vertical spacing is used between paragraphs and subheadings to maintain the low-density, editorial feel. Any inline images or pull quotes are treated as full-width or slightly inset elements, always centered within this text column and surrounded by extra white space (24–32px above and below). The bottom of the article body is clearly separated from the related articles section by either a thin horizontal rule or a 32–48px white-space gap.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows vertically as a standard scrollable text block within the main column, expanding in height based on content; no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorite control is positioned within the slim meta row that sits directly beneath the article header’s horizontal rule. In that row, the article metadata (date and category) appears on the left, while the favorite control is aligned to the far right edge of the main content column, creating a balanced left–right tension. The control itself is compact—a minimal icon/button (e.g., outlined heart with a subtle label such as ‘Save’ or ‘Favorite’)—occupying roughly a single text line in height and no more than 10–15% of the content width. Vertical padding in the meta row is light (about 8–12px above and below the baseline of the text), reinforcing the slim, understated nature of this band. The control’s minimal styling keeps it visually present but secondary to the article title and imagery, harmonizing with the mockup’s pattern of a small CTA on the right side of a meta line.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline right-aligned element within the meta row; scrolls with the content and does not remain fixed when the user scrolls the article.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related articles list is placed at the bottom of the page, following the article body. It is separated from the final paragraph by a generous 32–48px of white space, often prefaced by a thin horizontal rule or a small section label (e.g., ‘Related Journal Articles’) aligned with the main text column. The list itself spans the full width of the content container but typically showcases a small number of items (e.g., 2–3) to maintain low density. Each related item can mirror the original card pattern: a compact landscape image on the left (about 30–35% of the width of each row) with the article title and a one-line teaser on the right, using 2-column rows stacked vertically with 24–32px spacing between them. Overall vertical padding for the related section (top and bottom) is around 32–48px, giving it a clear sense of being a separate, but visually connected, continuation of the editorial experience.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section anchored at the bottom of the main content flow; expands vertically as related items are added, without fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the loading indicator occupies the same central column as the article content, starting just below the global header. It is horizontally centered within the fixed-width container and vertically positioned with ample breathing room—typically centered within the viewport or at least 120–160px below the header to feel visually balanced. The indicator itself is minimal (a thin spinner, subtle progress ring, or simple ‘Loading Journal Article…’ label), surrounded by generous white space so that no other article components are visible. This preserves the clean, minimalist aesthetic and ensures that the user’s attention is focused on the loading state without visual clutter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily replaces the main content stack within the controller’s region; scrollable if the viewport is shorter than the indicator’s vertical placement but typically fits comfortably without requiring scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error state appears in the same location as the loading indicator and main content: centered within the fixed-width main column beneath the global header. It is horizontally centered and given a moderate width (about 50–60% of the content width) to keep the message easy to read, with 24–32px of internal padding and ample surrounding whitespace. A concise error message (e.g., ‘Unable to load this article’) is top-aligned within this block, followed by a small, unobtrusive action link or button such as ‘Go back to Journal’ or ‘Retry’ aligned to the left or center, mirroring the understated editorial style. No other article components are shown behind it, maintaining a clean, singular focus when an error occurs.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the main article content stack within the controller’s region when an error occurs; scrolls naturally if content exceeds viewport height but is typically short and contained.

name: Style Quiz
title: Style Quiz
description: Page where users answer a dynamic style quiz, submit their responses, and are redirected to a style-matched results page.

core_features: Load active style quiz questions and their answer options using the getStyleQuizQuestions interface when the page initializes.
Display an introduction section that explains the purpose of the quiz and provides a Start Quiz button.
Show quiz questions one at a time with multiple-choice answer options, driven entirely by the data returned from getStyleQuizQuestions.
Allow users to select and change answers for each question and track their selections across the full quiz.
Provide navigation between questions (Previous/Next and optional direct step selection) using URL parameters to determine the currently active question.
Display a quiz progress indicator (e.g., current question index out of total) based on loaded questions and current URL state.
Validate that each question has at least one selected answer before enabling quiz submission.
Submit the full set of selected answerOptionIds via submitStyleQuizAnswers and handle loading and error states during submission.
On successful submission, navigate to style_results.html with styleKey and styleQuizResultId passed as URL parameters derived from the submitStyleQuizAnswers response.
Handle error states from both getStyleQuizQuestions and submitStyleQuizAnswers and display functional error messages with retry options.
user_workflows: Start the quiz: 1) User navigates to style_quiz.html (no URL parameters). 2) Page calls getStyleQuizQuestions and prepares quiz state. 3) Intro section is shown while questions load. 4) After questions are available, the user clicks the Start Quiz button. 5) The page updates the URL to include a question parameter (e.g., style_quiz.html?question=1) and shows the first question.

Answer questions and navigate: 1) Based on the question URL parameter, the corresponding StyleQuizQuestion is shown with its answer options. 2) User selects an answer option for the current question; the selection is stored in page state keyed by questionId. 3) User clicks Next to move forward; the URL question parameter is incremented, and the page scrolls/focuses to the question view. 4) User can click Previous to go back, which decrements the question parameter and restores previously selected answers from state. 5) Optionally, user can jump directly to another question by clicking a step indicator; the URL question parameter is set to that index.

Submit quiz and view results: 1) When the user is on the last question and all questions have at least one selected answer, the See My Style button becomes enabled. 2) User clicks See My Style. 3) The page gathers all selected answerOptionIds from state into a single array and calls submitStyleQuizAnswers with that array. 4) While awaiting the response, the submit button is disabled and a loading state is shown. 5) On success, the page reads styleQuizResultId and recommendedStyleKey from the response. 6) The page navigates to style_results.html, appending styleKey and styleQuizResultId as URL query parameters. 7) If submission fails, an error message is shown and the user may retry.

Return or deep-link behavior: 1) If a user navigates directly to style_quiz.html with a question parameter in the URL (e.g., from a bookmark or using the browser back button), the page still calls getStyleQuizQuestions on load. 2) After questions are loaded, the page validates that the requested question index exists; if not, it falls back to the closest valid index. 3) The corresponding question is displayed based on the URL without requiring the user to restart the quiz.
interactions: Click Start Quiz: Updates the URL to include question=1 (or the first available question order) and switches the view from introduction mode to question mode.

Change current question via URL: The component that reads the URL question parameter updates the displayed StyleQuizQuestion whenever the parameter changes (e.g., via Next, Previous, or browser navigation).

Select an answer option: Clicking or activating an answer option stores that answerOptionId in the quiz state associated with the current questionId. The UI reflects selection status based on this state.
Change an existing answer: Selecting a different answer option for the same question replaces the previously stored answerOptionId for that questionId in the quiz state.

Navigate to previous question: Clicking a Previous button decrements the question URL parameter (not going below the first valid question) and reloads the question content while preserving any stored selections.
Navigate to next question: Clicking a Next button increments the question URL parameter (not exceeding the last valid question) and moves to the next StyleQuizQuestion.
Direct step click: Clicking on an element in the progress indicator sets the question URL parameter to that step’s index, switching directly to the associated question.

Attempt to submit with incomplete answers: Clicking See My Style while some questions have no selected answers triggers validation; missing questions are flagged in state, and the submission is blocked until all are answered.

Submit quiz: Clicking See My Style when validation passes calls submitStyleQuizAnswers with the full list of selected answerOptionIds and then, on success, redirects to style_results.html with the returned styleKey and styleQuizResultId in the URL.

Retry after error: When an error banner is shown for either loading questions or submitting answers, clicking a Retry action triggers the relevant interface again (getStyleQuizQuestions for loading errors, submitStyleQuizAnswers for submission errors).
page_functionality.state_logic: The main URL parameter controlling page state is question, representing the currently active quiz question (1-based index, derived from the ordered list returned by getStyleQuizQuestions). When style_quiz.html is loaded: (1) The page always calls getStyleQuizQuestions. (2) After questions are retrieved, the page checks the query parameter question. If question is missing or invalid, it defaults to the first question. If question is larger than the number of questions, it is clamped to the last question. Changing the question parameter (through internal navigation controls or browser navigation) updates the visible question. Quiz answer selections are maintained in in-memory page state keyed by questionId and are not stored in URL parameters or localStorage. The URL is not used to represent completion state; instead, after successful submission via submitStyleQuizAnswers, the page navigates to style_results.html with two new URL query parameters: styleKey set to the recommendedStyleKey returned by submitStyleQuizAnswers, and styleQuizResultId set to the styleQuizResultId returned by submitStyleQuizAnswers. The Style Quiz page itself does not read any incoming parameters for pre-filling answers or results; it only reads and writes the question parameter for navigation purposes.

components.functionality: On initial page render, call getStyleQuizQuestions, store the ordered list of questions and their answer options in page state, and expose loading and error status to other components.

Display a concise explanation of how the quiz works and a Start Quiz button. The component is visible when the quiz is at the introduction state (no valid question parameter in the URL or explicitly in intro mode). On Start Quiz, it updates the URL to target the first available question.

Map the URL question parameter to the corresponding StyleQuizQuestion from the loaded list. Ensure the index is within bounds, and expose the active questionId, its answer options, and the total question count to dependent components.

Show current quiz progress based on the active question index and total number of questions. Optionally allow direct navigation to any question by clicking a step indicator, which updates the URL question parameter.

Maintain an in-memory mapping from questionId to selected answerOptionId(s). Provide methods to set or change the selection for a given questionId and to retrieve the full list of selected answerOptionIds in preparation for submission.

Render the currently active StyleQuizQuestion and its associated answerOptions. Reflect which answerOptionId is currently selected for this questionId using the selections provided by style-quiz-answer-state. Handle answer selection interactions and pass them back to the answer state manager.

Provide Previous and Next buttons for moving between questions and a See My Style button for submission on the final question. Previous and Next update the question URL parameter to navigate. The See My Style button is enabled only when the current question is the last one and all questions have selections according to validation from style-quiz-validation.

Inspect the mapping of questionId to selected answerOptionId(s) and determine whether all loaded questions have at least one associated answer. Provide per-question validation flags (e.g., missing answer) and an overall isQuizComplete flag that is used to enable or disable the See My Style button.

When triggered by the See My Style button and validation passes, collect all selected answerOptionIds from style-quiz-answer-state and call submitStyleQuizAnswers. Manage the submission lifecycle, including setting a submitting state, handling errors, and, on success, reading styleQuizResultId and recommendedStyleKey from the response and redirecting to style_results.html with these values as URL parameters.

Display error messages related to loading quiz questions or submitting quiz answers. Shows context-specific actions such as Retry for loading errors and Retry Submission for submission errors by delegating to the appropriate handlers.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a centered fixed-width layout on a 12-column grid (approx. 1100–1200px content width) with generous outer margins. Content within each major section appears to follow a simple 2-column grid (image vs text) for article listings.
layout.layout_pattern: Within the existing full-width site header/nav, the Style Quiz page uses a single, centered content column constrained to roughly 1100–1200px. Inside this column, elements are stacked in a linear top-to-bottom flow: optional error banner at the very top of the quiz area, then the quiz intro block (acting like the author block analogue), followed by a thin divider, then the progress indicator, then the primary quiz card which pairs a question image on the left with question text and answers on the right. Directly beneath this card sits a slim meta/navigation row for Back/Next/Submit controls and question meta. Invisible logic components (loader, state controller, answer state, validator, submit handler) either overlay this flow temporarily (loader, error states) or sit behind it without adding visual clutter.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Within the existing full-width site header/nav, the Style Quiz page uses a single, centered content column constrained to roughly 1100–1200px. Inside this column, elements are stacked in a linear top-to-bottom flow: optional error banner at the very top of the quiz area, then the quiz intro block (acting like the author block analogue), followed by a thin divider, then the progress indicator, then the primary quiz card which pairs a question image on the left with question text and answers on the right. Directly beneath this card sits a slim meta/navigation row for Back/Next/Submit controls and question meta. Invisible logic components (loader, state controller, answer state, validator, submit handler) either overlay this flow temporarily (loader, error states) or sit behind it without adding visual clutter.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main quiz content column occupies about 60–70% of the viewport width and is centered, with generous lateral white space on both sides. Major blocks (error banner, intro, progress indicator, question card, navigation/meta row) are separated vertically by 32–48px, often punctuated by thin horizontal rules. Inside each block, text elements are spaced with 8–16px vertical gaps, and components have internal padding around 16–24px. In the question card, the left-side image takes roughly 35–40% of the content width, while the right-side text and answers take 60–65%, maintaining a low, editorial profile rather than a tall app-like layout.
layout_strategies.content_density: Only one major interactive unit—the current question—appears at a time, supported by a concise introduction and minimal meta. Each section uses ample white space and thin typography, keeping the density deliberately low. Answer options are presented as a clean vertical list with comfortable spacing, avoiding crowded form controls. System messages (validation, errors) are small, understated lines of text that occupy minimal height and do not introduce heavy UI chrome.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye enters at the page header and then drops into the quiz: first scanning the error banner (if present) from left to right, then the quiz intro, starting at the small visual on the left and moving across to the introduction copy on the right. From there, the eye is gently led down to the slim progress indicator, then anchored by the question image on the left of the main quiz card before traversing to the question title and answers on the right. Finally, attention moves downward to the meta/navigation row at the bottom, reading subtle question meta on the left and ending on the primary action control (Next/See Results) aligned to the far right, echoing the mockup’s Z-pattern and bottom meta row pattern.

layout.overall_description: The Style Quiz page sits within a clean, centered editorial canvas. Below the site’s full-width header, a generous band of white space introduces a single main column, about 1100–1200px wide, floating in the center of the viewport. If an error is present, a slim, understated banner stretches across this column, framed by fine rules and airy padding. Beneath it, the quiz intro block mirrors an editorial author section: a modest image or icon on the left and a short, refined block of text on the right, explaining what the quiz will do. A thin horizontal rule marks the transition into the interactive area. Just below, a slim progress indicator quietly tracks how far the user has come, aligned with the column grid and leaving plenty of negative space. The main quiz card sits next: on the left, a landscape-style room or furniture image occupies roughly a third of the width; on the right, the current question is set in crisp typography above a vertical list of answers, each with breathing room and subtle states. All of this is contained in a low, wide block with moderate internal padding, giving it the presence of an editorial feature rather than a form. At the bottom, a slender navigation/meta row runs horizontally: on the left, low-key text such as question count or a Back link; on the right, a primary Next/See Results control. Thin dividers and carefully measured vertical spacing create a calm rhythm down the page. Invisible controllers and handlers support this structure without adding visible clutter, so the user experiences the quiz as a single, elegant, magazine-like story rather than a dense questionnaire.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader occupies the same centered main content column as the quiz, spanning roughly 1100–1200px in width and aligned to the grid. When active, it visually replaces the intro and question content: a translucent white overlay fills the quiz column from just below the site header down to approximately 60–70% of the viewport height. At the vertical and horizontal center of this overlay, a small, minimalist loading indicator (e.g., a thin circular spinner or a simple ‘Loading your style quiz…’ line of text) appears, using light typography and ample surrounding whitespace. There are no heavy boxes or shadows; the loader feels like a subtle veil over the editorial canvas, with at least 32px of clear space above and below the loading text to maintain the airy aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an absolute-positioned overlay within the main content column, covering the intro, progress, question, and navigation components while data is loading, but not extending beyond the centered column or fixing to the viewport edges.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The intro block is the first permanent element of the quiz experience, positioned at the top of the main quiz column, directly beneath any error banner and a 32–48px vertical gap. It spans the full width of the centered content column (about 60–70% of the viewport) and is composed as a horizontal, two-column band. On the left, occupying roughly 25–30% of the column width, sits a small square or circular visual (e.g., a stylized icon, abstract room vignette, or quiz mark) with a fixed size around 96–128px, centered vertically within the block. On the right, taking the remaining 70–75%, is the text column: a subtle label (e.g., ‘Style Quiz’) in small caps at the top, followed by a larger, editorial-style heading and a brief description paragraph beneath it. Text lines are spaced at about 8–12px within this column, and the entire block has internal padding of roughly 24px top and bottom. A generous 32–40px gap separates this intro block from a thin horizontal rule running full width across the column, which visually marks the transition into the question area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, flowing block-level section within the main column that does not stick or float; it simply pushes subsequent content downward according to the grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The question state controller acts as an invisible structural wrapper that occupies the full width of the main quiz column beneath the intro’s horizontal rule. Visually it does not draw any borders or backgrounds of its own; instead, it defines a vertical zone that contains, in order, the progress indicator, the active question card, the validation messaging, and the navigation/meta row. This zone starts approximately 24–32px below the intro divider and extends downward as far as needed to comfortably display one question card and its associated controls, generally within 60–80% of the viewport height for a single screen experience.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static container that participates in normal document flow, grouping the progress, question, answers, and navigation without adding its own visible UI or special behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The progress indicator sits at the top of the question zone, aligned with the left edge of the main column and beginning about 24px below the horizontal rule that ends the intro. It appears as a slim, understated bar and label rather than a heavy widget. A small line of text such as ‘Question 2 of 8’ is left-aligned at the top of this area, using the same fine typography as other meta text. Directly beneath this label, a thin horizontal bar—4–6px in height—runs across roughly 60–70% of the content column width, leaving a clean margin on the right side that reinforces the minimalist feel. The bar is mostly a faint track with a bolder segment showing progress. The entire progress block is low in height (around 32–40px total) with ample white space above and below, and it visually anchors the start of the interactive flow without competing with the main question card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the vertical flow, moving with the page scroll and not fixed or sticky; it always precedes the current question card within the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The answer state manager operates within the boundaries of the question card, specifically in the right-hand text/answers column. It has no visible container, border, or label of its own; instead, it subtly changes the visual state of individual answer options (e.g., weight of the text, a fine outline, or a light background tint) when selected or hovered. The spatial footprint exactly matches the area in which the list of answers is rendered—starting just below the question text and extending downward as far as the list requires—usually occupying the right 60–65% of the quiz card’s width. Vertical spacing between answer options remains generous (12–16px) regardless of state, maintaining the airy editorial style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-structural behavioral layer bound to the answer list within the question card; it does not alter the layout flow or dimensions, only the visual states of elements already present.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The question view is the primary quiz card and sits roughly 24–32px below the progress indicator. It spans the full width of the main content column and is composed as a balanced, low-profile two-column layout that echoes the article card from the mockup. The left column, taking about 35–40% of the width, contains a landscape-oriented image (e.g., a room, furniture composition, or pattern) with a consistent aspect ratio (around 3:2). This image is aligned flush left and vertically centered relative to the question text, with subtle top and bottom margins so it doesn’t feel boxed in. The right column, occupying about 60–65% of the width, houses the question copy and answer options. At the top of this column is a small label or category line in fine, uppercase text, followed by the main question set in a larger, editorial-style type with 8–12px spacing between these two lines. Below the question, after a gap of around 16–20px, a vertical list of answer options appears; each option is a full-width, low-chrome row within this right column, with about 16px internal padding and 12–16px gaps between options. No heavy containers wrap the entire card; at most a very subtle outline or none at all, letting white space define the card edges. The overall card height is kept compact to fit comfortably within a single screen when possible, with about 24px of padding above and below its contents and a 24–32px gap below before the navigation/meta row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, block-level component that participates in the normal vertical flow between the progress indicator above and the navigation/meta row below; no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation controls form a slim meta row anchored directly beneath the question card, separated from it by a 16–24px vertical gap and optionally a very fine horizontal rule that spans the full width of the main column. This row is laid out horizontally across the entire column width. On the left side, aligned with the left edge of the content column, are low-key meta elements: a Back link and, optionally, a small question index such as ‘2 / 8’, styled in the same fine, understated type as other meta text. On the right side, aligned with the column’s right edge, sits the primary action control (Next, Continue, or See My Results). This control is visually distinct but restrained—perhaps a thin-bordered button or a subtly filled rectangle—sized to be around 40–48px tall, but not excessively wide, leaving white space to its left. The vertical height of the entire row is modest (about 48–56px including padding), reflecting the compact meta row from the mockup and providing a clear visual endpoint for each question screen.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal row that stays directly beneath the question card; it scrolls with the page and does not fix to the viewport edges.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Validation messaging appears in the same visual region as the question answers, but as a minimal line of text rather than a dedicated block. When active (e.g., the user tries to proceed without selecting an answer), a short message is displayed below the lowest answer option, left-aligned with the right-hand column of the question card and indented to match the answer text. It sits roughly 8–12px beneath the final answer and about 8–12px above the navigation/meta row, ensuring it is clearly associated with the question while not crowding the controls. The message uses small, refined typography with subtle emphasis (e.g., a fine red or muted accent color) but no background panel, icons, or heavy borders, preserving the light editorial feel and adding only a small amount of vertical height when present.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears within the existing vertical flow under the answers; it does not reflow other sections beyond slightly shifting the navigation row downward when visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submit handler has no distinct visual footprint; it is conceptually attached to the primary action control in the navigation/meta row at the end of the quiz. When the user is on the final question, the right-aligned button in the navigation controls changes its label to something like ‘See My Results’ but retains the same size and alignment within the existing row. The submit handler simply wires this button to submission and redirection logic without adding extra panels, banners, or fields. Spatially, it leverages the same concise meta row at the bottom of the question card, ensuring submission feels like a natural continuation of the question flow rather than a separate form step.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual behavioral layer associated with the primary action button in the navigation row; it does not alter layout, positioning, or sizing of any components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error banner appears at the very top of the quiz content area, directly below the site’s global header/navigation and above the quiz intro, separated from the header by about 24–32px of white space. It spans the full width of the centered main column (approximately 1100–1200px), maintaining the same left and right margins as the rest of the content. Visually, it is a low, horizontal strip with a subtle, very light tinted background and a thin rule at its top or bottom edge, echoing the mockup’s slim dividers. The error message text is left-aligned, sitting on a single line or two with 16–20px of vertical padding inside the strip, and no heavy iconography; if an icon is used, it is small and aligned to the baseline of the text. The banner’s total height remains modest (around 40–56px), with at least 24–32px of space beneath it before the quiz intro, so it reads as a refined editorial alert rather than a dominant warning panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level alert that pushes the intro and subsequent content downward when present; it does not stick to the viewport or overlap other components.

name: Style Results
title: Style Results
description: Displays the user’s recommended interior design style based on the quiz result and provides navigation into products filtered by that style.

core_features: Resolve the recommended style using the URL parameter "styleKey" (and optionally validate it against the "StyleQuizResult" referenced by "styleQuizResultId").
Display the recommended style’s name, description, and a primary image or moodboard sourced from the "Style" entity.
Provide a primary "Shop This Style" action that navigates to products.html with style-based and category-based filtering using URL parameters (e.g., styleKey and categoryId).

Highlight key product categories that are relevant for the recommended style (for example decor or living room furniture), and provide category-specific navigation into products.html with styleKey and categoryId parameters.
Optionally show a small set of preview products associated with the recommended style, to give users a sense of how the style looks in actual items.
Allow users to retake the style quiz by navigating back to style_quiz.html.
Allow users to share their style result using share actions that include a URL back to this page with the relevant styleKey (and optionally styleQuizResultId).
user_workflows: Workflow: Landing on Style Results from the quiz completion. Step 1: User completes the quiz on style_quiz.html. Step 2: style_quiz.html redirects to style_results.html with URL parameters styleKey and styleQuizResultId. Step 3: On load, the page reads styleKey and styleQuizResultId. Step 4: The page fetches the corresponding StyleQuizResult (if styleQuizResultId is provided) and the Style whose key matches styleKey. Step 5: The recommended style summary (name, description, moodboard image) is displayed. Step 6: User chooses either the main "Shop This Style" button or one of the category cards to continue into filtered products.html listings.

Workflow: Shopping the recommended style (main CTA). Step 1: On style_results.html, the user clicks the main "Shop This Style" button. Step 2: The page constructs a navigation URL to products.html, including styleKey from the current page and a default categoryId (as defined in the outgoing connection, for example "decor"). Step 3: Browser navigates to products.html?styleKey={currentStyleKey}&categoryId=decor, where the products page uses these parameters to filter results.

Workflow: Shopping a specific category in the recommended style. Step 1: On style_results.html, the user views a grid of style-highlighted category cards (such as a card representing sofas and seating that maps to a specific categoryId such as living_room). Step 2: The user clicks one of the category cards. Step 3: The page navigates to products.html with styleKey set to the current style and categoryId set based on the chosen category (for example living_room for sofas and seating). Step 4: products.html loads and shows products filtered by both styleKey and categoryId.

Workflow: Retaking the quiz. Step 1: On style_results.html, the user clicks the "Retake Quiz" control. Step 2: The page navigates to style_quiz.html without additional parameters (or with any parameters required by the quiz implementation). Step 3: The user completes the quiz again, and on completion the flow repeats, returning to style_results.html with new styleKey and styleQuizResultId parameters.

Workflow: Sharing the style result. Step 1: On style_results.html, the user clicks a sharing action (for example "Copy Link" or a generic share button). Step 2: The page constructs a shareable URL that includes the current styleKey and, if available, styleQuizResultId as URL parameters so that the recipient can see the same style result. Step 3: Depending on the share option, the page either copies this URL to the clipboard, opens a mail or messaging protocol handler with the URL prefilled, or triggers a native share dialog where supported.
interactions: Interaction: Page load initialization. When style_results.html loads or when URL parameters change, the page reads styleKey and styleQuizResultId from the URL. If styleKey is present, it attempts to fetch the Style with the corresponding key and, if styleQuizResultId is present, the StyleQuizResult tied to that ID. Based on these responses, the page either shows the result, shows a loading state, or an error/recovery state if the parameters are invalid or data cannot be retrieved.

Interaction: Clicking the main "Shop This Style" button. When the user clicks this button, the page constructs a URL to products.html including styleKey from the current page state and a default categoryId that corresponds to a general or decor-focused category (as defined in the outgoing navigation). The click event triggers a client-side redirect to that URL.

Interaction: Clicking a style-highlighted category card. Each category card is bound to a specific categoryId representing a top-level Category (for example a living room or decor category). When the card is clicked, the component reads the current styleKey from the page state and navigates to products.html with both styleKey and the card’s categoryId as URL parameters.

Interaction: Clicking "Retake Quiz". When the user clicks the retake control, the event handler performs a navigation to style_quiz.html. It does not rely on any client-side storage; instead the quiz page starts a fresh quiz session based solely on its own logic.

Interaction: Triggering a share action. When the user selects a share option, the component constructs a share URL based on the current document location, ensuring it includes styleKey and, when available, styleQuizResultId. It then performs the appropriate action: for example copying the URL to the clipboard, opening a mailto: link with the URL in the body, or using a browser sharing API where supported.

Interaction: Optional preview product clicks (if enabled). If the page includes a limited preview of products for the recommended style, each preview tile can expose an event when clicked. The handler may either navigate directly to a product detail page using a URL parameter such as product.html?id={productId}, or navigate to a filtered products.html view emphasizing that product, depending on the global navigation patterns.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver is the URL parameter "styleKey". When the page initializes, it reads styleKey from the query string (for example style_results.html?styleKey={value}&styleQuizResultId={id}). If styleKey is present, the page attempts to fetch the Style whose key matches styleKey. If styleQuizResultId is also present, the page additionally fetches the StyleQuizResult with that id and can optionally validate that its recommendedStyleKey matches styleKey; if they do not match, the page can prioritize the server-derived recommendedStyleKey from the StyleQuizResult over the raw URL styleKey value by updating the in-page styleKey state and, if desired, updating the URL via navigation. If no valid styleKey can be resolved (no param or no matching Style), the page shows a recovery state instructing the user to retake the quiz, and may provide a direct link to style_quiz.html. All navigation actions from this page construct their target URLs purely through URL parameters rather than any localStorage or other persistent client state; for example, the main "Shop This Style" navigation uses the current in-page styleKey state and a fixed or configuration-derived categoryId (such as decor) to form products.html?styleKey={currentStyleKey}&categoryId=decor. Category-specific navigation behaves similarly but substitutes the categoryId tied to the clicked category card. The share feature reads the currently effective styleKey and optional styleQuizResultId from the page state and serializes them into the share URL, ensuring that reloading or visiting that URL later reconstructs the identical style result view.

components.functionality: Initializes and maintains page-level state for the style results view by reading URL parameters (styleKey and styleQuizResultId), fetching the corresponding Style and StyleQuizResult records, and exposing a resolved recommended style state to child components. It also manages loading and error states when style data cannot be resolved.

Displays the recommended style’s key details based on the resolved page state, including style name, descriptive text, and a primary visual (moodboard or representative image). If style data is loading or unavailable, it shows appropriate loading or fallback messages. It does not fetch data itself but receives style information from the state resolver.

Provides the main call-to-action to shop products that match the recommended style. When activated, it navigates the user to products.html with URL parameters styleKey (taken from the current resolved style) and a default categoryId (such as decor, in line with the outgoing navigation definition). It does not perform any data fetching, only URL construction and navigation.

Shows a grid or list of key product categories relevant to the recommended style (for example categories corresponding to living room seating or decor). For each category, it displays the category’s name and image sourced from the Category entity, and binds a navigation action so that clicking a category navigates to products.html with styleKey set to the currently resolved style and categoryId set to the selected Category’s key or id (e.g., living_room or decor as per the outgoing connections). The component can be configured to include only a subset of categories that should be highlighted on the style results page.

Optionally displays a limited preview of Products associated with the recommended style to help users immediately visualize the style in real items. It consumes the current styleKey from the page state and requests a small subset of Product records that have a matching styleKey, optionally constrained to one or more Category values (such as decor or living_room). Each preview item shows basic product information and can optionally provide navigation into the broader products listing.

Provides a clear action for users who want to change or confirm their style by retaking the quiz. When the user activates the control, the component navigates directly to style_quiz.html, starting a new quiz flow without relying on any cached state.

Enables users to share their style result by generating a shareable URL that includes the current styleKey and, where available, styleQuizResultId. It can expose options such as copying the link to the clipboard, opening an email composition window with the link included, or invoking a native share dialog if supported. It does not store any share state outside the current session; it relies entirely on URL parameters for reconstructing the style view for recipients.

Handles cases where no valid style can be resolved from the URL parameters or data sources. It listens to error or empty-state signals from the state resolver and, when triggered, displays explanatory text and a navigation option that directs the user to style_quiz.html to obtain a new style recommendation.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a centered fixed-width layout on a 12-column grid (approx. 1100–1200px content width) with generous outer margins. Content within each major section appears to follow a simple 2-column grid (image vs text) for article listings.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom editorial flow within a centered, fixed-width content column (~1100–1200px). Global header/navigation sits full-width at the very top (outside this spec). Immediately beneath, the Style Results content is stacked as distinct sections: (1) the state-resolver decides whether to show the main stack or the error fallback, (2) a style summary block that mirrors the author block pattern (visual on the left, explanation on the right), (3) a thin horizontal rule and a compact meta/CTA row with the primary 'Shop this style' navigation button on the right, (4) a style category grid as the primary discovery section, (5) a product preview strip below it as a secondary, more detailed preview of items, (6) a retake-quiz CTA presented as a low-density editorial card, and (7) a slim share panel row at the bottom. In error states, all of this is replaced by a single centered fallback panel.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom editorial flow within a centered, fixed-width content column (~1100–1200px). Global header/navigation sits full-width at the very top (outside this spec). Immediately beneath, the Style Results content is stacked as distinct sections: (1) the state-resolver decides whether to show the main stack or the error fallback, (2) a style summary block that mirrors the author block pattern (visual on the left, explanation on the right), (3) a thin horizontal rule and a compact meta/CTA row with the primary 'Shop this style' navigation button on the right, (4) a style category grid as the primary discovery section, (5) a product preview strip below it as a secondary, more detailed preview of items, (6) a retake-quiz CTA presented as a low-density editorial card, and (7) a slim share panel row at the bottom. In error states, all of this is replaced by a single centered fallback panel.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content column is centered on the page and uses roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, constrained to ~1100–1200px. Within this column, major sections (summary, CTA/meta row, category grid, product strip, retake CTA, share panel) are separated vertically by 32–48px. Inside each component, padding is in the 16–24px range, with 8–12px between typographic elements. Where two-column layouts appear (style summary avatar/image vs text, category tiles, retake CTA image vs copy), the left column occupies about 35–40% of the width and the right column 60–65%, following the image-left/text-right split from the mockup. Divider rules are thin, light, and span the full content width, reinforcing separation without adding visual weight.
layout_strategies.content_density: Overall density stays deliberately light and editorial. Only a few core elements are visible at once above the fold: the style summary and the primary 'Shop this style' CTA, with the category grid starting to peek in. Each block has ample breathing room: generous white space around and within the style summary, clear separation before the grid, and plenty of margin around the retake and share sections.
