Grids and product strips avoid tight, e-commerce-style packing; items are spaced with 16–24px gutters and limited to a small number of elements per row so that imagery and typography feel refined and calm rather than dense or transactional.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow preserves the mild Z-pattern from the mockup. The eye starts at the top-left of the main content column (style name and summary heading), travels right across the summary text, then drops diagonally down to the primary visual anchor (style image/mood thumbnail on the left), then back across to the explanatory copy on the right. From there, the eye moves downward to the slim rule and scans the meta/CTA row from left to right, ending on the prominent 'Shop this style' button at the far right. Below that, the flow continues down into the style category grid (left-to-right scanning of tiles), then into the horizontally oriented product preview strip. Finally, attention drops to the retake-quiz card and ends on the subtle share panel at the bottom, which provides an understated closing gesture.

layout.overall_description: The Style Results page reads like a minimalist editorial feature within a centered magazine layout rather than a typical shop grid. Beneath the global header, a generous band of white space introduces a refined style summary block: on the left, a contained image or icon representing the user’s recommended interior style; on the right, a clean stack of the style name, a short descriptor, and a concise explanation of why this style fits the user. Everything is framed within a fixed-width column, with thin horizontal rules and 32–48px vertical spacing organizing the sections. A delicate divider line runs across the page, leading into a slender meta row where subtle contextual information sits on the left and the primary ‘Shop this style’ CTA is anchored on the far right, echoing the mockup’s ‘Read More >’ treatment. Below, the page opens into a lightly populated style category grid—large, airy tiles arranged in a two-column structure—followed by a low-slung strip of product previews that hint at specific pieces without overwhelming the layout. Near the bottom, a softly bordered retake-quiz card echoes the image-left/text-right article pattern, and a final thin rule introduces a minimal share row. The result is a calm, spacious composition that guides the user from understanding their style to shopping it, all within a restrained, monochrome, design-studio aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This non-visual controller component sits conceptually at the top of the main content column, immediately below the global header and above all other Style Results components. It occupies the full width of the centered content area (~1100–1200px) but renders as either (a) a lightweight loading/skeleton state that mimics the eventual layout’s vertical rhythm (placeholder blocks where the style summary, CTA row, and first section of the grid will appear), (b) the full primary stack of components (summary, CTA/meta row, grid, product strip, retake CTA, share), or (c) the error fallback panel alone. Spacing around the resolver’s visible states matches the rest of the page: at least 32px from the global header above and 32–48px before the next major section or page footer below. In success states, this component visually disappears as its children maintain the editorial spacing and grid alignment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard flow container within the centered fixed-width column, switching between content states without affecting the page’s global alignment or scroll behavior. It does not fix or float; it simply determines which child layout (main content vs error fallback vs loading skeleton) occupies the main content region.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The style summary panel is the first visible section of the Style Results content, sitting directly below the global header and any page title, with 32–48px of white space above it. It spans the full width of the centered content column (~1100–1200px) but organizes its interior using a two-column layout reminiscent of the author block in the mockup. On the left, occupying roughly 35–40% of the width (about 4/12 columns), is a vertically centered style visual: a square or slightly portrait image, moodboard, or abstract icon set against ample padding, optionally with a subtle border or background tint. On the right, taking up 60–65% of the width (about 8/12 columns), is a stacked text column: the style name in a bold, but thin-weight, serif or sans headline at the top, followed by a short tagline or style descriptor, then a concise 2–3 line explanation of why this style was recommended by the quiz. Text elements are separated by 8–12px line spacing, and the entire panel is padded internally by 16–24px on all sides, maintaining calm, open white space. The bottom edge of this panel sits about 32px above the slim horizontal rule that introduces the meta/CTA row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows naturally within the main vertical stack as a standard block-level section. On smaller viewports, the two columns collapse into a single column with the visual on top and text below, but the centered alignment and generous vertical spacing are preserved. No fixed or sticky behavior; it scrolls with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary 'Shop this style' CTA is integrated into a slim meta row directly beneath the style summary panel. A thin, light grey horizontal rule spans the full width of the content column first, with 16px of vertical spacing above and below it. Immediately under this rule, a low-height row (roughly 40–56px tall) stretches from left to right. On the left side of this row, using about 50–60% of the width, is understated meta text—such as the style confidence score, key style tags (e.g., 'Warm minimalism, Neutral palette'), or a short label like 'Your recommended interior direction'. On the right, aligned to the far right edge of the content column, sits the 'Shop this style' element itself, visually echoing the mockup’s 'Read More >' link but treated as the main action: either as a minimally styled button or an enlarged, high-contrast text link with a subtle arrow (e.g., 'Shop this style >'). It hugs the right margin with 16–24px of vertical breathing room above and below, creating a strong end point for the eye’s horizontal scan. The row does not feel crowded; there is clear space between the left meta text and the right CTA, reinforcing the editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Remains in the normal vertical flow between the summary panel and the style category grid. It does not stick to the viewport or float; on scroll, it moves away like any other row. On narrow screens, the meta text stacks above and the CTA aligns full-width below, still respecting 16–24px vertical spacing and centered alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The style category grid sits below the CTA/meta row, separated from it by approximately 32–40px of vertical whitespace to maintain section clarity. It spans the full width of the centered content column and uses a two-column grid reminiscent of the article image/text split from the mockup. Each column occupies about 50% of the content width with a 24px gutter between them, or, in grid terms, two equal groups of 6 columns within the 12-column system. Within this grid, each category (e.g., 'Living Room', 'Bedroom', 'Dining') appears as a tall, airy tile: an image block at the top about 60–65% of the tile’s height, and a clean text label plus optional one-line descriptor beneath. Tiles have 16–24px internal padding and a subtle border or drop shadow is avoided in favor of a thin outline or none at all, staying true to the minimalist editorial style. Vertically, tiles are spaced by 24–32px, with only a few visible rows to keep density low. The entire grid feels more like a structured editorial index than a heavy e-commerce catalog.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies a standard block in the main vertical stack. It expands vertically as more categories are present, pushing subsequent content downward. On smaller viewports, the grid collapses to a single column with tiles stacked vertically while maintaining the same generous vertical spacing and thin borders. No fixed or sticky behavior; it scrolls normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product preview strip appears directly beneath the style category grid, with about 32–48px of vertical spacing separating the two sections, reinforcing that this is a secondary, more detailed exploration layer. It stretches across the full width of the centered column but maintains a relatively low vertical profile, akin to a horizontal editorial strip. Across this width, 3–4 product cards are arranged in a single row. Each card occupies roughly 20–25% of the content width (adjusting based on the total number of items) with 16–24px gutters between them. The cards are kept visually light: a product image on top with a consistent aspect ratio and no heavy shadows, followed by 1–2 lines of product name in small, refined type and an optional short meta line (e.g., category or price) below. There is ample whitespace around the strip, with a subtle section heading on the left above the first card (e.g., 'Featured pieces in your style'), and no heavy separators beyond possibly one thin rule above the heading mirroring the mockup’s use of dividers.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Renders as a standard horizontal row within the vertical stack. On smaller viewports, the strip becomes a horizontally scrollable rail, with the first card aligned to the left margin and gentle overflow handling, or it can wrap into two rows of 2 cards each while preserving generous spacing. It does not fix to the viewport; it moves with the page scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The retake-quiz CTA is positioned near the bottom of the content stack, below the product preview strip, with a generous 40–48px of vertical spacing to clearly separate it as a reflective, secondary action. It mirrors the article card pattern from the mockup: a horizontally oriented block roughly the same width as the style summary panel, with a left visual column (~35–40% width) and a right text column (~60–65% width). The left side may contain a simple illustration, icon, or abstract block of color suggesting a new start or quiz interface, framed with 16–24px internal padding and surrounded by white space. On the right, the text column includes a small heading like 'Not quite right?' or 'Want to try a different look?', followed by a brief 1–2 sentence explanation and a low-key inline link or small button 'Retake the style quiz'. Typographic hierarchy is softer than the main style summary: slightly smaller heading, regular-weight body copy, and a subtle link treatment, so it feels inviting but not dominant. Overall, the block feels like a calm, editorial callout rather than a large promotional banner.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits in the normal vertical flow after product content. On smaller screens, it collapses to a single column with the visual above the text, preserving internal padding and 24–32px spacing between its internal elements. There is no fixed or sticky behavior; it scrolls away like a regular content card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The style share panel forms the closing band of the page’s main content column. It is introduced by another thin horizontal rule that spans the full width, placed about 32px below the retake-quiz CTA to gently signal the end-of-article meta area, echoing the mockup’s bottom meta row. Below this rule, a low-height horizontal row (roughly 40–48px) runs left to right. On the left side, aligned with the column grid, sits a small label such as 'Share your style results', set in modest typography. On the right, aligned to the far right edge, are compact share targets (icons or text links) arranged horizontally with 8–12px spacing, maintaining a sparse feel. The panel uses minimal height and avoids heavy icons or colored badges, favoring monochrome or single-accent treatments that harmonize with the rest of the minimalist layout. It reads as a soft, last interaction rather than a feature section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored at the bottom of the main content stack, it scrolls normally and does not stick to the viewport. On small screens, the label and share options stack vertically with the label on top and the icons centered below, maintaining 16–24px spacing and the same thin divider above.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error fallback panel is displayed only when the state-resolver cannot load a valid style result. In that state, it replaces the entire main content stack and sits directly beneath the global header, centered within the same fixed-width content column. Visually, it appears as a single, self-contained card occupying roughly 40–50% of the content column’s height and 60–70% of its width, centered horizontally with generous white margins on all sides. Inside the card, there is 24–32px of padding, with a short, clear error headline at the top, a brief explanatory paragraph below, and a single prominent action (e.g., 'Retry' or 'Return to quiz') aligned to the right or centered, in keeping with the light editorial style. The card may have a very subtle border or a faint background tone but no heavy shadows, maintaining the system’s minimalist, magazine-like feel. The surrounding page area remains clean and uncluttered, focusing attention on this message.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Rendered in place of the normal content within the main flow. It is vertically centered within the first viewport-height where possible by using generous top and bottom margins, but it still scrolls normally if content exceeds the viewport (e.g., on very small screens). It does not overlay or modalize the rest of the page; instead, it occupies the same core column that the normal Style Results content would use.

name: Custom Furniture Overview
title: Custom Furniture Overview
description: This page loads and presents information about custom furniture services, including benefits and example projects, and provides navigation to the shelving planner, design services, and related journal content.

core_features: Fetch and display introductory text about custom furniture using getCustomFurnitureOverviewContent.
Fetch and display a list of benefits of custom furniture using getCustomFurnitureOverviewContent.
Fetch and display example custom furniture projects (title, description, image URL) using getCustomFurnitureOverviewContent.
Provide a primary call-to-action button to open the shelving planner at shelving_planner.html with mode=new.
Provide secondary navigation links to design_services.html for booking consultations related to custom pieces.
Provide navigation links to blog.html filtered by the storage tag for inspiration and journal content.
user_workflows: 1) User lands on custom_furniture.html without URL parameters. 2) On page load, the page calls getCustomFurnitureOverviewContent. 3) The intro text, benefits list, and example projects are rendered from the response. 4) The user scans the intro and benefits to understand the service. 5) The user clicks the primary "Design Your Shelving" button. 6) The browser navigates to shelving_planner.html?mode=new.

1) User lands on custom_furniture.html and scrolls to the benefits section. 2) The page has already loaded getCustomFurnitureOverviewContent and populated the benefits list. 3) The user expands or focuses a specific benefit item (e.g., via click). 4) The component updates the URL to include a benefit-focused parameter (e.g., ?section=benefits&benefitIndex={index}). 5) If the user reloads or shares the URL, the page reads these parameters on load and scrolls/focuses the corresponding benefit item after fetching content.

1) User lands on custom_furniture.html with URL parameter section=projects. 2) On load, the page calls getCustomFurnitureOverviewContent and stores exampleProjects in state. 3) Once data is loaded, the page checks the section URL parameter and automatically scrolls to the example projects gallery. 4) The user clicks an example project item. 5) The gallery component updates the URL with projectId={selectedProjectIndexOrId}. 6) The component highlights or expands the selected project details based on this URL parameter. 7) If the user reloads or shares the URL, the same project remains highlighted or expanded after content is reloaded.

1) User is reviewing example projects on custom_furniture.html. 2) The user decides they need broader help than shelving and looks for related services. 3) The user clicks a link or button in the related services area. 4) The browser navigates to design_services.html, optionally including a referrer parameter like ?source=custom_furniture to allow that page to contextualize the visit. 5) The user proceeds with booking on the design_services.html page.

1) User views the benefits or example projects and wants more inspiration. 2) The user clicks a journal inspiration link labeled to indicate storage or shelving ideas. 3) The browser navigates to blog.html?tag=storage so that the blog page can filter articles using the storage tag. 4) The user reads relevant journal articles on the blog page.
interactions: On page load: The page calls getCustomFurnitureOverviewContent once, without parameters. When the promise resolves, the introText, benefits, and exampleProjects are stored in page state and passed to child components for rendering.

Click on "Design Your Shelving" primary button: Triggers a navigation event that changes location to shelving_planner.html?mode=new. No additional API calls are performed on this page as a result of this click.

Click on a benefit item: Updates internal component state to mark the clicked benefit as active/expanded and updates the URL query string with section=benefits and benefitIndex set to the zero-based index or identifier of the benefit. The component reads from getCustomFurnitureOverviewContent.benefits to render content and never uses hardcoded benefit text.

Click on an example project card: Updates internal state to mark that project as selected and updates the URL query string with section=projects and projectId set to either the project index or a stable identifier derived from the data (e.g., a slug-like transformation of the title). The component reads from getCustomFurnitureOverviewContent.exampleProjects to render both the list and the selected project details.

URL parameter change (e.g., via browser back/forward or direct link): The page listens for URL changes. When parameters such as section, projectId, or benefitIndex change, the relevant components re-evaluate the URL, adjust which section is in focus or scrolled into view, and update which benefit or project is highlighted/expanded. No additional API calls are made; components reuse the data already loaded from getCustomFurnitureOverviewContent.

Click on "Design Services" related link: Triggers a navigation event to design_services.html, optionally appending a query parameter like ?source=custom_furniture&context=shelving to allow that page to infer the context. This page does not interpret or store any additional data for this action.

Click on "Journal: Storage Inspiration" link: Triggers a navigation event to blog.html?tag=storage. This page does not request or render article data directly; article retrieval is handled on blog.html.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary data state is the response object from getCustomFurnitureOverviewContent, containing introText, benefits, and exampleProjects. This is fetched once on page load. The URL query string is used to control which section is emphasized and which items in lists are considered selected or expanded. Supported URL parameters include: section, which can be set to values such as 'intro', 'benefits', or 'projects' to indicate which section should be scrolled into view or focused after content loads; benefitIndex, which is a numeric index used to mark a specific benefit in the benefits list as active/expanded (only applied when section=benefits); projectId, which identifies the selected example project (e.g., via zero-based index or a stable identifier derived from project data) when section=projects; and optional referrer parameters such as source or context that are passed through when navigating away but are not interpreted by this page. On initial load, the page fetches getCustomFurnitureOverviewContent and waits for the data to be available. After data is loaded, the page reads the current URL parameters and updates scroll position and component state to match. When the user interacts (clicks a benefit or example project, or jumps between internal sections), the page updates the URL query string using pushState/replaceState without causing a full reload. Browser navigation (back/forward) is handled by listening to popstate events and reapplying state based on the updated URL, using the already-fetched content data without re-calling getCustomFurnitureOverviewContent.

components.functionality: On page initialization, this component calls getCustomFurnitureOverviewContent, stores the resolved introText, benefits, and exampleProjects in page-level state, and exposes these values to child components. It also tracks loading and error states.
event_handlers: onLoad: Triggered when the page is first loaded; initiates the getCustomFurnitureOverviewContent API call.
onDataLoaded: Triggered when getCustomFurnitureOverviewContent resolves; updates shared state with introText, benefits, and exampleProjects and notifies dependent components.

onError: Triggered if getCustomFurnitureOverviewContent fails; updates error state so dependent components can render an appropriate functional error message (e.g., a generic 'content unavailable' state) without hardcoded content details.

components.functionality: Displays the introductory description of custom furniture services. It strictly renders the introText provided by getCustomFurnitureOverviewContent and does not embed any static descriptions.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When introText changes in shared state (after initial load or potential refresh), the component re-renders the intro content.
onNavigateToSection: When the URL parameter section=intro is detected (on load or via popstate), this component is instructed to scroll into view or gain focus to align with the URL state.

components.functionality: Displays the list of benefits for custom furniture as interactive items that can be expanded, collapsed, or highlighted. It uses the benefits array from getCustomFurnitureOverviewContent and allows the URL to control which benefit is active via benefitIndex.
event_handlers: onItemClick: Triggered when a benefit item is clicked; toggles the active/expanded state for that specific index and updates the URL query parameters to section=benefits and benefitIndex={clickedIndex} using pushState or replaceState.
onUrlParamChange: Triggered when the query string changes (such as via back/forward navigation); reads section and benefitIndex from the URL and updates which benefit is active/expanded accordingly.

onDataChange: Triggered when the benefits array is updated by the data loader; the component re-renders the benefit list and, if section=benefits and benefitIndex is present, activates the corresponding benefit after rendering.

components.functionality: Renders exampleProjects as a gallery or list of project items. Each item can be selected to show more detailed information (e.g., expanded text) within the component, with selection controlled by the projectId parameter in the URL.
event_handlers: onProjectClick: Triggered when a project item is clicked; sets the clicked project as selected in internal state and updates the URL query parameters to section=projects and projectId to a value that uniquely maps to this project (e.g., its index or an identifier derived from its title) using pushState or replaceState.

onUrlParamChange: Triggered when URL parameters change via browser navigation or internal updates; if section=projects and projectId matches an existing project, the component updates selection state to highlight or expand that project.

onDataChange: Triggered when exampleProjects is updated by the data loader; the component rebuilds its internal project list and attempts to reselect a project based on the current projectId parameter, if present.

components.functionality: Provides the main call-to-action to open the shelving planner in 'new' mode. It does not depend on dynamic content and always navigates to shelving_planner.html with mode=new.
event_handlers: onClick: Navigates the browser to shelving_planner.html?mode=new. Optionally, additional query parameters such as source=custom_furniture or referrerSection (derived from the current section parameter) may be appended to support context on the target page, but no extra API calls are made here.

components.functionality: Displays navigation options to related services and content: a link to the general design services page and a link to the journal/blog page filtered by storage-related content. The links use URL parameters but do not fetch service or article data themselves.
event_handlers: onDesignServicesClick: Navigates to design_services.html, optionally including source=custom_furniture and context values inferred from current URL parameters (such as section or projectId) to provide context to the next page.
onJournalStorageClick: Navigates to blog.html?tag=storage. The component does not interpret or display article data; it only constructs the navigation URL.

onSectionContextChange: Optionally updates any contextual query parameters appended to outgoing links when the current section or selected project/benefit changes, so that downstream pages can know where the user came from without altering core behavior.

components.functionality: Coordinates URL query parameters with section-level behavior across the page. It interprets section, benefitIndex, and projectId from the URL and directs relevant components (intro, benefits list, projects gallery) to scroll into view or update their internal state accordingly.
event_handlers: onLoad: After getCustomFurnitureOverviewContent finishes and content is rendered, reads the initial URL parameters and triggers appropriate actions (e.g., scroll to intro when section=intro, scroll to benefits and focus benefitIndex when section=benefits, scroll to projects and select projectId when section=projects).
onPopState: Listens to browser popstate events; when the user navigates back or forward, re-reads the URL parameters and updates child components to keep the UI in sync with the URL.

onSectionChangeRequest: When a child component wants to switch the primary section (for example, a 'view projects' internal link), this handler updates the URL query parameters (section and any relevant index/ID) and notifies other components of the change without causing a full page reload.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a centered fixed-width layout on a 12-column grid (approx. 1100–1200px content width) with generous outer margins. Content within each major section appears to follow a simple 2-column grid (image vs text) for article listings.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the full-width site header/navigation, all components sit inside a single, centered main content column with a fixed max-width of roughly 1100–1200px. Within this column, content follows a vertical, top-to-bottom stack: first the intro section (acting like the 'author block' analog), then a thin rule and the benefits accordion, then another rule and the example projects gallery (using the left-image / right-copy split), and finally a slim horizontal meta row where related service links sit on the left and the primary shelving planner CTA sits on the right, echoing the mockup's date/category vs 'Read more' pattern.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the full-width site header/navigation, all components sit inside a single, centered main content column with a fixed max-width of roughly 1100–1200px. Within this column, content follows a vertical, top-to-bottom stack: first the intro section (acting like the 'author block' analog), then a thin rule and the benefits accordion, then another rule and the example projects gallery (using the left-image / right-copy split), and finally a slim horizontal meta row where related service links sit on the left and the primary shelving planner CTA sits on the right, echoing the mockup's date/category vs 'Read more' pattern.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content column occupies about 60–70% of the viewport width and is centered with generous side margins. Each major section (intro, benefits, gallery, meta row) is separated by 32–48px of vertical spacing and often a thin horizontal rule. Inside sections, internal paddings and gaps follow a 16–24px rhythm, with 8–12px between text lines. The example projects gallery uses a two-column split where the image takes about 35–40% of the horizontal space on the left and the descriptive text about 60–65% on the right, closely mirroring the mockup's article card.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density remains deliberately low throughout the page. Each major section is visually isolated with ample whitespace above and below, and the number of simultaneous focal points is kept minimal: one intro block, one benefits stack, a single featured project (or one project at a time in a gallery), and a simple meta row. Controls (accordion toggles, gallery navigation, CTA) are visually light and unobtrusive, emphasizing typography, whitespace, and imagery over heavy UI chrome.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow begins at the site header, then drops into the intro section where the eye is first caught by a small visual anchor on the left (e.g., icon or thumbnail) and then drawn to the right into headline and introductory copy, forming the initial Z. From there, the eye moves down into the benefits list for more detailed content, then further down to the example projects gallery where the left-aligned project imagery pulls attention before moving right into the descriptive text. Finally, the eye is led to the bottom meta row, scanning from left-aligned related services links to the right-aligned primary shelving planner CTA, clearly signaling the next action.

layout.overall_description: The page reads like a minimalist editorial feature in a design magazine: a centered, fixed-width column framed by generous white margins on both sides. Below the slim global header, a calm intro block introduces the custom furniture offering with understated typography and a small visual cue on the left, giving an immediate but unhurried overview. A fine horizontal rule and generous vertical spacing guide the eye down into a clean, linear benefits accordion that feels more like a series of sectioned articles than a dense FAQ. Another rule separates this from a wide, low-slung example projects gallery that pairs a large, left-aligned project image with spacious, right-aligned copy, echoing the mockup’s article card pattern. At the very bottom of the content column, a slim meta row stretches across the width: on the left, lightly styled navigation links to related services; on the right, the prominent yet refined shelving planner CTA, mirroring the mockup’s date/category and 'Read More >' pairing. Invisible structural components (loader and section router) orchestrate data and state without disrupting the clean, continuous vertical rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader occupies the same centered main content column as all other sections but is visually present only when data is being fetched. When active, it overlays or temporarily replaces the interior of the content column starting just below the global header, spanning the full column width (approximately 1100–1200px) and extending downward just far enough to mimic the page’s main structures. Use a skeleton-style layout: a wide horizontal bar at the top representing the intro heading, followed by a stack of narrower bars hinting at paragraph text, then a larger rectangular placeholder on the left and several text bars on the right to suggest the future example project card. Vertical spacing between these placeholders follows the 16–24px rhythm, with 32–40px of breathing room at the top and bottom so the loader feels integrated into the minimalist editorial layout rather than like a heavy overlay. When loading completes, this component collapses completely and yields its space to the intro section and subsequent components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits at the top of the main content column in normal flow when active, optionally using a semi-transparent overlay within the column; collapses to no height and becomes non-visible when not loading.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned immediately below the site’s full-width header/navigation, the intro section is the first visible block of the main content column. It is horizontally centered with a max-width of about 1100–1200px and 32–48px of whitespace above and below. Internally, it echoes the mockup’s author block: on the far left, within the column, a compact visual element (e.g., a circular avatar-style icon, a small furniture detail photo, or a logo mark) sits at roughly 64–80px square, aligned to the top of the text content. To its right, starting about 24px away, a vertical stack of text fills roughly 70–75% of the column width: a small, subtle label (e.g., 'Custom Furniture') in light-weight, all caps or small caps; then a bolder, larger headline spanning one or two lines; then a short lead paragraph describing the service. Line spacing within this text stack uses 8–12px gaps, while the distance between the label, headline, and paragraph steps up to 16–24px. The overall effect is a horizontally anchored left visual element balancing a right-aligned text column, creating the beginning of the Z-pattern and setting the editorial tone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, participates in the normal vertical document flow as the first major section beneath the global header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The benefits accordion list sits directly below the intro section, separated from it by 32–40px of vertical whitespace and a thin, full-width horizontal rule that spans the main content column. The component itself aligns with the same centered column (max-width ~1100–1200px). At the top of the benefits list, an optional section label or small heading sits flush left, with 16–24px of space below before the first accordion item. Each accordion item runs nearly the full width of the column, with 16–24px internal padding on all sides. Items are divided by subtle hairline rules or 1px borders, maintaining the minimalist editorial feel. The accordion header row (benefit title) is left-aligned, with the disclosure icon or plus/minus control tucked close to the right edge but visually lightweight. When an item is expanded, the body copy flows directly underneath the header within the same card, with the text constrained to approximately 70–80% of the column width to keep lines readable, leaving a comfortable right margin of white space. Vertical spacing between lines of copy is about 8–12px, and between stacked items about 16–24px, so even with several benefits the list feels airy and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked vertically in normal flow beneath the intro, expanding and collapsing within its allocated column width without shifting the overall page layout beyond natural height adjustments.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The example projects gallery is placed below the benefits list, separated from it by 32–48px of whitespace and another thin horizontal rule spanning the main content column. It reuses the mockup’s article card pattern: within the centered column (max-width ~1100–1200px), a two-column layout is established. On the left, occupying about 35–40% of the column width, a large landscape-oriented image represents a featured custom furniture project. This image aligns flush left within the column and sits on a clean white background, with 16–24px of internal padding from any container edges if used. On the right, taking about 60–65% of the width, a vertical stack of text mirrors the article copy: a small project label (e.g., project type or year), a bold project title, and a concise description of the brief and solution, with optional 1–2 lines of key details. Spacing between these text blocks is 12–20px, echoing the mockup’s title-to-paragraph rhythm. If the gallery supports multiple projects, only one project is fully visible at a time: navigation dots or previous/next controls sit subtly below or to the lower right of the text column, small and low-contrast, to preserve the minimalist look. The entire card has 24–32px of space above and below before the next section, maintaining the impression of a single, prominent feature rather than a dense gallery grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main column and part of the normal vertical flow; internal navigation changes the featured project within the fixed left-image/right-text frame without shifting the component’s position on the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shelving planner primary CTA is positioned in the bottom meta row of the page, echoing the mockup’s 'Read More >' alignment. This meta row sits immediately below the example projects gallery, separated by 24–32px of whitespace and a thin horizontal rule across the main content column. Within this row, which spans the full column width, the CTA is right-aligned: visually it appears as a refined text-based button or subtle outlined button containing the main action label (e.g., 'Open Shelving Planner >'). It sits horizontally aligned with the related services links on the left, so the row reads left-to-right as contextual navigation followed by a clear primary action. The CTA itself occupies only about 15–20% of the row’s width, leaving generous white space between it and the related links cluster. Vertical height of the row is modest (around 48–64px total), reinforcing the slim meta feel while ensuring enough breathing room above and below the CTA text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and inline within the bottom meta row, right-aligned; it participates in normal flow and does not stick or float as the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component shares the same bottom meta row as the shelving planner CTA and is anchored to the left side of the main content column, directly mirroring the mockup’s date and category cluster. Positioned just below the example projects gallery and aligned with the column’s left edge, it appears after a thin horizontal rule and 24–32px of whitespace. The links are presented as a compact, editorial-style inline list: for example, a small label such as 'More services:' followed by 2–3 text links (e.g., 'Design Services', 'Shelving Planner', 'Journal') separated by thin vertical dividers or centered dots. The typography is modest—smaller size, lighter weight—so these links feel like supporting metadata rather than competing calls-to-action. The entire left cluster occupies roughly 40–50% of the row’s width, leaving the remainder visually open, with the primary CTA on the far right. Vertical alignment ensures the link text baseline lines up with the CTA text baseline, creating a clean, continuous horizontal line across the meta row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and left-aligned within the bottom meta row, sharing the same horizontal band as the shelving planner CTA and participating in the normal document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section router is a non-visual, structural component that coordinates URL state with the visible sections on the page. Conceptually it sits at the top of the main content column, just above or logically wrapped around the intro, benefits list, example gallery, and meta row, but it does not occupy any visible space or alter the centered column layout. Its primary spatial effect is indirect: when the URL indicates a specific sub-section (e.g., a particular benefit, project, or planner state), the router may scroll the viewport to the corresponding section or set which accordion item is expanded or which project is active, all while preserving the linear, top-to-bottom arrangement. No additional margins, paddings, or visual dividers are introduced by this component.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-intrusive; it does not render any UI or take up layout space, but coordinates scroll position and component state within the existing static, centered layout.

name: Shelving Planner
title: Shelving Planner
description: Configure a custom shelving unit by setting wall dimensions, finish, and modules, with live price updates and options to save and edit designs.

core_features: Initialize or load the current shelving configuration using getCurrentShelvingConfiguration based on URL parameters (new or edit mode).
Allow users to input and update wall width and height in centimeters, and apply them to the shelving configuration via setShelvingDimensions.
Provide a finish selector for the shelving (e.g., different material/finish keys) and apply the chosen finish to the configuration using setShelvingFinish.
Enable adding modules (shelf, cabinet, drawer) to the current configuration using addShelvingModule, updating the module list and total price.
Enable removing modules from the configuration using removeShelvingModule, updating the module list and total price.
Display an interactive layout representation of the configuration using module data (type, positionIndex, widthCm, heightCm) returned from the interfaces.
Show the current total price and currency of the configuration and automatically refresh this information after any change.
Provide a user-entered budget field and indicate whether the configuration total price is within or exceeds the entered budget (without hardcoding any budget value).
Offer a Save Design / Save to Wishlist action that opens a naming prompt, then persists the configuration using saveShelvingConfiguration.
After saving, confirm success using data returned by saveShelvingConfiguration and optionally provide navigation to account.html with the designs section parameter.
Provide navigation back to the Custom Furniture overview (custom_furniture.html) and to the list of saved designs (account.html?section=designs) using link interactions.
user_workflows:
Start a new shelving design from Custom Furniture:
1. User clicks a link or button on custom_furniture.html that navigates to shelving_planner.html with URL parameter mode=new.
2. Shelving Planner reads mode from the URL and calls getCurrentShelvingConfiguration.
3. The page initializes with an empty or default configuration returned by getCurrentShelvingConfiguration.
4. User enters wall width and height values and submits the dimensions form.
5. The page calls setShelvingDimensions with the entered dimensions and updates the configuration state and total price from the response.
6. User selects a finish option; the page calls setShelvingFinish with the selected finishKey and updates finish and total price from the response.
7. User adds modules (for example shelves, cabinets, drawers) using add controls. Each click calls addShelvingModule with the selected moduleType.
8. After each addShelvingModule response, the page updates the module list, layout preview, and total price.
9. User optionally enters a target budget in the budget field. The page compares the live total price with this budget and displays whether the configuration is within or above the budget.
10. User iteratively adjusts dimensions, finish, and modules (including removing modules) until satisfied with both layout and total price.

Edit an existing saved design from Account & Favorites:
1. On account.html, the user selects a saved shelving configuration to edit; navigation goes to shelving_planner.html with mode=edit and configurationId set in the URL.
2. Shelving Planner reads mode and configurationId from the URL.
3. On initialization, the page calls getCurrentShelvingConfiguration, which returns the existing configuration corresponding to the URL parameters.
4. The dimensions form, finish selector, module list, layout preview, and price summary are pre-populated from the returned configuration data.
5. The user updates dimensions, finish, or modules as needed:
   - Changing dimensions calls setShelvingDimensions.
   - Changing finish calls setShelvingFinish.
   - Adding modules calls addShelvingModule.
   - Removing modules calls removeShelvingModule.
6. After each change, the configuration state and total price are updated from the interface responses.
7. When ready, the user clicks Save Design; the page prompts for a design name (pre-filling with the existing name if present) and calls saveShelvingConfiguration with the confirmed name.
8. On success, the page displays a confirmation and offers a link to manage designs on account.html?section=designs.

Adjust design to meet a budget constraint:
1. At any time in either new or edit mode, the user enters a numeric value into the budget input field to represent their target budget.
2. The page stores this budget value in page state (not in URL or external storage) and compares it to the currently displayed totalPrice of the configuration.
3. If totalPrice is less than or equal to the budget, the page displays an in-budget indication; if totalPrice is greater than the budget, it displays an over-budget indication.
4. The user then modifies the configuration to reach their goal:
   - Removing modules by clicking remove on specific modules; each action calls removeShelvingModule and updates totalPrice.
   - Optionally changing finish or dimensions if those influence totalPrice via setShelvingFinish and setShelvingDimensions.
5. After every change, the budget indicator is recalculated against the updated totalPrice, giving the user immediate feedback on budget fit.

Save design and review it in Account & Favorites:
1. With a configuration prepared (new or edited), the user clicks the Save Design / Save to Wishlist button.
2. The save dialog opens requesting a name. If the configuration already has a name, it is pre-filled; the user can modify it.
3. The user submits the form; the page calls saveShelvingConfiguration with the entered name.
4. The page uses the response (success flag, configurationId, name, totalPrice, currency, savedAt, message) to show a confirmation state.
5. If saveShelvingConfiguration indicates success, the page shows a link or button allowing the user to navigate to account.html with URL parameter section=designs.
6. If the user clicks this link, the browser navigates to account.html?section=designs, where the saved configuration can be managed.
interactions:
Page initialization:
- Event: onInit (page load).
- Behavior: Read mode and configurationId from the current URL query string. Call getCurrentShelvingConfiguration. Use the returned configuration data to populate dimensions, finish, modules, and price summary. Set internal page mode to "new" or "edit" according to URL mode parameter, defaulting to "new" if not provided or invalid.

Dimensions form submission:
- Event: onSubmitDimensions.
- Trigger: User enters width and height values (in centimeters) and submits the dimensions form.
- Behavior: Validate that width and height are present and numeric. Call setShelvingDimensions with widthCm and heightCm. On success, update the configuration state with returned widthCm, heightCm, and totalPrice. Reflect dimension changes in the layout preview and price summary.

Finish selection change:
- Event: onChangeFinish.
- Trigger: User selects a finish option from a list or dropdown.
- Behavior: Call setShelvingFinish with the selected finishKey. On success, update configuration finishKey, finishLabel, and totalPrice in state. Update the finish display and price summary.

Add module actions:
- Event: onClickAddModuleShelf / onClickAddModuleCabinet / onClickAddModuleDrawer (or a generic onClickAddModule with a moduleType parameter).
- Trigger: User clicks an add button corresponding to the desired module type.
- Behavior: Call addShelvingModule with moduleType matching the button (e.g., "shelf", "cabinet", or "drawer"). On success, replace the current modules array in state with the returned modules, update totalPrice, and refresh the layout preview and price summary.

Remove module actions:
- Event: onClickRemoveModule.
- Trigger: User clicks a remove control associated with a specific module in the module list or layout.
- Behavior: Read the moduleId from the clicked module. Call removeShelvingModule with that moduleId. On success, update the modules array and totalPrice from the response, and refresh the layout preview and price summary.

Budget input change:
- Event: onChangeBudget.
- Trigger: User types or changes a numeric value in the budget input field.
- Behavior: Store the current budget value in page state. Compare the budget value numerically to the current configuration totalPrice. Update budget status display to indicate whether totalPrice is within or over the budget. Recompute this status whenever totalPrice changes from any interface call.

Save design dialog open:
- Event: onClickOpenSaveDialog.
- Trigger: User clicks Save Design / Save to Wishlist button.
- Behavior: Open a modal or inline form requesting a design name. Pre-fill the name field with the current configuration name when available.

Save design form submission:
- Event: onSubmitSaveDesign.
- Trigger: User submits the design name form.
- Behavior: Validate that a name string is provided. Call saveShelvingConfiguration with the entered name. On success, store configurationId, name, totalPrice, currency, savedAt, and message from the response in state. Show a success confirmation state with the provided message and name. If success is false, display the error message and keep the dialog open for correction or retry.

View saved designs navigation:
- Event: onClickViewSavedDesigns.
- Trigger: User clicks a link or button labeled such as View Saved Designs either in a persistent location or within the save confirmation state.
- Behavior: Navigate the browser to account.html with query parameter section=designs (e.g., account.html?section=designs).

Breadcrumb navigation back to Custom Furniture:
- Event: onClickBreadcrumbCustomFurniture.
- Trigger: User clicks breadcrumb or navigation link back to Custom Furniture overview.
- Behavior: Navigate the browser to custom_furniture.html without additional parameters.

Handling URL parameter changes:
- Event: onUrlChange (if user navigates via browser controls or internal links that change URL parameters without full reload).
- Behavior: If the URL query string changes to a different mode or configurationId while on this page, re-run initialization logic: interpret mode and configurationId, call getCurrentShelvingConfiguration, and replace the current configuration state with the new data.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is primarily driven by URL parameters and interface responses. URL parameters: mode and configurationId. On load, the page reads these from the query string. If mode=edit and a configurationId value is present, the page considers itself in edit mode and calls getCurrentShelvingConfiguration, which is expected to return the configuration associated with that identifier in the user's context. If mode is absent or not equal to "edit", the page assumes new mode, calls getCurrentShelvingConfiguration, and uses whatever default or newly created configuration the interface returns. The configuration state includes: configurationId, name, widthCm, heightCm, finishKey, finishLabel, modules (array with moduleId, moduleType, positionIndex, widthCm, heightCm, price), totalPrice, currency, and lastUpdatedAt. After each modifying interface call (setShelvingDimensions, setShelvingFinish, addShelvingModule, removeShelvingModule), the page overwrites the relevant parts of this state with values from the response. The internal page mode (new/edit) is used to decide whether to pre-fill the save dialog with an existing name. The URL configurationId parameter is not directly passed into any interface (only read for mode determination); any server-side logic within getCurrentShelvingConfiguration is responsible for aligning URL parameters with configuration data. No configuration data is stored in browser storage mechanisms; the authoritative configuration is always based on the latest interface response for the current session. A separate, transient budget value is maintained only in in-memory page state, not in the URL or via interfaces; it is used solely for UI comparison with totalPrice. Navigation away from this page (to account.html or custom_furniture.html) is performed via standard hyperlinks that include or omit URL parameters as defined, and returning to shelving_planner.html with appropriate mode and configurationId will cause the configuration to be re-loaded via getCurrentShelvingConfiguration.

components.functionality: Handles initial page setup by parsing URL parameters, determining whether the planner is in new or edit mode, calling getCurrentShelvingConfiguration, and populating shared configuration state for all other components.
Provides navigation back to the Custom Furniture overview and access to the saved designs section in the account area.
Displays and edits the wall width and height for the current shelving configuration, submitting changes via setShelvingDimensions and updating the configuration accordingly.
Allows the user to select a shelving finish and applies the selection to the configuration by calling setShelvingFinish, updating finishKey, finishLabel, and totalPrice.
Provides controls to add different module types (shelf, cabinet, drawer) to the current configuration using addShelvingModule and refreshes the module list and pricing data.

Displays the current modules belonging to the configuration, using their type, positionIndex, widthCm, and heightCm, and provides remove controls that call removeShelvingModule for the selected module.
Shows the configuration's totalPrice and currency, and updates after every interface call that can influence pricing.
Captures a user-defined budget amount and compares it to the current totalPrice, displaying whether the configuration is within or over the budget.
Provides a Save Design / Save to Wishlist button that, when clicked, opens the save-design-dialog component.

Prompts the user for a design name, pre-filling it from the current configuration when available, and saves the configuration using saveShelvingConfiguration. On success, it exposes confirmation data to other components (such as a saved-state banner or navigation link).

Displays the result of saveShelvingConfiguration, including success state, configurationId, name, totalPrice, currency, savedAt, and message, and can provide a direct link to manage designs in account.html.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a centered fixed-width layout on a 12-column grid (approx. 1100–1200px content width) with generous outer margins. Content within each major section appears to follow a simple 2-column grid (image vs text) for article listings.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a single, centered content column (approx. 1100–1200px wide) beneath the site’s full‑width header/nav. Inside this column, content flows top-to-bottom: a slim breadcrumb row, then a compact introductory initializer block, followed by a large, two-column planner card. In this card, the shelving visualization (modules-list-layout) sits on the left, while configuration forms (dimensions-form, finish-selector) stack vertically on the right. Directly below this planner card, a thin horizontal rule introduces a meta-style bottom row that groups price-summary and budget-control on the left and the save-design-trigger on the far right. The dialog and confirmation banner overlay this structure when active but do not disrupt the underlying linear arrangement.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a single, centered content column (approx. 1100–1200px wide) beneath the site’s full‑width header/nav. Inside this column, content flows top-to-bottom: a slim breadcrumb row, then a compact introductory initializer block, followed by a large, two-column planner card. In this card, the shelving visualization (modules-list-layout) sits on the left, while configuration forms (dimensions-form, finish-selector) stack vertically on the right. Directly below this planner card, a thin horizontal rule introduces a meta-style bottom row that groups price-summary and budget-control on the left and the save-design-trigger on the far right. The dialog and confirmation banner overlay this structure when active but do not disrupt the underlying linear arrangement.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content column occupies roughly 60–70% of the viewport width and is centered with generous side gutters. Vertical spacing between major sections (breadcrumbs → initializer → planner card → meta row) is generous (32–48px). Within each block (labels, fields, text lines), spacing is more compact (8–16px). In the planner card, the left visualization column takes about 35–40% of the content width, while the right control column uses about 60–65%. The bottom meta row is shallow in height, echoing the thin horizontal meta treatment from the mockup, and stretches across the full content width.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout maintains a light, editorial feel: few components are visible at once and each has room to breathe. The initializer block is concise, the planner card shows a single prominent viewport of the shelving system alongside a focused stack of controls, and the meta row presents only essential price and action information. Controls and forms are grouped logically to avoid clutter, with clear white space between groups rather than a dense application-style panel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mimics the mockup’s mild Z-pattern. The eye starts at the site logo/nav (outside this spec), moves down slightly to the left-aligned breadcrumbs, then into the initializer heading and copy. From there, the eye drops to the strong visual anchor of the shelving preview on the left, then travels horizontally to the right-hand configuration forms. Finally, the gaze moves down to the bottom meta row, scanning price and budget on the left and terminating at the save-design-trigger on the far right. The confirmation banner and save dialog temporarily redirect attention but are aligned with this central column, preserving the overall flow.

layout.overall_description: Within a centered, fixed-width content column, the Shelving Planner page reads like a minimalist editorial feature rather than a dense configurator. A slim breadcrumb line sits just under the site header, followed by a compact introductory block that quietly explains what the planner does. Below a comfortable vertical gap, a large planner card dominates the page: on the left, a generous shelving visualization area anchors the layout; on the right, a clean column of dimension and finish controls mirrors the copy column from the mockup. A thin horizontal rule then separates this main card from a shallow meta row along the bottom, where the price and budget sit understated on the left while the save action is isolated on the far right, echoing the mockup’s date/category versus ‘Read More’ tension. When saving, a centered modal gently darkens the surroundings, and a slim confirmation banner temporarily appears near the top of the content column, all while respecting the restrained, monochrome, high‑whitespace aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The initializer block is positioned near the top of the main content column, directly below the planner-breadcrumbs with approximately 24–32px of vertical space between them. It spans the full width of the central content column (roughly 1100–1200px) and adopts an author-block-like composition: on the left, a compact visual element or icon area (about 72–96px wide) sits flush with the content column’s left edge; to its right, occupying the remaining 80–85% of the block, a vertical stack of text (page title, short description, and any subtle helper text) is left-aligned. Padding inside the block is around 16–24px on all sides, with 8–12px between text lines. The block is visually separated from the large planner card below it by a generous 32–40px of whitespace, optionally capped at the bottom by a thin, low-contrast horizontal rule that cues the transition into the main configuration area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the normal document flow, expanding vertically with content but maintaining its position relative to breadcrumbs above and the planner card below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb navigation sits at the very top of the planner’s main content area, just below the site’s global header/navigation, aligning left within the centered content column. It occupies a single horizontal row, no more than 40–56px high, and spans nearly the full width of the content column with ample left and right margin to the viewport edges. The breadcrumb text is small and understated, with links and separators arranged in a single line. There is approximately 24–32px of whitespace above it (to the global header) and 16–24px of space below it before the shelving-planner-init block, reinforcing a clear but subtle separation of hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, flowing at the top of the content column; it scrolls away with the rest of the page and does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The dimensions-form is anchored in the upper portion of the right-hand column of the main planner card. The planner card itself begins approximately 32–40px below the bottom edge of the initializer block, potentially introduced by a thin horizontal rule. Within the card, the right column occupies roughly 60–65% of the content width and starts flush with the right side of the centered column, leaving 35–40% to the left visualization column. The dimensions-form sits at the top of this right column, spanning its full width, with a clean vertical stack of labels and inputs. Internal padding is about 16–24px; vertical spacing between individual form fields is around 8–16px, ensuring a light yet structured feel. There is 16–24px of whitespace below the dimensions-form before the finish-selector begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column of the planner card; it scrolls naturally with the page and defines the upper anchor point of the control stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The finish-selector is positioned directly below the dimensions-form in the same right-hand column of the planner card, maintaining the same column width (about 60–65% of the content area). A vertical gap of about 16–24px separates it from the bottom of the dimensions-form, visually grouping them while keeping each module distinct. The selector’s internal layout is a compact vertical list or grid of finish options, but the outer block maintains generous side padding (16–24px) and moderate vertical spacing (8–12px) between options to preserve the minimalist aesthetic. The bottom of the finish-selector aligns roughly with the lower half of the shelving visualization on the left, so the two columns feel balanced in height rather than top-heavy on one side.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the stacked control column, occupying normal flow beneath dimensions-form and above the visual end of the planner card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The modules-toolbar sits in the left column of the planner card, immediately associated with the shelving visualization. It occupies the full width of the left column (approximately 35–40% of the content width) and is relatively shallow in height, similar to a caption or tool strip. Positioned directly above the modules-list-layout, it appears as a slim horizontal band with minimal text or icon buttons aligned to the left and possibly one or two secondary controls aligned to the right, echoing the meta-row structure. Vertical spacing above the toolbar from the top of the planner card is modest (16–24px), while the shelving preview below it starts after an 8–16px gap, making the toolbar feel tightly connected to the visual canvas without crowding it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column of the planner card; on taller pages it scrolls away with the rest of the content and does not remain fixed over the visualization.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The modules-list-layout forms the main visual anchor of the page and occupies the bulk of the left column in the planner card. It sits directly below the modules-toolbar with a small 8–16px gap. The left column takes about 35–40% of the total content width and extends vertically to roughly match or slightly exceed the height of the stacked controls on the right, giving the impression of a large landscape-oriented canvas showing the shelving on a stylized wall. Internally, the preview area is open with generous padding around the actual shelving representation, reinforcing the spacious editorial feel. The right edge of the preview aligns visually with the start of the right-hand control column, and the bottom edge aligns closely with the bottom of the finish-selector so the entire card reads as one cohesive rectangle containing image-left, controls-right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, acting as the core visual panel within the planner card; it scales vertically with its content but remains bounded by the left column within the centered layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The price-summary sits in the meta-style bottom row directly beneath the main planner card. A thin, low-contrast horizontal rule stretches across the content column, positioned approximately 24–32px below the bottom of the planner card. Immediately below this rule, the price-summary is aligned to the left edge of the content column within a shallow horizontal band. It occupies the left half of this band (about 40–50% of the content width), presenting total price and key pricing details in one or two lines of small, refined type. There is 8–12px of vertical padding above and below the text to keep the band slim and understated, mirroring the elegance of the mockup’s date/category display.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline with the meta row content; it sits to the left of the budget-control and opposite the save-design-trigger across the same horizontal line.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The budget-control shares the same bottom meta row as the price-summary, positioned just to the right of it but still in the left segment of the content width. It either appears inline with the price-summary (e.g., to its immediate right) or stacked directly beneath it with 4–8px of vertical separation, depending on available width, but always aligned to the same left baseline. This creates a compact block occupying roughly the left 50–60% of the meta row, where price and budget considerations are grouped together. The control itself is minimal, with a single input or slider and a small label, respecting the slim vertical height (approx. 40–56px overall) of the meta row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the meta row, adjusting inline wrapping responsively but remaining anchored to the left side beneath the horizontal rule.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save-design-trigger is the focal action on the bottom meta row, mirroring the mockup’s ‘Read More >’ link. It is placed on the far right end of the same horizontal band that contains price-summary and budget-control. Horizontally, it aligns with the right edge of the content column, leaving sufficient whitespace between it and the budget block on the left so it reads as an independent action. Vertically, it sits centered within the shallow meta row, at the same line height as the primary price text. The button or link styling is minimal—likely text-based with a subtle chevron or ‘>’—and occupies no more than 15–20% of the content width, ensuring it is noticeable but not visually heavy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the meta row, aligned to the far right; it scrolls with the page and does not remain fixed once the user scrolls past the planner section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save-design-dialog appears as a centered modal overlay that temporarily supersedes the main planner content. When triggered, it sits in the middle of the viewport, aligned both horizontally and vertically, with a width of approximately 40–50% of the viewport (not exceeding the main content column width) and a maximum height that allows some margin above and below (at least 64px). Its internal layout is a simple vertical stack: title, brief instructional text, form fields (e.g., design name), and actions (Save/Cancel), each separated by 12–20px. Surrounding the dialog, a semi-opaque monochrome backdrop covers the full viewport, slightly dimming the planner card and meta row beneath without losing the sense of the centered column. The dialog edges align visually with the underlying grid, reinforcing the editorial geometry.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay anchored to the viewport center, sitting above all other components; it locks the background scroll while open and dismisses back to the normal document flow when closed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save-confirmation-banner is a slim horizontal band that appears near the top of the content area after a design is successfully saved. It spans the full width of the main content column (matching the width of the planner card) and is positioned just below the global header and typically above the planner-breadcrumbs, with a small vertical gap (8–16px) above and below so it doesn’t feel cramped. The banner height is modest (32–48px), with its message centered vertically and aligned slightly left of center horizontally, leaving ample whitespace around the text. Its visual treatment is subtle yet distinct enough—through a slight tonal shift or a thin rule—to be noticed without breaking the minimalist aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears dynamically; when visible, it occupies a static position in the normal flow between the header and breadcrumbs, pushing content below it downward. It scrolls away with the page and is not sticky.

name: Account & Favorites
title: Account & Favorites
description: Manage your basic account profile, view and manage favorited articles, and access saved custom shelving designs.

core_features: Load and display the single-user account profile using getAccountProfile, with fields for first name, last name, email, and password.
Create or update the account profile via a profile form that calls createOrUpdateAccountProfile on submit.
Route users between logical sections (Profile, Favorites, Designs) based on the URL parameter section (e.g., account.html?section=favorites).
Load a combined overview of favorited articles and saved shelving configurations using getAccountFavoritesOverview for quick counts and summaries.
Display a detailed list of favorited articles using getFavoriteArticles, including title, excerpt, tags, and saved date.
Allow users to remove an article from favorites from within the favorites list by calling removeFavoriteArticle and updating the list accordingly.
Display a detailed list of saved shelving configurations using getSavedShelvingConfigurations, including name, dimensions, finish label, and pricing information.
Allow users to rename a saved shelving configuration inline using renameSavedShelvingConfiguration and immediately reflect the updated name in the list.
Enable navigation from a favorite article entry to the article detail page (article.html) by passing the favoriteArticleId as the id URL parameter.
Enable navigation from a saved shelving configuration entry to the shelving planner (shelving_planner.html) in edit mode by passing mode=edit and the selected configurationId as URL parameters.
user_workflows:
View and update profile details:
1. User navigates to account.html (e.g., from header "Account" link or footer "Account & Favorites").
2. On page initialization, getAccountProfile is called.
3. If an existing profile exists (exists=true), the profile form is populated with firstName, lastName, and email; the password field remains empty for security and is used only for updating.
4. User edits one or more fields in the profile form.
5. User submits the form.
6. The page calls createOrUpdateAccountProfile with firstName, lastName, email, and password from the form.
7. On success, the form reflects the returned values, and the page can show a non-marketing confirmation state (e.g., inline status) without changing the URL.
8. If there are validation or API errors, they are displayed near the corresponding fields without leaving the page.

Access the Favorites section from navigation:
1. User clicks a "View Favorites" link elsewhere in the site that navigates to account.html?section=favorites.
2. On page load, the section router reads section=favorites and sets the active section to Favorites.
3. The favorites overview and detailed favorites list for articles are initialized.
4. getAccountFavoritesOverview is called to populate summary counts and quick links.
5. getFavoriteArticles is called to load the detailed list of favorited articles for display in the Favorites section.

Remove an article from favorites:
1. While in the Favorites section, the user locates a favorite article in the list.
2. User clicks a remove action (e.g., an icon or button) associated with that article.
3. The page calls removeFavoriteArticle with the favoriteArticleId associated with that list item.
4. On success (success=true), the article is removed from the in-memory list and the UI updates without a full page reload.
5. Optionally, the favorites overview section is updated (using either the previous response or a refreshed getAccountFavoritesOverview call) to reflect the new remainingFavoritesCount.

Open a favorite article in detail view:
1. In the Favorites section, the user clicks on a favorite article card or title.
2. The click handler reads the favoriteArticleId (and optionally articleId if needed for internal logic) from that list item.
3. The browser is navigated to article.html?id={favoriteArticleId} using the outgoing navigation definition.
4. article.html is responsible for interpreting the id parameter and showing the full article content.

View saved shelving designs from the Designs section:
1. User navigates to account.html?section=designs, or switches to the Designs tab inside account.html.
2. The section router sets the active section to Designs.
3. getAccountFavoritesOverview may be called (if not already loaded) to provide summary information.
4. getSavedShelvingConfigurations is called to retrieve the list of saved configurations.
5. The list of configurations is displayed, each with name, finishLabel, dimensions (widthCm, heightCm), and pricing (totalPrice, currency).

Rename a saved shelving configuration:
1. In the Designs section, the user clicks an edit or rename action on a specific configuration entry.
2. The row or item transitions into an inline edit state for the name field (without changing the URL).
3. User enters a new name and confirms (e.g., presses a save button or hits Enter).
4. The component calls renameSavedShelvingConfiguration with configurationId and newName.
5. On success (success=true), the configuration name in the list updates to the returned name field for that configurationId.
6. The row exits edit mode and returns to the normal display state.

Edit a saved shelving configuration in the planner:
1. In the Designs section, the user clicks an "Edit in Planner" action on a configuration.
2. The click handler reads the configurationId for that list item.
3. The browser navigates to shelving_planner.html?mode=edit&configurationId={configurationId}.
4. The shelving planner page is responsible for loading and editing the configuration based on configurationId and mode=edit.

Navigate between Profile, Favorites, and Designs sections using tabs:
1. The account page displays a tabs navigation component (e.g., Profile, Favorites, Designs).
2. When the user clicks a tab, the tab component triggers a tab change event.
3. The section router updates the browser URL to account.html?section={selectedSectionKey} using pushState or by reloading with the new query parameter.
4. Based on the new section value, the relevant section components (profile form, favorites list, designs list) become active and fetch their data if not already loaded.

Handle initial load with unknown or missing section parameter:
1. User arrives at account.html with no section parameter or with an unsupported value.
2. The section router treats this as a default state and sets the active section to Profile (or a chosen default section), without changing the URL unless desired.
3. The profile form component initializes and calls getAccountProfile.
4. If the user later navigates to another section via tabs, the URL section parameter is updated to reflect that section.
interactions: Page initialization (onInit): read the section URL parameter and determine which section is active; call getAccountProfile if Profile section is active; call getAccountFavoritesOverview and getFavoriteArticles if Favorites section is active; call getAccountFavoritesOverview and getSavedShelvingConfigurations if Designs section is active.

Tab click in section navigation (onTabChange): update the URL to account.html?section={profile|favorites|designs} and update the active section state; trigger initialization of the newly active section if not already loaded.
Profile form load (onInit): call getAccountProfile and, if exists=true, populate the form fields with returned firstName, lastName, and email values; leave password empty for user input.
Profile form input change (onChange): update local component state for firstName, lastName, email, and password fields, enabling or disabling the submit action based on validation rules.

Profile form submit (onSubmit): validate required fields and then call createOrUpdateAccountProfile with the current form values; handle the response by updating the displayed profile data and showing validation or system error messages as needed.

Favorites overview initialization (onInit): call getAccountFavoritesOverview to retrieve favoriteArticles and savedShelvingConfigurations arrays; use their lengths and basic data to show counts and quick access links to the Favorites and Designs sections.
Favorites list initialization (onInit when favorites section becomes active): call getFavoriteArticles to retrieve the detailed list of saved articles for display in the Favorites section.
Click on a favorite article card or title (onClickArticleCard): navigate the browser to article.html?id={favoriteArticleId} using the favoriteArticleId associated with that item.
