Click on remove favorite article (onRemoveFavoriteArticle): call removeFavoriteArticle with the favoriteArticleId for that item; on success, remove the item from the in-memory list and optionally refresh the favorites overview via getAccountFavoritesOverview.
Designs list initialization (onInit when designs section becomes active): call getSavedShelvingConfigurations to retrieve all saved shelving configurations and display them in a list.
Click on rename configuration (onEnterRenameMode): toggle the corresponding configuration row into an editing state for the name field.

Save renamed configuration (onRenameSavedConfiguration): call renameSavedShelvingConfiguration with configurationId and newName; on success, update the corresponding list item's name with the returned name and exit edit mode; on failure, show an inline error and keep the row in edit mode.
Click on "Edit in Planner" for a configuration (onClickEditInPlanner): navigate the browser to shelving_planner.html?mode=edit&configurationId={configurationId} for the selected configuration.

URL change via browser navigation (onUrlChange, e.g., back/forward): re-read the section parameter, update the active tab, and ensure the correct section components are visible; if data for that section is not yet loaded, trigger the relevant get* interface calls.

Empty-state handling (onDataLoaded): when lists from getFavoriteArticles or getSavedShelvingConfigurations return zero items, display appropriate empty-state messaging within the section while allowing navigation and other account actions to remain available.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses the URL query parameter section as the primary external state for determining which section is active (Profile, Favorites, or Designs). Valid values include at least 'profile', 'favorites', and 'designs'. On initial load, the page reads section from the URL. If section is missing or not one of the supported values, it defaults to 'profile' without requiring a URL change. When users interact with the tab navigation, the tab component updates the section parameter in the URL (e.g., navigating to account.html?section=favorites) so that the current section is reflected in the address bar and supports browser back/forward navigation. Section-specific components initialize lazily: when section='profile', the profile form calls getAccountProfile; when section='favorites', the favorites components call getAccountFavoritesOverview and getFavoriteArticles; when section='designs', the designs components call getAccountFavoritesOverview if needed and getSavedShelvingConfigurations. Internal UI states such as loading, error, or inline edit modes (e.g., renaming a configuration) are handled in component state and do not modify the URL. Navigations to other pages are encoded as new URLs with parameters: clicking a favorite article navigates to article.html?id={favoriteArticleId}; clicking 'Edit in Planner' for a configuration navigates to shelving_planner.html?mode=edit&configurationId={configurationId}.

components.functionality: Determines which logical section (Profile, Favorites, Designs) is active based on the section URL parameter, coordinates initial data loading for that section, and responds to tab changes and browser navigation events.
Renders the top-level navigation tabs for Profile, Favorites, and Designs. Highlights the active tab based on the current section and updates the URL parameter when a different tab is selected.
Displays and edits the single-user account profile, including first name, last name, email, and password. Initializes from getAccountProfile and saves changes via createOrUpdateAccountProfile.

Shows a compact overview of the count and key information for favorite articles and saved shelving configurations using getAccountFavoritesOverview, and provides quick navigation controls to jump to the Favorites or Designs sections.

Displays the detailed list of favorited articles in the Favorites section using getFavoriteArticles, including title, excerpt, tags, and saved date. Allows users to open an article in article.html and to remove articles from the favorites list using removeFavoriteArticle.

Lists all saved custom shelving configurations in the Designs section using getSavedShelvingConfigurations. Supports inline renaming of configurations through renameSavedShelvingConfiguration and navigation to shelving_planner.html in edit mode for a selected configuration.

Monitors the results of getFavoriteArticles and getSavedShelvingConfigurations (via the overview or detailed lists) to determine when to display empty-state messaging and alternative actions within the Favorites and Designs sections.

Captures and displays non-field-specific errors from any of the account-related interfaces (getAccountProfile, createOrUpdateAccountProfile, getAccountFavoritesOverview, getFavoriteArticles, removeFavoriteArticle, getSavedShelvingConfigurations, renameSavedShelvingConfiguration) and provides retry actions where appropriate.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a centered fixed-width layout on a 12-column grid (approx. 1100–1200px content width) with generous outer margins. Content within each major section appears to follow a simple 2-column grid (image vs text) for article listings.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a single, centered main content column beneath the existing full-width site header/navigation. Within this column, layout follows a linear top-to-bottom stack: (1) the account-section-router as a slim, textual context/breadcrumb row, (2) the account-tabs-navigation as a horizontal tab row, (3) below the tabs, exactly one active section at a time, routed by the section-router: either the account-profile-form (Profile), or the Favorites view (favorites-overview-panel followed by favorite-articles-list and saved-shelving-configurations-list), with the favorites-empty-state-handler conditionally replacing the lists when empty. The account-page-error-boundary wraps the entire main content column and, in error state, replaces the inner content with a centered error message block. Inside each content section, we mimic the mockup’s two-column editorial pattern (visual element left, text right) for major cards/lists.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a single, centered main content column beneath the existing full-width site header/navigation. Within this column, layout follows a linear top-to-bottom stack: (1) the account-section-router as a slim, textual context/breadcrumb row, (2) the account-tabs-navigation as a horizontal tab row, (3) below the tabs, exactly one active section at a time, routed by the section-router: either the account-profile-form (Profile), or the Favorites view (favorites-overview-panel followed by favorite-articles-list and saved-shelving-configurations-list), with the favorites-empty-state-handler conditionally replacing the lists when empty. The account-page-error-boundary wraps the entire main content column and, in error state, replaces the inner content with a centered error message block. Inside each content section, we mimic the mockup’s two-column editorial pattern (visual element left, text right) for major cards/lists.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content column is centered and constrained to roughly 1100–1200px width on a 12-column grid, occupying about 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, with generous left and right margins. Vertical spacing between major blocks (router to tabs, tabs to profile/overview, overview to lists, between lists) uses 32–48px of space, often punctuated by a thin horizontal rule that runs the full width of the content column. Inside components, padding is about 16–24px, with 8–12px spacing between lines of text. In list rows and cards, roughly 35–40% of the horizontal space is allocated to imagery or thumbnails on the left and 60–65% to textual content on the right, echoing the mockup’s article card proportion.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density is deliberately low and editorial: only a few key blocks are visible at any time (router, tabs, a single main section with its immediate content). Each section uses generous whitespace, thin dividing rules, and light typography. Forms and lists avoid heavy chrome; they present a small number of high-value controls and information. Lists of favorites and shelving configurations show only essential metadata (title, brief description, simple meta row) with ample breathing room between rows, rather than dense tables or grids.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows the mockup’s mild Z-pattern. The eye starts at the top-left of the content column with the account-section-router label, then moves right across the tabs navigation. From there, attention drops down to the main section header block (profile heading or favorites-overview-panel). Within the favorites lists, each row pulls the eye first to the thumbnail on the left, then across to the title and summary on the right, and finally down to a bottom meta row that leads from left-aligned info (dates, counts, labels) to right-aligned actions (primary links like 'Open', 'Read article >', or 'Remove'). Error and empty states are centered in the column so they become the immediate focal point without breaking the overall calm, minimal hierarchy.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s full-width header, the Account & Favorites page presents a calm, centered editorial column floating in generous white space. At the very top of this column, a slim line of text quietly labels the context, aligned to the left, like a refined breadcrumb. Just beneath, a minimalist tab row stretches across the column, with a few evenly spaced tab labels that define the main modes: Profile, Favorites, and Saved Designs. The active tab’s content fills the space below while the rest remain hidden, keeping the page visually uncluttered. In the Profile view, a wide, soft-edged block mirrors an editorial author layout: a compact avatar and name cluster on the left third, with neatly stacked profile fields on the right two-thirds, all breathing comfortably with thin rules marking major breaks. In the Favorites view, an overview strip replaces the profile form, presenting a concise summary of favorited articles and saved shelving designs, again with a left-anchored visual icon or avatar and right-aligned text. A hairline rule spans the column, and below it favorite articles appear as a vertical sequence of magazine-like cards—each row has a wide, landscape thumbnail on the left and a clear title, one or two lines of description, and a trailing ellipsis on the right. A fine meta row under each card carries dates and categories on the left, and a 'Read article >' or 'Remove' action on the far right, echoing the mockup’s bottom meta strip. Another slim rule separates this from the saved shelving section, where each configuration is treated similarly: a schematic thumbnail on the left, name and dimensions on the right, and subtle actions at the bottom. When there’s nothing saved, the lists dissolve into a centered empty-state message block, keeping the same width and white-space rhythm. All of this is wrapped by an error boundary that, in failure states, replaces the content column with a simple, centered panel, preserving the clean grid and white-space logic. The overall effect is a restrained, magazine-like account page that feels more like an interior studio’s project index than a busy dashboard.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The account-section-router sits at the very top of the main content column, directly beneath the site’s global header/navigation and aligned flush with the left edge of the centered content area. It occupies a shallow horizontal band (about 40–56px tall) spanning the full width of the content column (roughly 1100–1200px), with generous top padding (32–48px from the global header) and about 16–24px of padding below before the tabs row. Visually, it behaves like a refined breadcrumb or section label: a single line or two of small, monochrome text that may show the current section name (e.g., 'Account & Favorites / Favorites') left-aligned, leaving ample empty space to the right. No heavy boxes or backgrounds are used; the router text simply sits on the white background, optionally anchored by a very subtle hairline rule beneath it that runs the full column width and aligns precisely with the grid. It does not scroll independently or stick; it moves with the page as part of the main document flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element within the main content flow; scrolls away naturally with the page and does not use sticky or fixed positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The account-tabs-navigation is positioned immediately below the account-section-router, separated by about 16–24px of vertical whitespace. It spans the full width of the centered content column, with tab labels aligned starting from the left and extending horizontally, each occupying roughly equal space but with ample breathing room between them. Vertically, the tab row is slim (around 48px in height), with 12–16px padding above and below the tab labels. A thin, continuous horizontal rule runs along the bottom of the tab row, visually grounding it and echoing the editorial navigation of the mockup’s top bar. The active tab is indicated through subtle typographic emphasis (slightly bolder text, small underline or rule) rather than heavy backgrounds, maintaining the minimalist, monochrome feel. There is about 32px of vertical space below the tabs row before the main section content begins, reinforcing separation. On scroll, the entire tabs row can remain lightly sticky within the main content column—staying pinned just below the global header—so that users can quickly switch between Profile, Favorites, and Saved Designs while viewing longer lists.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a horizontal navigation bar within the main column. On desktop, it uses CSS position: sticky, anchoring itself to the top of the content viewport just beneath the global header when the user scrolls down past it; otherwise, it sits in the normal flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The account-profile-form occupies the main content area directly beneath the tabs when the Profile tab is active. It sits centered in the column, starting about 32–40px below the tabs row. The form’s outer container spans the full width of the content column, with an internal padding of around 24px on all sides and, optionally, a faint bordering hairline or bottom-only rule to define it against the white background. Inside, the layout follows a two-column pattern reminiscent of the mockup’s author block: the left third (approximately 35% of the width) contains the user avatar (a simple circular image or placeholder) and key identity details (name, email, short label like 'Member since 2021') stacked vertically with 8–12px spacing. The right two-thirds (about 65% width) hosts the actual form fields. These fields are arranged in a single vertical stack aligned to the left within this right column, with labels set above each input, not beside them, to keep the structure airy and editorial. Each label-input pair has 12–16px vertical spacing, with slightly larger breaks of 24–32px between conceptual groupings (e.g., personal info vs. password). The bottom of the form includes a thin horizontal rule spanning only the right column or the full width (depending on visual preference), and a compact button row aligned to the right edge of the text column, following the mockup’s meta-row pattern: secondary text (e.g., 'Cancel') on the left of the button row, primary action button (e.g., 'Save changes') aligned to the far right. Ample whitespace (32–48px) separates the form’s bottom from any subsequent sections on the Profile tab.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears only when the Profile tab is active; otherwise, it is hidden or unmounted. Within its active state, it participates in the normal vertical flow and scrolls with the rest of the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites-overview-panel appears directly beneath the tabs row when the Favorites (or equivalent combined favorites/saved designs) tab is active. It starts about 32px below the tabs bar and spans the full width of the centered column, with 24px padding on all sides. This panel visually echoes the mockup’s author information block: the left 35–40% of the width is reserved for a compact visual anchor—either the user avatar reused, or a simple icon representing 'Favorites'—plus a short heading like 'Your Favorites' stacked with a small subheading line describing the section. The right 60–65% holds a concise, horizontally arranged summary: counts of favorited articles and saved shelving configurations, plus possibly a one-line description or status message. These count elements are set in a light, grid-like arrangement (e.g., two or three columns of stats) with 16–24px spacing between them, but ample white space remains around the text. A thin horizontal rule runs along the bottom edge of the panel across the full content width, clearly separating it from the lists below. The vertical spacing beneath this rule is around 32–40px before the first favorite article card appears, reinforcing hierarchy and giving the overview its own moment in the visual flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Displayed only when the Favorites tab is active and when not overridden by an error state; otherwise hidden. It is a standard block in the vertical flow and scrolls naturally with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorite-articles-list sits immediately below the favorites-overview-panel’s bottom rule when there are favorited articles to show. The section begins with a subtle, left-aligned label or heading ('Articles') positioned about 8–16px below the rule, followed by a vertical stack of article cards. Each card follows the mockup’s article layout: it occupies the full width of the content column and is built as a horizontal split. The left segment is roughly 35–40% of the column width and holds a landscape-oriented image thumbnail representing the article; the image has a fixed height (for example, 180–220px) and aligns flush to the left grid edge. The right segment (about 60–65% of the width) begins with the article title, set prominently near the top, then a short excerpt of one to three lines with about 8–12px line spacing, and ends with an ellipsis to emphasize the teaser nature of the content. The right column content is vertically centered or slightly top-aligned relative to the image, preserving a calm visual rhythm. Beneath each card, separated by about 12–16px of vertical space, lies a slim meta row spanning the same width: on the left side, small, muted text shows the publication date and category; on the right side, aligned flush with the right column edge, lives an inline 'Read article >' link and a more discreet 'Remove from favorites' text link. A very thin horizontal rule runs below each card’s meta row, full width, with approximately 24–32px of vertical space before the next card begins. The list can contain several such cards, but due to generous spacing and the editorial layout, only one to two cards are likely visible at a time above the fold, keeping perceived density low.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Rendered only when the Favorites tab is active and when at least one article is favorited; otherwise replaced (partially or completely) by the favorites-empty-state-handler. It scrolls vertically with the page as a standard list and does not employ any fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The saved-shelving-configurations-list appears below the favorite-articles-list within the same Favorites tab when there are saved designs. It is visually separated from the articles by a full-width hairline rule with 32–40px of vertical whitespace above and below. At the top of this section, a simple left-aligned heading such as 'Saved shelving designs' appears, with 8–12px space below it. Each configuration is presented as a refined horizontal card similar in structure to the article cards: the left 35–40% of the width holds a small, clean thumbnail—either a simplified line drawing or a muted image of the shelving configuration—possibly on a light, subtle background to distinguish it from pure white. The right 60–65% contains the configuration’s name as the primary text, a short one-line descriptor (dimensions, room type, or main material), and optionally a second line for status (e.g., 'Last edited on 12 Jan 2026') with 8–12px spacing between lines. Underneath this right-side content, a meta row runs across the width of the card: on the left, small text summarises key properties (e.g., width × height, number of shelves); on the right, a set of inline actions appears in order of importance ('Open design >' as primary, with secondary actions like 'Duplicate' and 'Remove' presented as lighter text links). Each configuration card is separated from the next by a thin horizontal rule and 24–32px of vertical space, ensuring that the list feels like a curated project index rather than a dense control panel. The entire saved-shelving-configurations-list is kept to the same width and grid alignment as the article list, preserving a consistent visual rhythm down the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Shown when the Favorites tab is active and there is at least one saved configuration; otherwise it may be omitted or consolidated into the empty-state experience. It sits in the standard vertical flow and scrolls with the page without any sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites-empty-state-handler takes over the content area below the favorites-overview-panel when there are no favorite articles and/or no saved shelving configurations. Instead of showing the regular lists, it renders a centered, lightweight block within the main content column. This block is constrained to about 50–60% of the column width and is centered horizontally, creating a narrower, more intimate column of text. It begins approximately 32–40px below the overview panel’s bottom rule to maintain the established vertical rhythm. Inside, a simple icon or abstract mark (monochrome, minimal) appears at the top, occupying a small portion of space, followed by a short title like 'No favorites yet' and a body line or two explaining how to add favorites or save shelving designs. Text lines are center-aligned to reinforce the state’s focus. Below the body text, there may be a single primary call-to-action link or button (e.g., 'Browse articles' or 'Start a shelving design') aligned to the center. Surrounding the message, generous white space above and below (at least 48–64px) keeps the page feeling open and calm. If only one list is empty (e.g., no shelving designs but some articles), this handler can render as a smaller block within that specific section, aligned to the left of the content column but still with ample whitespace and perhaps no icon, acting as an inline, editorial note within the structured flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally displayed in place of one or both lists on the Favorites tab, depending on the data state. It occupies standard block space within the flow and scrolls naturally; it does not overlay other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The account-page-error-boundary wraps the entire main content column, starting just below the global header and encompassing the account-section-router, tabs navigation, and all routed sections. Under normal conditions, it is invisible and does not add visual elements of its own. When an error is caught, it replaces the inner content with a single, centered error panel that respects the same grid and white-space logic as the rest of the page. This panel is placed in the center of the content column, with generous margins above and below (at least 64px of whitespace from the top of the viewport), and spans roughly 60–70% of the column width to avoid feeling heavy. The error message is minimal: a short heading ('Something went wrong'), a concise explanation line, and a simple action such as 'Try again' or 'Back to home', all left-aligned within the panel but visually centered within the page. A thin border or a single horizontal rule at the top of the panel is sufficient to delineate it, keeping in line with the restrained aesthetic. The rest of the page background remains white, and no other account components are visible while the error boundary is active, focusing attention solely on the error state without visual noise.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Functions as an overlaying state within the main column: in error mode, it suppresses or replaces all child components inside the content area while still occupying the same general page region. It scrolls with the page but is typically sized so that the error panel comfortably fits within the initial viewport height.

name: About Studio
title: About Studio
description: Page that loads and presents the studio’s story, design philosophy, team, and contact details using getAboutStudioContent, and routes users to service and journal sections of the site.

core_features: Load studio content (story, design philosophy, team members, contact details) from getAboutStudioContent on page initialization.
Display the studio story as a dedicated section that can be focused via URL parameters.
Display the design philosophy as a dedicated section that can be focused via URL parameters.
List team members with name, role, bio, and image, allowing deep-linking to a specific team member via URL parameters.
Show contact information (email, phone, location) and enable interaction (email, call, map) based on the loaded data.
Provide contextual navigation links from the About page to Design Services, Design Packages, Custom Furniture, and Journal pages, including passing URL parameters indicating the origin context.
user_workflows:
Explore studio overview:
1. User opens about.html (without URL parameters).
2. The page controller component calls getAboutStudioContent.
3. Once data is returned, the story, design philosophy, team overview, and contact sections are rendered.
4. The default section focus is determined (for example, story if no section parameter is present).
5. User scrolls or uses section navigation links to move between story, philosophy, team, and contact sections.
6. Clicking a navigation item updates the URL with ?section=<targetSection> and scrolls/focuses the corresponding section.

View design philosophy and go to Design Services:
1. User opens about.html?section=philosophy.
2. The page controller reads the section URL parameter and calls getAboutStudioContent.
3. After data is loaded, the page scrolls/focuses the philosophy section and highlights it in the section navigation.
4. User reads the philosophy content.
5. User clicks the link within the services navigation area that routes to design_services.html.
6. The click handler navigates to design_services.html with URL parameters that may include source=about and context=philosophy for downstream usage.

Learn about the team and focus on a specific member:
1. User opens about.html?section=team&teamMemberIndex=<index> from an external link or internal link.
2. The page controller parses section and teamMemberIndex from the URL and calls getAboutStudioContent.
3. After data is loaded, the team section is scrolled into view.
4. The team list component interprets teamMemberIndex and highlights the corresponding team member entry and/or opens the detail view component for that member.
5. User can click on another team member entry, which updates the URL to teamMemberIndex=<newIndex> and updates the detail view accordingly.

Use contact information to get in touch or visit the studio:
1. User opens about.html with any section parameter, or none.
2. The page controller calls getAboutStudioContent and passes the contact object to the contact section component.
3. Contact section renders email, phone, and location based on returned data.
4. User clicks the email action; the handler constructs a mailto link using contact.email and opens it in the user’s email client (no form submission).
5. User clicks the phone action; the handler constructs a tel link using contact.phone and triggers the device dialer where supported.
6. User clicks the location action; the handler navigates to a map URL generated using contact.location with the configured maps provider.

Navigate from About page to design packages, custom furniture, or journal:
1. User scrolls to the services and journal navigation area in about.html (section may be auto-focused via ?section=services or ?section=journal).
2. User clicks on the Design Packages link; the handler navigates to design_packages.html with URL parameters such as source=about and possibly sectionOrigin=<currentSection>.
3. Alternatively, user clicks the Custom Furniture link; the handler navigates to custom_furniture.html with similar origin parameters.
4. User clicks the Journal link; the handler navigates to blog.html with URL parameters such as source=about and context=philosophy if the user came from the philosophy section.
interactions: Initial page load: on page initialization, the controller component reads URLSearchParams (e.g., section, teamMemberIndex) and then calls getAboutStudioContent. No user action is required.

Section navigation click: when a user clicks a section navigation item (e.g., Story, Philosophy, Team, Contact, Services, Journal), the click handler updates the section parameter in the URL (using pushState or by reloading with the updated query) and scrolls to the corresponding section anchor.

Team member click: when a user clicks on a team member in the team list, the click handler updates the teamMemberIndex parameter in the URL while keeping section=team, and triggers the team-member-detail component to show that member’s details.

Email click: when a user clicks on the email element in the contact section, the click handler builds a mailto URL using the contact.email value from getAboutStudioContent and opens it via the browser.
Phone click: when a user clicks on the phone element in the contact section, the click handler builds a tel URL using the contact.phone value and opens it via the browser where supported.
Location click: when a user clicks on the location element in the contact section, the click handler generates a map-navigation URL based on contact.location and navigates the browser to that URL.

Service link click to Design Services: when a user clicks the design consultations link in the services navigation area, the click handler navigates to design_services.html, appending relevant context such as source=about and possibly section=<currentSection> to the query string.

Service link click to Design Packages: when a user clicks the design packages link in the services navigation area, the click handler navigates to design_packages.html with appropriate URL parameters (e.g., source=about).

Service link click to Custom Furniture: when a user clicks on the custom furniture link in the services navigation area or within a descriptive text, the click handler navigates to custom_furniture.html with context parameters (e.g., source=about).

Journal link click: when a user clicks the Journal link in the philosophy or services/journal navigation area, the click handler navigates to blog.html, including parameters such as source=about and context=philosophy where applicable.

URL change handling: when URL parameters change (for example via internal navigation without full page reload), the controller listens for those changes, recalculates the active section and active team member index, and instructs relevant components to re-render or refocus.
page_functionality.state_logic:
Page state is driven solely by URL query parameters and the data returned from getAboutStudioContent. No localStorage or other client-side persistence mechanisms are used.

URL parameters:
- section: optional; controls which logical section of the About page is considered active or focused. Supported values can include story, philosophy, team, contact, services, and journal. Behavior:
  - If section is omitted or empty, the page controller applies a default (e.g., story) and highlights that navigation item.
  - If section is set to story, the story section is scrolled into view after content has loaded, and the navigation highlights the story item.
  - If section is set to philosophy, the philosophy section is scrolled into view and highlighted in the navigation.
  - If section is set to team, the team section is scrolled into view and highlighted; the teamMemberIndex parameter (if present) is also considered.
  - If section is set to contact, the contact section is scrolled into view and highlighted.
  - If section is set to services or journal, the services/journal navigation area is scrolled into view and highlighted.

- teamMemberIndex: optional; used when section=team. Behavior:
  - Parsed as an integer index into the teamMembers array returned by getAboutStudioContent.
  - If the index is within the bounds of the teamMembers array, the team-member-detail component shows that member's details and the corresponding list item is highlighted.
  - If the index is invalid or absent, the team-member-detail component remains in a default state (no specific member expanded), and no list item is highlighted beyond default rules.

- source and context (for outgoing navigation): while About page itself does not require source or context parameters for its internal rendering, it can append them to outgoing links to design_services.html, design_packages.html, custom_furniture.html, and blog.html. These parameters are not interpreted by about.html but are included to support downstream behavior.

On every page load or URL change event:
1. The controller reads the current URLSearchParams.
2. The controller calls getAboutStudioContent and waits for the response.
3. Once data is available, the controller derives the activeSection from section or a default value.
4. For section=team, the controller derives activeTeamMemberIndex from teamMemberIndex and validates it against the length of teamMembers.
5. The controller passes the resolved activeSection and activeTeamMemberIndex down to child components as props or inputs.
6. If necessary, the controller performs scrolling or focus actions once both data and state (from URL parameters) are ready.
7. When internal navigation changes any parameter (e.g., user clicks a nav item), the controller updates the URL (e.g., via history.pushState) and re-runs the state derivation logic without re-calling getAboutStudioContent unnecessarily, unless a full reload occurs.

components.functionality: Coordinates page initialization, calls getAboutStudioContent, reads URL parameters, computes active section and active team member index, and passes data and state to all child components.

Provides navigation controls to jump between sections of the About page (story, philosophy, team, contact, services, journal) and updates the section parameter in the URL when a user selects a section.
Displays the studio story text loaded from getAboutStudioContent and responds to being the active section (e.g., by being scrolled into view when section=story).

Displays the design philosophy text from getAboutStudioContent and responds to the section parameter; when section=philosophy, this section is treated as active and may be automatically scrolled into view.
Renders an interactive list of team members using the teamMembers array. Each item is selectable and controls the active team member index in the URL when clicked.
Shows detailed information (name, role, bio, image) for the currently active team member determined by teamMemberIndex. If no valid index is set, remains in a neutral or collapsed state.
Displays contact information (email, phone, location) based on getAboutStudioContent.contact and provides interactive actions to initiate email, call, or open a map view.

Provides contextual navigation links from the About page to design_services.html, design_packages.html, and custom_furniture.html, enriching each navigation with URL parameters describing the source and current section.
Provides navigation from the About page to blog.html (Journal), optionally using the current section or philosophy context to set URL parameters for the target page.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a centered fixed-width layout on a 12-column grid (approx. 1100–1200px content width) with generous outer margins. Content within each major section appears to follow a simple 2-column grid (image vs text) for article listings.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a single, centered content column (approx. 1100–1200px wide) within the viewport, stacked in a linear top-to-bottom flow: section navigation at the top of the page content, then the studio story block, followed by a philosophy feature card, a slim meta row with navigation to services and journal, a team section with list and detail in a 2-column band, and finally a contact section at the bottom. Within key sections (story, philosophy, team, contact), apply the 2-column image/summary vs. copy pattern derived from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a single, centered content column (approx. 1100–1200px wide) within the viewport, stacked in a linear top-to-bottom flow: section navigation at the top of the page content, then the studio story block, followed by a philosophy feature card, a slim meta row with navigation to services and journal, a team section with list and detail in a 2-column band, and finally a contact section at the bottom. Within key sections (story, philosophy, team, contact), apply the 2-column image/summary vs. copy pattern derived from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Allocate the full browser width for the background and global header, but constrain the main content to a centered fixed-width column using a 12-column grid. Major sections (section nav, story, philosophy, team, contact) are separated vertically by roughly 32–48px plus occasional thin horizontal rules. Inside sections, internal padding is ~16–24px with 8–16px between textual elements. Image vs. text splits generally allocate about 35–40% of the content width to imagery or side content and 60–65% to text, matching the article card pattern.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a light, editorial density: only one major section is visually dominant at a time, with generous white space above and below each block. Text is set in short paragraphs with clear line spacing; lists and navigation groups are minimal and uncluttered. Visual focus is on key imagery (studio/project, portraits) and concise copy rather than large volumes of content.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye with a mild Z-pattern in each major band: scan left-to-right across the section navigation; drop down to the story section where a small visual anchor on the left leads into text on the right; continue down to the philosophy card where a strong image on the left pulls attention, then text on the right, then a meta row that leads from services links on the left to journal links on the right. The team band repeats the left (list) to right (detail) flow, and the contact section uses left contact details to right interaction area, preserving the same left-anchor to right-detail rhythm.

layout.overall_description: The About Studio page sits inside a calm, white background with a centered content column roughly 1100–1200px wide. Below the global site header, the page begins with a slim, full-width-in-column section navigation that feels like an editorial section label bar. Generous white space then leads into a studio story block that echoes an author bio: a compact studio mark or image sits on the left, while the right side presents the About Studio heading and narrative copy. A thin horizontal rule spans the content width to introduce the next band: a feature-style philosophy card where a wide, landscape studio or interior image occupies the left third of the column and a stacked title and paragraphs occupy the right two-thirds. Directly beneath this, a fine rule and a shallow meta row hold two opposing navigation groups—services links aligned left and journal links aligned right—forming a quiet directional footer to the philosophy content. Further down, another rule and generous spacing introduce the team section, which occupies a single horizontal band: a narrow left column lists team members like a clean roster, while a wider right column shows the selected member’s portrait and bio, mirroring the image/text balance seen earlier. Finally, the page closes with a contact section that returns to a simple 2-column structure: contact details and studio information on the left, a minimal contact/visit call-to-action on the right, all surrounded by ample breathing room and thin dividers that keep the entire layout feeling like a contemporary design magazine spread.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller defines the main content canvas as a centered, fixed-width column (approx. 1100–1200px wide) within the full browser width. It sits directly below the global site header, with about 48px of top margin before the first page-specific element. All other About Studio components are stacked vertically inside this controller and aligned to its left and right edges, ensuring consistent gutters and alignment for rules, imagery, and text. The vertical rhythm uses 32–48px gaps between major blocks and 16–24px inside each block, enforcing the spacious editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as the primary scrollable container for the About Studio page content; full-width background but constrains inner content to a centered fixed-width column. No sticky or floating behavior; all nested components scroll normally within it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section navigation sits at the very top of the About Studio content column, immediately inside the page controller and roughly 48px below the global header. It spans the full width of the content column but is visually slim: a single horizontal row of section labels (Story, Philosophy, Team, Contact) aligned left, with generous horizontal spacing between each label and a thin baseline rule running underneath the active item or the entire row. There is 16–24px of padding above and below the labels, and about 32px of white space separates this navigation from the story section below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Horizontally laid out within the main content column; becomes sticky to the top edge of the viewport once the user scrolls past its initial position, so it remains visible as a slim bar while other sections scroll beneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The story section appears directly below the section navigation, separated by approximately 32–40px of white space. It spans the full content width and is internally divided into two columns: a narrow left column occupying about 20–25% of the content width and a wider right column occupying about 75–80%. In the left column, a square or slightly vertical studio image, logo, or subtle graphic anchors the section, centered vertically relative to the text on the right. The right column starts with a small all-caps label (e.g., “About the Studio”) followed by a larger, editorial-style heading and 1–3 short paragraphs of copy, each separated by 8–12px. The section has 24px of internal padding at the top and bottom, and there is a 40–48px gap followed by a thin horizontal rule before the philosophy section begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section inside the main scroll; maintains a two-column layout at desktop widths and can collapse to stacked columns on smaller screens if needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The philosophy section sits beneath the story section, introduced by a full-width thin horizontal rule and about 32–40px of vertical spacing. It adopts the article card layout from the mockup: on the left, a large landscape image of a key interior or project occupies roughly 35–40% of the content width, extending nearly the full height of the card and aligned flush with the left edge of the content column. On the right, occupying about 60–65% of the width, a text column begins with a small label (e.g., “Design Philosophy”) above a bold, succinct heading and a compact block of explanatory copy. Spacing between label, heading, and paragraphs is about 8–16px, preserving a clean hierarchy. The overall card has 24px of vertical padding, and its bottom edge sits about 16px above the thin rule that introduces the services/journal meta row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level feature card in the main scroll; fixed in place relative to surrounding sections, with image and text aligned on a shared baseline grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The services navigation appears directly underneath the philosophy section as the left half of a shallow meta row. A thin horizontal rule runs across the full content width, with about 12–16px of vertical spacing between the bottom of the philosophy text and this rule. Immediately below the rule, the services link group is aligned to the left edge of the content column, occupying roughly 45–50% of the width. It may include a small label such as “Studio Services” followed by a vertical stack of 2–4 minimal links, each separated by 8px, all set in light-weight typography. The right side of this same horizontal band is visually reserved for the journal navigation, creating a balanced left-right composition.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Shares a common horizontal band with the journal navigation; behaves as a standard inline block anchored to the left, with no sticky or floating behavior. As the viewport narrows, it can stack above the journal navigation while preserving the rule above as a unifying element.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The journal navigation is positioned in the same horizontal band as the services navigation, aligned to the right edge of the content column and visually forming the right half of the meta row beneath the philosophy section. It occupies about 45–50% of the content width and is right-aligned, echoing the ‘Read More >’ position in the original mockup. A subtle label such as “From the Journal” sits above a short vertical list of 2–4 journal links or a single highlighted journal entry link, with 8–12px between items. The vertical alignment of the journal navigation matches that of the services navigation so that both groups read as a single unified row separated only by whitespace.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard inline block aligned to the right within the meta row; no sticky behavior. On narrower screens it can reflow to sit below the services navigation, retaining alignment with the content column edges.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The team list introduces the team section and is positioned below the services/journal meta row, separated by approximately 40–48px of white space and, optionally, another thin horizontal rule to mark the new band. Within this band, the team area is divided into two columns: the team list forms the left column, occupying about 35–40% of the content width. At the top of this column, a small label (e.g., “Team”) and a simple section title sit with 16px of spacing, followed by a vertical list of team members. Each member row uses a compact layout with the name and role, possibly with a small circular avatar aligned to the far left, and each row is separated by 8–12px and subtle spacing rather than heavy borders. The list’s total height roughly matches or slightly exceeds the height of the team detail panel on the right, creating a balanced, editorial roster.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored as the left column of a two-column team band. Scrolls normally; on narrower screens, it can reflow to appear above the team member detail panel in a stacked arrangement.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The team member detail panel sits to the right of the team list within the same horizontal band, occupying approximately 60–65% of the content width and aligned flush with the right edge of the content column. It starts at the same vertical height as the first member in the list, reinforcing the shared band structure. Inside the panel, a larger portrait or representative image sits at the top (left-aligned within this column) with 16–24px of spacing beneath it. Below the image, the member’s name appears as a bold heading, followed by the role in a lighter style, and then a few short paragraphs or bullet lines of bio text, each spaced by 8–12px. The entire panel is surrounded by 24px of internal padding and may optionally be bounded by a subtle outline or left-edge rule to differentiate it gently from the background while maintaining the minimalist aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Shares horizontal space with the team list as the right column in the team band; scrolls with the page. When a different team member is selected from the list, this panel’s content updates in place without shifting its spatial position. On small screens, it reflows to sit directly below the team list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact section is placed near the bottom of the page, following the team band with approximately 48px of vertical spacing and a thin horizontal rule to clearly separate it as the final main section. It spans the full width of the content column and is divided into two columns similar to the earlier patterns: the left column (about 35–40% width) contains studio address, contact details, and possibly opening hours, set in a vertically stacked format with 8–12px between lines and 16–24px between grouped blocks. The right column (about 60–65% width) hosts a minimal contact form or a clear call-to-action block (e.g., “Schedule a Consultation”), with fields or actions stacked vertically and surrounded by generous white space. The entire section has 24–32px of vertical padding at top and bottom, and additional 32–40px of whitespace separates it from any footer content below, preserving a calm, editorial ending to the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section at the end of the main scroll; remains in place as users scroll to the bottom. Columns may stack vertically on narrower screens while maintaining the same internal spacing rules.
